syllabus.title: Functions and graphs
syllabus.content: Represent, interpret, and analyse functions, including domain, range, transformations, intersections, and graphical features.

syllabus.title: Graphing techniques
syllabus.content: Use transformations and graphing techniques to analyse, sketch, and interpret functions.

syllabus.title: Logarithms and exponentials
syllabus.content: Use logarithmic and exponential functions, equations, and transformations in algebraic and applied contexts.

syllabus.title: Differential calculus
syllabus.content: Use derivatives to determine rates of change, gradients, stationary points, tangents, normals, and optimisation results.

syllabus.title: Applications of differentiation
syllabus.content: Apply differentiation to rates of change, curve features, optimisation, and contextual modelling problems.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Use definite and indefinite integrals, areas under curves, and accumulation ideas to solve mathematical and applied problems.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Use integration methods, definite integrals, areas, and accumulation to solve mathematical and applied problems.

syllabus.title: Modelling financial situations
syllabus.content: Use sequences, series, interest factors, annuities, loans, and recurrence relationships to model financial situations.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions
syllabus.content: Work with trigonometric functions, identities, exact values, equations, graphs, and practical angle relationships.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions and identities
syllabus.content: Use trigonometric identities and algebraic manipulation to prove identities and solve trigonometric problems.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric functions and graphs
syllabus.content: Analyse trigonometric functions, graphs, equations, periodic behaviour, and transformations.

syllabus.title: Probability and discrete probability distributions
syllabus.content: Use probability rules, conditional probability, discrete distributions, and contextual probability models.

syllabus.title: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
syllabus.content: Use scatterplots, association, least-squares regression lines, interpolation, extrapolation, and contextual interpretation to analyse bivariate datasets.

syllabus.title: Random variables
syllabus.content: Use discrete and continuous random variables, probability density functions, normal models, percentiles, and expected-value reasoning.

syllabus.title: Working with functions
syllabus.content: Working with functions: Algebraic techniques; Introduction to functions and relations; Linear functions; Quadratic and cubic functions; Reciprocal functions; Constructing and using functions; Direct and inverse variation; Circles and semicircles; Properties of functions, relations and graphs; Piecewise-defined functions; Absolute value functions.

syllabus.title: Trigonometry and measure of angles
syllabus.content: Trigonometry and measure of angles: Trigonometry with acute angles; Trigonometry with angles of any magnitude; Radians.

syllabus.title: Trigonometric identities and equations
syllabus.content: Trigonometric identities and equations: Reciprocal trigonometric functions, identities, expressions and equations.

syllabus.title: Introduction to differentiation
syllabus.content: Introduction to differentiation: Estimating change; The derivative; Calculations with the derivative; Graphical applications of the derivative; The derivative as a rate of change.

syllabus.title: Exponential and logarithmic functions
syllabus.content: Exponential and logarithmic functions: Exponential functions; Logarithmic functions.

syllabus.title: Graph transformations
syllabus.content: Graph transformations: Function transformations, graphing circles, and order of transformations.

syllabus.title: Probability and data
syllabus.content: Probability and data: Sets and set notation; Probability; Conditional probability; Data.

syllabus.title: Further graph transformations and modelling
syllabus.content: Further graph transformations and modelling: Transformations of trigonometric functions; Modelling with functions.

syllabus.title: Sequences and series
syllabus.content: Sequences and series: Sequences and series; Arithmetic sequences and series; Geometric sequences and series.

syllabus.title: Differential calculus
syllabus.content: Differential calculus: Differentiation with exponential functions; Differentiation with logarithmic functions; Differentiation with trigonometric functions; Using derivatives.

syllabus.title: Integral calculus
syllabus.content: Integral calculus: Primitive functions; The definite integral; The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus; Indefinite integrals; Integration with exponential functions; Integration with logarithmic functions; Integration with trigonometric functions; Areas and the definite integral.

syllabus.title: Applications of calculus

questions.title: Q1 Which inequality gives the domain of y = 2x - 3? A. x < 32 B. x > 32 C. x 32 D. x 32
questions.promptLatex:
Which inequality gives the domain of \(y = \sqrt{2x - 3}\)?
A. \(x < \frac{3}{2}\)
B. \(x > \frac{3}{2}\)
C. \(x \le \frac{3}{2}\)
D. \(x \ge \frac{3}{2}\)

questions.title: Q2 The function f(x) = x^3 is transformed to g(x) = (x - 2)^3 + 5 by a horizontal transla
questions.promptLatex:
The function \(f(x) = x^3\) is transformed to \(g(x) = (x - 2)^3 + 5\) by a horizontal translation of 2 units followed by a vertical translation of 5 units. Which row of the table shows the directions of the translations?
A. Left, Up
B. Right, Up
C. Left, Down
D. Right, Down

assets.label: Question 2 table
assets.alt: Table showing directions of horizontal and vertical translations with options A, B, C, D

questions.title: Q3 John recently did a class test in each of three subjects. The class scores on each tes
questions.promptLatex:
John recently did a class test in each of three subjects. The class scores on each test were normally distributed. The table shows the subjects and John's scores as well as the mean and standard deviation of the class scores on each test.

\[\begin{tabular}{|l|c|c|c|} \hline Subject & John's score & Mean & Standard deviation \\ \hline French & 82 & 70 & 8 \\ \hline Commerce & 80 & 65 & 5 \\ \hline Music & 74 & 50 & 12 \\ \hline \end{tabular}\]

Relative to the rest of the class, which row of the table below shows John's strongest subject and his weakest subject?
A. Commerce, French
B. French, Music
C. Music, French
D. Commerce, Music

assets.label: Question 3 table
assets.alt: Table showing Subject, John's score, Mean, and Standard deviation for French, Commerce, and Music

assets.label: Question 3 table
assets.alt: Multiple choice table with options A-D showing Strongest subject and Weakest subject combinations

questions.title: Q4 What is e + e^3x \, dx? A. ex + 3e^3x + c B. ex + 13e^3x + c C. e + 3e^3x + c D. e + 1
Q5 Which of the following could represent the graph of y = -x^2 + bx + 1, where b > 0? A.
questions.promptLatex:
Which of the following could represent the graph of \(y = -x^2 + bx + 1\), where \(b > 0\)?
A. Graph A
B. Graph B
C. Graph C
D. Graph D

assets.label: Question 5 graph
assets.alt: Four graphs (A, B, C, D) showing possible representations of y = -x² + bx + 1 where b > 0, each with labeled axes and y-intercept of 1.

questions.title: Q6 Which interval gives the range of the function y = 5 + 2 3x? A. [2, 8] B. [3, 7] C. [4
questions.promptLatex:
Which interval gives the range of the function \(y = 5 + 2\cos 3x\)?
A. \([2, 8]\)
B. \([3, 7]\)
C. \([4, 6]\)
D. \([5, 9]\)

questions.title: Q7 The diagram shows the graph y = f(x), which is made up of line segments and a semicirc
questions.promptLatex:
The diagram shows the graph \(y = f(x)\), which is made up of line segments and a semicircle. What is the value of \(\int_{0}^{12} f(x)dx\)?
A. \(24 + 2\pi\)
B. \(24 + 4\pi\)
C. \(30 + 2\pi\)
D. \(30 + 4\pi\)

assets.label: Question 7 graph
assets.alt: Graph of y = f(x) made up of line segments and a semicircle, with axes labeled, tick marks at x = 4, 8, 10, 12 and y = 3, -3

questions.title: Q8 The graph of y = f(x) is shown. Which of the following inequalities is correct? A. f''
questions.promptLatex:
The graph of \(y = f(x)\) is shown. Which of the following inequalities is correct?
A. \(f''(1) < 0 < f'(1) < f(1)\)
B. \(f''(1) < 0 < f(1) < f'(1)\)
C. \(0 < f''(1) < f'(1) < f(1)\)
D. \(0 < f''(1) < f(1) < f'(1)\)

assets.label: Question 8 graph
assets.alt: Graph of y = f(x) showing a curve starting from origin, increasing with decreasing gradient (concave down), with axes labeled x and y, origin O marked, and point at x=1 indicated.

questions.title: Q9 Suppose the weight of melons is normally distributed with a mean of and a standard dev
questions.promptLatex:
Suppose the weight of melons is normally distributed with a mean of \(\mu\) and a standard deviation of \(\sigma\). A melon has a weight below the lower quartile of the distribution but NOT in the bottom 10% of the distribution. Which of the following most accurately represents the region in which the weight of this melon lies?
A. Graph A
B. Graph B
C. Graph C
D. Graph D

assets.label: Question 9 graph
assets.alt: Four normal distribution curves (A, B, C, D) each with shaded regions indicating different areas, labeled with axis points μ−2σ, μ−σ, μ, μ+σ, μ+2σ.

questions.title: Q10 The graph shows two functions y = f(x) and y = g(x). Define h(x) = f(g(x)). How many s
questions.promptLatex:
The graph shows two functions \(y = f(x)\) and \(y = g(x)\).

Define \(h(x) = f(g(x))\).

How many stationary points does \(y = h(x)\) have for \(1 \le x \le 5\)?
A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. 3

assets.label: Question 10 graph
assets.alt: Graph showing two functions y = f(x) and y = g(x) plotted on the same axes, with x-axis from -1 to 6 and y-axis up to 5+

questions.title: Q11 (a) There are two tanks on a property, Tank A and Tank B. Initially, Tank A holds 1000
questions.promptLatex:
(a) There are two tanks on a property, Tank \(A\) and Tank \(B\). Initially, Tank \(A\) holds \(1000\) litres of water and Tank \(B\) is empty. Tank \(A\) begins to lose water at a constant rate of \(20\) litres per minute. The volume of water in Tank \(A\) is modelled by \(V = 1000 - 20t\) where \(V\) is the volume in litres and \(t\) is the time in minutes from when the tank begins to lose water. On the grid below, draw the graph of this model and label it as Tank \(A\).

(b) Tank \(B\) remains empty until \(t = 15\) when water is added to it at a constant rate of \(30\) litres per minute. By drawing a line on the grid on the previous page, or otherwise, find the value of \(t\) when the two tanks contain the same volume of water.

(c) Using the graphs drawn, or otherwise, find the value of \(t\) (where \(t > 0\)) when the total volume of water in the two tanks is \(1000\) litres.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) The graph shows the intersection point at \(t = 29\) minutes.
(b) 29 minutes
(c) 45 minutes
workingLatex: (a) Marking criteria: Provides the correct solution
(b) 29 minutes

questions.title: Q16 Sketch the graph of the curve y = -x^3 + 3x^2 - 1, labelling the stationary points and
questions.promptLatex: Sketch the graph of the curve \(y = -x^3 + 3x^2 - 1\), labelling the stationary points and point of inflection. Do NOT determine the \(x\) intercepts of the curve.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
Stationary points are \((0, -1)\) and \((2, 3)\). The point of inflection is \((1, 1)\).
workingLatex:
\[ y = -x^3 + 3x^2 - 1 \]
\[ \frac{dy}{dx} = -3x^2 + 6x = 3x(2 - x) \]
When \(\frac{dy}{dx} = 0\), we have \(3x(2 - x) = 0\).
\(x = 0, x = 2\)
\(y = -1, y = 3\)
Stationary points are \((0, -1)\) and \((2, 3)\).
\[ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -6x + 6 \]
When \(\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 0\), we have \(6(1 - x) = 0\).
\(x = 1\)
\(y = 1\)
The point of inflection is \((1, 1)\).

Marking criteria: Provides the correct solution; Finds the stationary points and the point of inflection, or equivalent merit; Finds the stationary points, or equivalent merit; Finds the derivative of the function, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q19 Prove that - = .
questions.promptLatex: Prove that \(\sec \theta - \cos \theta = \sin \theta \tan \theta\).
workingLatex:
\[\sec\theta - \cos\theta = \sin\theta\tan\theta\]
\[LHS = \frac{1}{\cos\theta} - \cos\theta\]
\[= \frac{1-\cos^2\theta}{\cos\theta}\]
\[= \frac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta}\]
\[= \sin\theta \times \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\]
\[= \sin\theta \times \tan\theta\]
\[= RHS\]

questions.title: Q25 (a) A landscape gardener wants to build a garden bed in the shape of a rectangle attac
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A landscape gardener wants to build a garden bed in the shape of a rectangle attached to a quarter-circle. Let \(x\) and \(y\) be the dimensions of the rectangle in metres, as shown in the diagram. The garden bed is required to have an area of \(36\text{ m}^2\) and to have a perimeter which is as small as possible. Let \(P\) metres be the perimeter of the garden bed. Show that \(P = 2x + \frac{72}{x}\).

(b) Find the smallest possible perimeter of the garden bed, showing why this is the minimum perimeter.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) \[ P = 2x + \frac{72}{x} \]
(b) The minimum perimeter is 24 m.
workingLatex: (a) Marking criteria: Provides the correct solution; Writes an expression for the perimeter in terms of \(x\) and \(y\) and finds a correct expression for \(y\) in terms of \(x\), or equivalent merit; Writes an expression for a perimeter in terms of \(x\) and \(y\) which includes the curved length of a quadrant, or equivalent merit

(b) \[ \frac{dP}{dx} = 2 - \frac{72}{x^2} \]
Setting \(\frac{dP}{dx} = 0\), we have
\[ 2 = \frac{72}{x^2} \]
\[ x^2 = 36 \]
\[ x = 6 \quad (since  x > 0) \]
\[ \frac{d^2P}{dx^2} = \frac{144}{x^3} \]
When \(x = 6\),
\[ \frac{d^2P}{dx^2} = \frac{144}{6^3}  which is greater than zero. \]
\(\therefore P\) is a minimum when \(x = 6\).
The minimum perimeter \(= 2 \times 6 + \frac{72}{6} = 12 + 12 = 24  m\)

(b) Marking criteria: Provides the correct solution; Verifies that at \(x = 6\) there is a minimum turning point, or equivalent merit; Solves \(\frac{dP}{dx} = 0\), or equivalent merit; Finds \(\frac{dP}{dx}\), or equivalent merit

\[= -642.7\dots\]
Therefore the mice population is decreasing at 643 mice per week.

(b(i)) 60

(d) \[ \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos 5x \sin 3x \, dx = \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{2}(\sin 8x - \sin 2x) \, dx = \left[ \frac{1}{2} \left( -\frac{\cos 8x}{8} + \frac{\cos 2x}{2} \right) \right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} = \frac{1}{2} \left( -\frac{1}{8} - \frac{1}{2} \right) - \frac{1}{2} \left( -\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{2} \right) = -\frac{1}{2} \]
(e) \(x^2 + y^2 = 1\)

For \(n = 1\) \(LHS = 1(2) = 2\), \(RHS = 1(2) = 2\)
\(\therefore\) Statement is true for \(n = 1\)
Assume statement true for \(n = k\), that is,
\[(1 \times 2) + (2 \times 5) + (3 \times 8) + \dots + k(3k - 1) = k^2(k + 1)\]
Prove true for \(n = k + 1\)
That is, we show that
\[(1 \times 2) + (2 \times 5) + \dots + k(3k - 1) + (k + 1)(3(k + 1) - 1) = (k + 1)^2(k + 2)\]
\(LHS = k^2(k + 1) + (k + 1)(3k + 2)\)
\(= (k + 1)(k^2 + 3k + 2)\)
\(= (k + 1)(k + 1)(k + 2)\)
\(= (k + 1)^2(k + 2)\)
\(= RHS\)
\(\therefore\) By the principle of mathematical induction the statement is true for \(n \geq 1\).

questions.title: Q14 (ai) Use the identity (1+x)^2n = (1+x)^n (1+x)^n to show that  inom2nn =  inomn0^2 +
questions.promptLatex:
(ai) Use the identity \((1+x)^{2n} = (1+x)^n (1+x)^n\) to show that \[ inom{2n}{n} =  inom{n}{0}^2 +  inom{n}{1}^2 + \dots +  inom{n}{n}^2\] where \(n\) is a positive integer.

(aii) A club has \(2n\) members, with \(n\) women and \(n\) men. A group consisting of an even number \((0, 2, 4, \dots, 2n)\) of members is chosen, with the number of men equal to the number of women. Show, giving reasons, that the number of ways to do this is \( inom{2n}{n}\).

(aiii) From the group chosen in part (ii), one of the men and one of the women are selected as leaders. Show, giving reasons, that the number of ways to choose the even number of people and then the leaders is \[1^2  inom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2  inom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2  inom{n}{n}^2.\]

(aiv) The process is now reversed so that the leaders, one man and one woman, are chosen first. The rest of the group is then selected, still made up of an equal number of women and men. By considering this reversed process and using part (ii), find a simple expression for the sum in part (iii).

(bi) Show that \(\sin^3 \theta - \frac{3}{4}\sin \theta + \frac{\sin(3\theta)}{4} = 0\).

(bii) By letting \(x = 4\sin \theta\) in the cubic equation \(x^3 - 12x + 8 = 0\). Show that \(\sin(3\theta) = \frac{1}{2}\).

(biii) Prove that \(\sin^2 \frac{\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{5\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{25\pi}{18} = \frac{3}{2}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) The left hand side is \(\binom{2n}{n}\) which is the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^{2n}\). This means that \(\binom{n}{0}\binom{n}{0} + \binom{n}{1}\binom{n}{1} + \dots + \binom{n}{n}\binom{n}{n}\) should be the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^n(1+x)^n\).
(a(i)) The coefficient of \(x^n\) in expansion of \((1+x)^{2n}\) is \( inom{2n}{n}\). The coefficient of \(x^n\) in \((1+x)^n(1+x)^n\) is \( inom{n}{0} inom{n}{n} +  inom{n}{1} inom{n}{n-1} + \dots +  inom{n}{k} inom{n}{n-k} + \dots +  inom{n}{n} inom{n}{0}\). But \( inom{n}{n-k} =  inom{n}{k}\). So the coefficient is \( inom{n}{0} inom{n}{0} +  inom{n}{1} inom{n}{1} + \dots +  inom{n}{n} inom{n}{n}\). Hence \( inom{2n}{n} =  inom{n}{0}^2 +  inom{n}{1}^2 + \dots +  inom{n}{n}^2\).
(a(ii)) We can choose: 0 men and 0 women in \( inom{n}{0} inom{n}{0} =  inom{n}{0}^2\) ways, or 1 man and 1 woman in \( inom{n}{1}^2\) ways, ..., or \(n\) men and \(n\) women in \( inom{n}{n}^2\) ways. Hence the total number of ways is \( inom{n}{0}^2 + \dots +  inom{n}{n}^2 =  inom{2n}{n}\) from part (i).
(a(iii)) The total is \(1^2 \binom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 \binom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 \binom{n}{n}^2\)
(a(iv)) \[ 1^2 inom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 inom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 inom{n}{n}^2 = n^2 inom{2n-2}{n-1} \]
(b(i)) \[ \sin^3\theta - \frac{3}{4}\sin\theta + \frac{\sin(3\theta)}{4} = 0 \]
(b(ii)) \[\sin 3\theta = \frac{1}{2}\]
(b(iii)) \(\sin^2 \frac{\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{5\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{25\pi}{18} = \frac{3}{2}\)
workingLatex: (a(i)) The left hand side is \(\binom{2n}{n}\) which is the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^{2n}\). This means that \(\binom{n}{0}\binom{n}{0} + \binom{n}{1}\binom{n}{1} + \dots + \binom{n}{n}\binom{n}{n}\) should be the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion of \((1+x)^n(1+x)^n\). The first term, \(\binom{n}{0}\) is the constant term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) and so should be multiplied by an \(x^n\) term. But \(\binom{n}{0}=\binom{n}{n-0}=\binom{n}{n}\) is equal to the coefficient of \(x^n\) in the expansion \((1+x)^n\). Thus \(\binom{n}{0}^2\) is the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term that comes from the constant in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) times the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\). Similarly \(\binom{n}{1}^2\) is the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term that comes from the coefficient of \(x\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\) times the coefficient of the \(x^{n-1}\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n\). Therefore, the right hand side is the coefficient of the \(x^n\) term in the expansion of \((1+x)^n(1+x)^n\).
(a(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Identifies coefficient of \(x^n\) on the left hand side, or equivalent merit
(a(i)) Marking criteria: This page provides the continuation of the sample solution for Question 14 (a) (i).

(a(ii)) We can choose: 0 men and 0 women in \( inom{n}{0} inom{n}{0} =  inom{n}{0}^2\) ways, or 1 man and 1 woman in \( inom{n}{1}^2\) ways, ..., or \(n\) men and \(n\) women in \( inom{n}{n}^2\) ways. Hence the total number of ways is \( inom{n}{0}^2 + \dots +  inom{n}{n}^2 =  inom{2n}{n}\) from part (i).
(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Explains why one of the terms is \( inom{n}{k} \times  inom{n}{k}\), or equivalent merit

(a(iii)) We can choose 1 woman and 1 leader in \(\binom{n}{1} \times 1\) ways and similarly for the men. This gives \(\binom{n}{1} \times 1 \times \binom{n}{1} \times 1 = \binom{n}{1}^2 \times 1^2\) for this case. We can choose 2 women and 1 leader in \(\binom{n}{2} \binom{2}{1} = \binom{n}{2} \times 2\) ways and similarly for the men. This gives \(\binom{n}{2} \times 2 \times \binom{n}{2} \times 2 = \binom{n}{2}^2 \times 2^2\) for this case. We can choose \(n\) women and 1 leader in \(\binom{n}{n} \binom{n}{1} = \binom{n}{n} \times n\) ways and similarly for the men. This gives \(\binom{n}{n} \times n \times \binom{n}{n} \times n = \binom{n}{n}^2 \times n^2\) for this case. And so the total is \(1^2 \binom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 \binom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 \binom{n}{n}^2\).
(a(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Explains why one of the terms is \(k^2 \binom{n}{k}^2\), or equivalent merit

(a(iv)) There are \(n\) ways to choose a woman leader, leaving \(n-1\) women from which to choose. There are \(n\) ways to choose a man leader, leaving \(n-1\) men from which to choose. By part (ii) there are \( inom{2(n-1)}{n-1}\) ways to choose the \(n-1\) women and \(n-1\) men. Hence \(1^2 inom{n}{1}^2 + 2^2 inom{n}{2}^2 + \dots + n^2 inom{n}{n}^2 = n^2 inom{2n-2}{n-1}\).
(a(iv)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Recognises that choosing the leaders first reduces the problem to using part (ii) with \(n-1\) women and \(n-1\) men, or equivalent merit

(b(i)) \(\sin 3\theta = \sin(2\theta + \theta) = \sin 2\theta \cos \theta + \cos 2\theta \sin \theta = 2\sin \theta \cos^2 \theta + (1 - 2\sin^2 \theta)\sin \theta = 2\sin \theta(1 - \sin^2 \theta) + \sin \theta - 2\sin^3 \theta = 3\sin \theta - 4\sin^3 \theta\). Therefore, \(4\sin^3 \theta - 3\sin \theta + \sin(3\theta) = 0\), so \(\sin^3 \theta - \frac{3}{4}\sin \theta + \frac{\sin(3\theta)}{4} = 0\).
(b(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Expands \(\sin(2\theta + \theta)\), or equivalent merit

(b(ii)) \[x^3 - 12x + 8 = 0\]
\[x = 4\sin\theta,  so\]
\[64\sin^3\theta - 48\sin\theta + 8 = 0\]
\[\therefore \sin^3\theta - \frac{3}{4}\sin\theta + \frac{8}{64} = 0\]
\[comparing with (i),\]
\[\frac{8}{64} = \frac{\sin 3\theta}{4} \implies \sin 3\theta = \frac{32}{64} = \frac{1}{2}\]
(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains \(\sin^3 \theta - \frac{3}{4}\sin \theta + \frac{8}{64} = 0\), or equivalent merit

(b(iii)) From \(\sin 3\theta = \frac{1}{2}\) we have \(3\theta = \frac{\pi}{6}, \frac{5\pi}{6}, \frac{\pi}{6} + 2\pi, \frac{5\pi}{6} + 2\pi, \dots\) \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{18}, \frac{5\pi}{18}, \frac{13\pi}{18}, \frac{17\pi}{18}, \frac{25\pi}{18}, \dots\) So \(4\sin \frac{\pi}{18}, 4\sin \frac{5\pi}{18}, 4\sin \frac{13\pi}{18}\) etc are roots of the cubic, but they are not all distinct. The angles \(\frac{\pi}{18}\) and \(\frac{5\pi}{18}\) are distinct angles in the first quadrant and so have different positive sine values. The angle \(\frac{25\pi}{18}\) is in the third quadrant and so has a negative sine value. Hence we can take \(\alpha = 4\sin \frac{\pi}{18}, \beta = 4\sin \frac{5\pi}{18}, \gamma = 4\sin \frac{25\pi}{18}\) as 3 distinct roots. Now \(\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 = (\alpha + \beta + \gamma)^2 - 2(\alpha\beta + \alpha\gamma + \beta\gamma) = 0^2 + 2 \times 12 = 24\). \(\therefore 16(\sin^2 \frac{\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{5\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{25\pi}{18}) = 24\). \(\therefore \sin^2 \frac{\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{5\pi}{18} + \sin^2 \frac{25\pi}{18} = \frac{24}{16} = \frac{3}{2}\).

(b(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Obtains all the roots of the cubic, or equivalent merit; Obtains \(4\sin \frac{\pi}{18}\) as a solution of the cubic in part (b) (ii), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q13 (a) A particle is undergoing simple harmonic motion with period 3. The central point o
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A particle is undergoing simple harmonic motion with period \(\frac{\pi}{3}\). The central point of motion of the particle is at \(x = \sqrt{3}\). When \(t = 0\) the particle has its maximum displacement of \(2\sqrt{3}\) from the central point of motion. Find an equation for the displacement, \(x\), of the particle in terms of \(t\).

(b) Consider the two lines in three dimensions given by \[ \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_1 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \text{ and } \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_2 \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}. \] By equating components, find the point of intersection of the two lines.

(ci) (i) By considering the right-angled triangle below, or otherwise, prove that \(\frac{a + b}{2} \geq \sqrt{ab}\), where \(a > b \geq 0\).

(cii) (ii) Prove that \(p^2 + 4q^2 \geq 4pq\).

(di) (i) Show that for any integer \(n\), \(e^{in\theta} + e^{-in\theta} = 2\cos(n\theta)\).

(dii) (ii) By expanding \((e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta})^4\), show that \[ \cos^4 \theta = \frac{1}{8} (\cos(4\theta) + 4\cos(2\theta) + 3). \]

(diii) (iii) Hence, or otherwise, find \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cos^4 \theta \, d\theta\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) \(x(t) = 2\sqrt{3} \cos(6t) + \sqrt{3}\)
(b) The point of intersection is \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -5 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix} \)
(c(i)) By Pythagoras, \((a+b)^2 = x^2 + (a-b)^2\), \(\therefore x = 2\sqrt{ab}\). Now the hypotenuse is the longest side so \(a+b \geq 2\sqrt{ab}\), \(\therefore \frac{a+b}{2} \geq \sqrt{ab}\).
(c(ii)) Put \(a = p^2, b = (2q)^2\). Then \(\frac{p^2 + 4q^2}{2} \geq \sqrt{4p^2q^2}\). So \(p^2 + 4q^2 \geq 4pq\).
(d(i)) \(e^{in\theta} + e^{-in\theta} = (\cos n\theta + i\sin n\theta) + (\cos n\theta - i\sin n\theta) = 2\cos(n\theta)\)
(d(ii)) \(\cos^4 \theta = \frac{1}{8}(\cos 4\theta + 4\cos 2\theta + 3)\)
(d(iii)) \[\frac{3\pi}{16}\]

(a) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Correctly finds any two of amplitude, n, function with consistent phase; Correctly finds one of amplitude, n, function with consistent phase

(b) \( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -1 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_1 \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \)
\( \underset{\sim}{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda_2 \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} \)
Equating components,
\( 3 + \lambda_1 = 3 - 2\lambda_2 \quad (1) \)
\( -1 + 2\lambda_1 = -6 + \lambda_2 \quad (2) \)

\( (2) \Rightarrow -1 - 4\lambda_2 = -6 + \lambda_2 \Rightarrow \lambda_2 = 1 \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = -2 \).

Left hand side = 5, Right hand side = 5

questions.title: Q14 (aiii) Let z_1 be a complex number and let z_2 = e^i3 z_1. The diagram shows points A
questions.promptLatex:
(aiii) Let \(z_1\) be a complex number and let \(z_2 = e^{\frac{i\pi}{3}} z_1\). The diagram shows points \(A\) and \(B\) which represent \(z_1\) and \(z_2\), respectively, in the Argand plane.

(i) Explain why triangle \(OAB\) is an equilateral triangle.
(ii) Prove that \(z_1^2 + z_2^2 = z_1 z_2\).

(b) A particle starts from rest and falls through a resisting medium so that its acceleration, in \(\text{m/s}^2\), is modelled by \[a = 10(1 - (kv)^2),\] where \(v\) is the velocity of the particle in \(\text{m/s}\) and \(k = 0.01\). Find the velocity of the particle after 5 seconds.

(c) Prove by mathematical induction that, for \(n \ge 2\), \[\frac{1}{2^2} + \frac{1}{3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{n^2} < \frac{n - 1}{n}.\]

(d) Prove that for any integer \(n > 1\), \(\log_n(n + 1)\) is irrational.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) Multiplying \(z_1\) by \(e^{\frac{i\pi}{3}}\) rotates it anticlockwise through an angle \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), giving \(z_2\). Also \(|z_1| = |z_2|\). So \(	riangle OAB\) is isosceles giving all angles equal to \(\frac{\pi}{3}\) and hence \(	riangle OAB\) is equilateral.
(a(ii)) \[ z_1^2 + z_2^2 = z_1z_2 \]
(b) \[ v = 100\left(\frac{e-1}{e+1}\right) \approx 46.2  m/s \]
(c) Let \(P(n)\) be the given proposition. \(P(2)\) is true since \(\frac{1}{2^2} < \frac{1}{2}\). Let \(k\) be an integer for which \(P(k)\) is true. Thus we assume that \(\frac{1}{2^2} + \frac{1}{3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{k^2} < \frac{k-1}{k}\). Consider \(\frac{1}{2^2} + \frac{1}{3^2} + \dots + \frac{1}{k^2} + \frac{1}{(k+1)^2} < \frac{1}{(k+1)^2} + \frac{k-1}{k} = \frac{k+(k+1)^2(k-1)}{(k+1)^2 k} < \frac{(k+1)+(k+1)^2(k-1)}{(k+1)^2 k} = \frac{1+k^2-1}{(k+1)k} = \frac{k}{k+1}\). \(\therefore P(k+1)\) is true. \(\therefore P(n)\) true for all integers \(n \ge 2\) by induction.
(d) Suppose \(\log_n(n+1)\) is rational. Then \(\log_n(n+1) = \frac{p}{q}\), where \(p, q\) are integers and \(q \neq 0\). \(\therefore n+1 = n^{\frac{p}{q}}\), so \((n+1)^q = n^p\). If \(n\) is even then RHS is even, LHS is odd - a contradiction. If \(n\) is odd then RHS is odd, LHS is even - a contradiction. Hence in either case we have a contradiction, so \(\log_n(n+1)\) is irrational.

questions.title: Q15 (a) In the set of integers, let P be the proposition: 'If k + 1 is divisible by 3, the
questions.promptLatex:
(a) In the set of integers, let \(P\) be the proposition:

'If \(k + 1\) is divisible by \(3\), then \(k^3 + 1\) is divisible by \(3\).'

(i) Prove that the proposition \(P\) is true.
(ii) Write down the contrapositive of the proposition \(P\).
(iii) Write down the converse of the proposition \(P\) and state, with reasons, whether this converse is true or false.

(b(i)b(ii)b(iii)b(iv)) The point \(C\) divides the interval \(AB\) so that \(\frac{CB}{AC} = \frac{m}{n}\). The position vectors of \(A\) and \(B\) are \(\underset{\sim}{a}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{b}\) respectively, as shown in the diagram.

(i) Show that \(\vec{AC} = \frac{n}{m+n}(\underset{\sim}{b} - \underset{\sim}{a})\).

(ii) Prove that \(\vec{OC} = \frac{m}{m+n}\underset{\sim}{a} + \frac{n}{m+n}\underset{\sim}{b}\).

Let \(OPQR\) be a parallelogram with \(\vec{OP} = \underset{\sim}{p}\) and \(\vec{OR} = \underset{\sim}{r}\). The point \(S\) is the midpoint of \(QR\) and \(T\) is the intersection of \(PR\) and \(OS\), as shown in the diagram.

(iii) Show that \(\vec{OT} = \frac{2}{3}\underset{\sim}{r} + \frac{1}{3}\underset{\sim}{p}\).

(iv) Using parts (ii) and (iii), or otherwise, prove that \(T\) is the point that divides the interval \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) Suppose \(k+1\) is divisible by 3, then \(k+1=3j\), for some integer \(j\). Now \(k^3+1=(k+1)(k^2-k+1)=3j(k^2-k+1)\). Hence \(k^3+1\) is divisible by 3.
(a(ii)) If \(k^3+1\) is not divisible by 3, then \((k+1)\) is not divisible by 3.
(a(iii)) The converse states that: 'If \(k^3 + 1\) is divisible by 3, then \((k + 1)\) is divisible by 3'. This statement is true. The integer \(k\) must be of the form \(3j\), \(3j + 1\) or \(3j - 1\), for some integer \(j\). Then \(k^3 + 1 = 27j^3 + 1\) or \((3j + 1)^3 + 1\) or \((3j - 1)^3 + 1\). Only the third case gives \(k^3 + 1\) divisible by 3. OR Using proof by contradiction, suppose \(k^3 + 1\) is divisible by 3. But \((k + 1)\) is not divisible by 3. \(\therefore k^2 - k + 1\) is divisible by 3. \(\therefore (k + 1)^2 - 3k\) is divisible by 3. \(\therefore (k + 1)^2\) and hence \((k + 1)\) is divisible by 3. This is a contradiction.
(b(i)) \(\vec{AC} = \frac{n}{m+n}(\vec{b}-\vec{a})\)
(b(ii)) \(\vec{OC} = \frac{m}{m+n}\vec{a} + \frac{n}{m+n}\vec{b}\)
(b(iii)) \(\vec{OT} = \frac{2}{3}\underset{\sim}{r} + \frac{1}{3}\underset{\sim}{p}\)
(b(iv)) \(T\) divides the interval \(PR\) in the ratio \(2:1\).

Only the third case gives \(k^3 + 1\) divisible by 3. OR Using proof by contradiction, suppose \(k^3 + 1\) is divisible by 3. But \((k + 1)\) is not divisible by 3. \(\therefore k^2 - k + 1\) is divisible by 3. \(\therefore (k + 1)^2 - 3k\) is divisible by 3. \(\therefore (k + 1)^2\) and hence \((k + 1)\) is divisible by 3. This is a contradiction.

questions.title: Q16 (a) Two masses, 2m kg and 4m kg, are attached by a light string. The string is placed
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Two masses, \(2m\text{ kg}\) and \(4m\text{ kg}\), are attached by a light string. The string is placed over a smooth pulley as shown. The two masses are at rest before being released and \(v\) is the velocity of the larger mass at time \(t\) seconds after they are released. The force due to air resistance on each mass has magnitude \(kv\), where \(k\) is a positive constant.

(ai) Show that \(\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{gm - kv}{3m}\).

(aii) Given that \(v < \frac{gm}{k}\), show that when \(t = \frac{3m}{k}\ln 2\), the velocity of the larger mass is \(\frac{gm}{2k}\).

(b) Let \(I_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^{2n+1}(2\theta) \, d\theta, \, n = 0, 1, \dots

(i) Prove that \(I_n = \frac{2n}{2n+1} I_{n-1}, \, n \ge 1.

(ii) Deduce that \(I_n = \frac{2^n (n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}.

Let \(J_n = \int_0^1 x^n (1-x)^n \, dx, \, n = 0, 1, 2, \dots

(iii) Using the result of part (ii), or otherwise, show that \(J_n = \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!}.

(iv) Prove that \((2^n n!)^2 \le (2n+1)!.\)
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) Let \(a\) be the acceleration of the masses. Comparing forces,
\[4gm - kv - 2gm - kv = 2ma + 4ma\]
\[\therefore 2gm - 2kv = 6am\]
\[\therefore a = \frac{gm - kv}{3m}\]
(a(ii)) v = \(\frac{gm}{2k}\)
(b(i)) \[ I_n = \frac{2n}{2n+1} I_{n-1} \]
(b(ii)) \( I_n = \frac{2^n (n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \)
(b(iii)) \[ J_n = \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \]
(b(iv)) The quadratic expression \(x(1-x)\) has maximum value of \(\frac{1}{4}\) when \(x=\frac{1}{2}\).
\[ J_n = \int_0^1 x^n(1-x)^n dx \leq \int_0^1 \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n dx = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \]
\[ \therefore \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \leq \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \implies (2^n n!)^2 \leq (2n+1)! \]

\[ \therefore J_n = \int_0^1 x^n(1-x)^n dx \leq \int_0^1 \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n dx = \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \]
\[ \therefore \frac{(n!)^2}{(2n+1)!} \leq \frac{1}{2^{2n}} \]
\[ \Rightarrow (2^n n!)^2 \leq (2n+1)! \]
(b(iv)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the maximum value of \(x(1-x)\), for \(0 \leq x \leq 1\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q28 Consider the following dataset. 1 5 9 10 15 Suppose a new value, x, is added to this d
questions.promptLatex: Consider the following dataset. \[ 1 \quad 5 \quad 9 \quad 10 \quad 15 \] Suppose a new value, \(x\), is added to this dataset, giving the following. \[ 1 \quad 5 \quad 9 \quad 10 \quad 15 \quad x \] It is known that \(x\) is greater than 15. It is also known that the difference between the means of the two datasets is equal to ten times the difference between the medians of the two datasets. Calculate the value of \(x\).
workingLatex:
Mean of first set of data = 8
Mean of second set of data = \(\frac{40 + x}{6}\)
Median of first set of data = 9
Median of second set of data = 9.5
\[\frac{40 + x}{6} - 8 = 10(9.5 - 9)\]
\[\frac{40 + x}{6} - 8 = 5\]
\[\frac{40 + x}{6} = 13\]
\[40 + x = 13 \times 6\]
\[= 78\]
\[x = 38\]

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Calculates the mean and median of the first dataset and the mean of the second dataset, or equivalent merit; Calculates mean and median of first dataset, or equivalent merit; Calculates median of first dataset, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q32 The diagram shows a regular decagon (ten-sided shape with all sides equal and all inte
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows a regular decagon (ten-sided shape with all sides equal and all interior angles equal). The decagon has centre \(O\). questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
Area \(\approx 492.4  cm^2\)
workingLatex:
\[ AB = 80 \div 10 = 8  cm \]
\[ \angle AOB = 360^\circ \div 10 = 36^\circ \]
\[ \angle OAB = \frac{180^\circ - 36^\circ}{2} = 72^\circ \]
\[ \frac{r}{\sin 72^\circ} = \frac{8}{\sin 36^\circ} \]
\[ r = 12.944... \]
\[ Area = 10 \times \frac{1}{2} \times (12.944...)^2 \times \sin 36^\circ \]
\[ \approx 492.4  cm^2 \]

questions.title: Q37 Wilma deposited a lump sum into a new bank account which earns 2\% per annum compound
questions.promptLatex:
Wilma deposited a lump sum into a new bank account which earns \(2\%\) per annum compound interest. Present value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) for various interest rates (\(r\)) and numbers of periods (\(N\)) are given in the table. Wilma was able to make the following withdrawals from this account:

- \(\$1000\) at the end of each year for twenty years (starting one year after the account is opened)
- \(\$3000\) each year for ten years starting 21 years after the account is opened.
Calculate the minimum lump sum Wilma must have deposited when she opened the new account.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
Present value = \(\$34\,486\)
workingLatex:
Present value of \(\$1000\) annuity for 20 years: \(16.351 \times 1000 = \$16\,351\). Present value of \(\$3000\) annuity for 30 years: \(22.396 \times 3000 = \$67\,188\). Present value of \(\$3000\) annuity for 20 years: \(16.351 \times 3000 = \$49\,053\). Present value = \(16\,351 + 67\,188 - 49\,053 = \$34\,486\).

Alternate solution:
Present value of \(\$3000\) annuity for the last 10 years = \(\frac{3000 \times 8.983}{(1 + 0.02)^{20}}\).
Total present value = \(16.351 \times 1000 + \frac{3000 \times 8.983}{(1 + 0.02)^{20}} = \$34\,486.90\).

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Provides calculation for the value of the \(\$1000\) annuity for 20 years, or equivalent merit; Identifies an appropriate annuity factor, or equivalent merit

questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find the maximum height above ground level reached by the particle.

(ab) A particle is shot vertically upwards from a point 100 metres above ground level. The position of the particle, \(y\) metres above the ground after \(t\) seconds, is given by \(y(t) = -5t^2 + 70t + 100\).

(b) To find when the particle hits the ground, we solve \[ -5t^2 + 70t + 100 = 0 \] \[ t^2 - 14t - 20 = 0 \] \[ t = \frac{14 \pm \sqrt{196 + 80}}{2} = 7 \pm \sqrt{69} \] Since \(t > 0\), \(t = 7 + \sqrt{69}\). \[ y'(7 + \sqrt{69}) = -10(7 + \sqrt{69}) + 70 = -10\sqrt{69} \] Therefore, \(a = -10\) and \(b = 69\).

questions.title: Q27 (ab) Kenzo has a solar powered phone charger. Its power, P, can be modelled by the fun
questions.promptLatex:
(ab) Kenzo has a solar powered phone charger. Its power, \(P\), can be modelled by the function \[P(t) = 400 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right), 0 \le t \le 12,\] where \(t\) is the number of hours after sunrise.

(a) Sketch the graph of \(P\) for \(0 \le t \le 12\).

Power is the rate of change of energy. Hence the amount of energy, \(E\) units, generated by the solar powered phone charger from \(t = a\) to \(t = b\), where \(0 \le a \le b \le 12\) is given by \[E = \int_{a}^{b} P(t) dt.\]

(b) Show that \(E = \frac{4800}{\pi} \left(\cos\frac{a\pi}{12} - \cos\frac{b\pi}{12}\right). \)

(c) To make a phone call, a phone battery needs at least \(300\) units of energy. Kenzo woke up \(3\) hours after sunrise and found that his phone battery had no units of energy. He immediately began to use his solar powered charger to charge his phone battery. Find the least amount of time he needed to wait before he could make a phone call. Give your answer correct to the nearest minute.

(d) The next day, Kenzo woke up \(6\) hours after sunrise and again found that his phone battery had no units of energy. He immediately began to use his solar powered charger to charge his phone battery. Would it take more time or less time or the same amount of time, compared to the answer in part (c), to charge his phone battery in order to make a phone call? Explain your answer by referring to the graph drawn in part (a).

(c) To make a phone call, a phone battery needs at least \(300\) units of energy. Kenzo woke up \(3\) hours after sunrise and found that his phone battery had no units of energy. He immediately began to use his solar powered charger to charge his phone battery. Find the least amount of time he needed to wait before he could make a phone call. Give your answer correct to the nearest minute.

(d) The next day, Kenzo woke up \(6\) hours after sunrise and again found that his phone battery had no units of energy. He immediately began to use his solar powered charger to charge his phone battery. Would it take more time or less time or the same amount of time, compared to the answer in part (c), to charge his phone battery in order to make a phone call? Explain your answer by referring to the graph drawn in part (a).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) Period = \( \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{12}} = 24 \) hours. Graph showing a sine curve starting at (0,0), peaking at (6, 400), and returning to the t-axis at (12,0).
(b) \[ E = \int_{a}^{b} 400 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) dt = \frac{4800}{\pi} \left( \cos\left(\frac{a\pi}{12}\right) - \cos\left(\frac{b\pi}{12}\right) \right) \]
(c) b = \(3.95, \therefore He needs to wait  0.95 \times 60 = 57  minutes (to the nearest minute)\)
(d) It would take less time to charge his phone battery. By starting to charge the battery six hours after sunrise, he is charging the phone at the time when the power is greatest.
workingLatex: (a) Period = \( \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{12}} = 24 \) hours.

(a) Marking criteria: Provides correct sketch; Provides a sine graph with the correct amplitude, or equivalent merit; Provides correct solution for (b); Provides correct anti-derivative of \( \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) \), or equivalent merit; Provides correct solution for (c); Calculates the value of b, or equivalent merit; Substitutes into E given in part (b), or equivalent merit; Provides correct solution for (d)

(b) \[ E = \int_{a}^{b} P(t) dt = \int_{a}^{b} 400 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) dt = -\frac{400(12)}{\pi} \left[ \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) \right]_{a}^{b} = -\frac{4800}{\pi} \left( \cos\frac{b\pi}{12} - \cos\frac{a\pi}{12} \right) = \frac{4800}{\pi} \left( \cos\frac{a\pi}{12} - \cos\frac{b\pi}{12} \right) \]
(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Provides correct anti-derivative of \( \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) \), or equivalent merit

(c) \[ 300 = \frac{4800}{\pi} \left( \cos \frac{3\pi}{12} - \cos \frac{b\pi}{12} \right) \] \[ \frac{\pi}{16} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \cos \frac{b\pi}{12} \] \[ \cos \frac{b\pi}{12} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{\pi}{16} \] \[ b = \frac{12}{\pi} \cos^{-1} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - \frac{\pi}{16} \right) \] \[ b = 3.95 \] \[ \therefore He needs to wait  0.95 \times 60 = 57  minutes (to the nearest minute) \]
(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Calculates the value of b, or equivalent merit; Substitutes into E given in part (b), or equivalent merit
(d) It would take less time to charge his phone battery. By starting to charge the battery six hours after sunrise, he is charging the phone at the time when the power is greatest.

questions.title: Q31 By considering the equation of the tangent to y = x^2 - 1 at the point (a, a^2 - 1), f
questions.promptLatex: By considering the equation of the tangent to \(y = x^2 - 1\) at the point \((a, a^2 - 1)\), find the equations of the two tangents to \(y = x^2 - 1\) which pass through \((3, -8)\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
The equations of the two tangents are \(y = 14x - 50\) and \(y = -2x - 2\).
workingLatex:
\[y = x^2 - 1\]
\[y' = 2x\]
Gradient of tangent at \(x = a\) is \(2a\).
\[\therefore Equation of tangent at  (a, a^2 - 1)  is\]
\[y - (a^2 - 1) = 2a(x - a)\]
\[y - a^2 + 1 = 2ax - 2a^2\]
\[y = 2ax - a^2 - 1\]
Since the tangent passes through \((3, -8)\) then
\[-8 = 6a - a^2 - 1\]
\[a^2 - 6a - 7 = 0\]
\[(a - 7)(a + 1) = 0\]
\[a = 7, \quad a = -1\]
\[\therefore The equations of the two tangents are\]
When \(a = 7\), \(y = 2(7)x - 7^2 - 1 \implies y = 14x - 50\)
and when \(a = -1\), \(y = 2(-1)x - (-1)^2 - 1 \implies y = -2x - 2\)

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Substitute \((3, -8)\) into the equation of the tangent at \(x = a\), or equivalent merit; Finds the equation of the tangent at \(x = a\), or equivalent merit; Finds the gradient of the tangent at \(x = a\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q32 In a particular city, the heights of adult females and the heights of adult males are
questions.promptLatex:
In a particular city, the heights of adult females and the heights of adult males are each normally distributed. Information relating to two females from that city is given in Table 1.

Table 1

| Height | Gender | Percentage of females in this city shorter than this person |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| \(175\text{ cm}\) | Female | \(97.5\%\) |
| \(160.6\text{ cm}\) | Female | \(16\%\) |

The means and standard deviations of adult females and males, in centimetres, are given in Table 2.

Table 2

| | Mean | Standard deviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Females | \(\mu\) | \(\sigma\) |
| Males | \(1.05\mu\) | \(1.1\sigma\) |

A selected male is taller than \(84\%\) of the population of adult males in this city. By first labelling the normal distribution curve below with the heights of the two females given in Table 1, calculate the height of the selected male, in centimetres, correct to two decimal places.
workingLatex:
\[ \sigma = \frac{175 - 160.6}{3} = 4.8 \]
\[ \mu = 160.6 + 4.8 = 165.4 \]
\(Selected male is taller than  84\%  of population\)
\(\therefore Selected male is  1  standard deviation above mean.\)
\(Mean height for males = 1.05 \times 165.4 = 173.67\)
\(Standard deviation of height for males = 4.8 \times 1.1 = 5.28\)
\(\therefore Height = 1.05\mu + 1.1\sigma = 173.67 + 5.28 = 178.95  cm\)

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the mean and standard deviation for the heights of adult males, or equivalent merit; Finds \(\sigma\), or equivalent merit; Labels the diagram, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q33 (a) People are given a maximum of six hours to complete a puzzle. The time spent on th
questions.promptLatex:
(a) People are given a maximum of six hours to complete a puzzle. The time spent on the puzzle, in hours, can be modelled using the continuous random variable \(X\) which has probability density function
\[ f(x) = \[\begin{cases} \frac{Ax}{x^2 + 4}, & \text{for } 0 \le x \le 6, \text{ (where } A > 0) \\ 0, & \text{for all other values of } x \end{cases}\] \]
The graph of the probability density function is shown below. The graph has a local maximum.

(a) Show that \(A = \frac{2}{\ln 10}\).

(b) Show that the mode of \(X\) is two hours.

(c) Show that \(P(X < 2) = \log_{10}2\).

(d) The Intelligence Quotient (IQ) scores of people are normally distributed with a mean of \(100\) and standard deviation of \(15\). It has been observed that the puzzle is generally completed more quickly by people with a high IQ. It is known that \(80\%\) of people with an IQ greater than \(130\) can complete the puzzle in less than two hours. A person chosen at random can complete the puzzle in less than two hours. What is the probability that this person has an IQ greater than \(130\)? Give your answer correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) A = \(\frac{2}{\ln 10}\)
(b) The mode of \(X\) is \(2\).
(c) \(\log_{10} 2\)
(d) 0.066
workingLatex: (a) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the anti-derivative of \(\frac{Ax}{x^2 + 4}\), or equivalent merit
(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the correct derivative of \(f(x)\), or equivalent merit
(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Provides correct definite integral, or equivalent merit

(d) \[ P(IQ > 130) = P\left(\frac{IQ - 100}{15} > \frac{130 - 100}{15}\right) = P(Z > 2) = 2.5\% \] \[ P(IQ > 130 \mid Time to complete < 2) = \frac{P(IQ > 130 \cap Time to complete < 2)}{P(Time to complete < 2)} = \frac{2.5\% \times 80\%}{\log_{10} 2} = 0.06643... = 0.066  (three decimal places) \]
(d) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Attempts to find the conditional probability, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q34 A discrete random variable has probability distribution as shown in the table where n
questions.promptLatex: A discrete random variable has probability distribution as shown in the table where \(n\) is a finite positive integer. Show that \(E(X) = n(2r - 1)\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
E(X) = nr^{n+1} = n(2r-1)
workingLatex:
\[r + r^2 + \dots + r^n = 1\]
Sum of GP with \(a=r\), common ratio \(r\), number of terms \(n\)
\[\therefore \frac{r(r^n - 1)}{r - 1} = 1\]
\[r^{n+1} - r = r - 1\]
\[r^{n+1} = 2r - 1 \quad (\ast)\]
\[E(X) = \sum xP(X=x)\]
\[= r \times r^n + r^2 \times r^{n-1} + \dots + r^n \times r\]
\[= r^{n+1} + r^{n+1} + \dots + r^{n+1}\]
\[= r^{n+1} \times n\]
\[= nr^{n+1}\]
\[= n(2r-1) \quad from  (\ast)\]

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Shows \(E(X) = nr^{n+1}\) and attempts to find the sum of probabilities using sum of GP formula, or equivalent merit; Finds correct expression for \(E(X)\) or writes the sum of probabilities equal to 1

questions.promptLatex:

The temperature of the water can be modelled using the differential equation \[\frac{dT}{dt} = k(T - 25)\] (Do NOT prove this.) where \(k\) is the growth constant. After 8 minutes, the temperature of the water is \(10^{\circ}\text{C}\). (dii) Find the equation of the inverse function, \(f^{-1}(x)\), and state its domain.

(diii) Sketch the graph of \(y = f^{-1}(x)\).

(a) Sketch the graph of the particular solution that passes through the point \(P\).

(d) A tail with area 2.5% means \(\hat{p} = \frac{4}{500}\) is two standard deviations above the mean. So
\[ \frac{4}{500} = \mu + 2\sigma \]
\[ \frac{4}{500} = \frac{3}{500} + 2\sqrt{\frac{\frac{3}{500} \times \left(1 - \frac{3}{500}\right)}{n}} \]
\[ \frac{1}{500} = 2\frac{\sqrt{\frac{3}{500} \left(\frac{497}{500}\right)}}{\sqrt{n}} \]
\[ \sqrt{n} = 2 \times 500 \sqrt{\frac{3}{500} \times \frac{497}{500}} = 2 \times 500 \frac{\sqrt{3 \times 497}}{500} \]
\[ n = 4 \times 3 \times 497 = 5964 \]
The sample size to be chosen so that the chances of shutting down unnecessarily is less than 2.5% is \(n = 5964\), ie approximately 6000.

(d) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Recognises \(2\sigma\) is important AND has \(\sigma\) in terms of \(n\) OR Recognises \(2\sigma\) is important AND has the value of \(\sigma\) OR Obtains \(2 = \frac{\sqrt{n}}{c}\) where \(c\) is a constant, or equivalent merit; Finds \(\sigma\) in terms of \(n\) OR Sketches a normal distribution and shades the correct region OR Writes \(P\left( \hat{p} \ge \frac{4}{500} \right) < 0.025\), or explains in words, or equivalent merit

(e) \[g(1)=3 \implies g^{-1}(3)=1 \\ Using product rule: \\ f'(x)=g^{-1}(x).1+x.\frac{d}{dx}(g^{-1}(x)) \\ Now if  g^{-1}(x)=y  then  x=g(y) \\ \therefore \frac{dx}{dy}=g'(y) \\ and  \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{g'(y)} \\ \therefore \frac{d}{dx}(g^{-1}(x))=\frac{1}{g'(g^{-1}(x))} \\ \therefore f'(x)=g^{-1}(x)+\frac{x}{g'(g^{-1}(x))} \\ \therefore f'(3)=g^{-1}(3)+\frac{3}{g'(g^{-1}(3))} \\ =1+\frac{3}{g'(1)} \\ Now,  g(x)=x^3+4x-2 \\ \therefore g'(x)=3x^2+4 \\ \therefore f'(3)=1+\frac{3}{3(1)^2+4} \\ \therefore gradient of tangent = \frac{10}{7}\]
(e) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains an expression for the derivative of \(g^{-1}(x)\) in terms of \(x\), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q12 (a) Find 2x+3x^2+2x+2 dx. (bi) Consider Statement A: 'If n^2 is even, then n is even.'
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find \(\int \frac{2x+3}{x^2+2x+2} dx\).

(bi) Consider Statement A: 'If \(n^2\) is even, then \(n\) is even.' What is the converse of Statement A?

(bii) Show that the converse of Statement A is true.

(c) Two lines are given by \(\mathbf{r}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{r}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \\ q \end{pmatrix} + \mu \begin{pmatrix} p \\ 3 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\), where \(p\) and \(q\) are real numbers. These lines intersect and are perpendicular. Find the values of \(p\) and \(q\).

(d) Prove by mathematical induction that \(\sqrt{n!} > 2^n\), for integers \(n \ge 9\).

(ei) The diagram shows the pyramid \(ABCDS\) where \(ABCD\) is a square. The diagonals of the square bisect each other at \(H\). Show that \(\vec{HA} + \vec{HB} + \vec{HC} + \vec{HD} = \mathbf{0}\).

(eii) Let \(G\) be the point such that \(\vec{GA} + \vec{GB} + \vec{GC} + \vec{GD} + \vec{GS} = \mathbf{0}\). Using part (i), or otherwise, show that \(4\vec{GH} + \vec{GS} = \mathbf{0}\).

(eiii) Find the value of \(\lambda\) such that \(\vec{HG} = \lambda\vec{HS}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) \[ \int \frac{2x+3}{x^2+2x+2} dx = \ln(x^2+2x+2) + \tan^{-1}(x+1) + k \]
(b(i)) If \( n \) is even, then \( n^2 \) is even.
(b(ii)) If \( n \) is even then \( n = 2k \) for some integer \( k \), then \( n^2 = (2k)^2 = 2(2k^2) \) which is also even.
(c) \(p=2, q=20\)
(d) By principle of mathematical induction, the inequality \(\sqrt{n!} > 2^n\) is true for \(n \ge 9\).
(e(i)) \(\vec{HA} + \vec{HB} + \vec{HC} + \vec{HD} = \vec{0}\)
(e(ii)) \(4\vec{GH} + \vec{GS} = \vec{0}\)
(e(iii)) \(\lambda = \frac{1}{5}\)

(b(i)) The converse is 'If \( n \) is even, then \( n^2 \) is even'.
(b(i)) Marking criteria: States the converse
(b(ii)) If \( n \) is even then \( n = 2k \) for some integer \( k \), then \( n^2 = (2k)^2 = 2(2k^2) \) which is also even.
(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof

(c) \[\mathbf{r}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix} + \lambda \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 0 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ -2 \\ q \end{pmatrix} + \mu \begin{pmatrix} p \\ 3 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}\]
They are perpendicular \(\therefore \mathbf{r}_1 \cdot \mathbf{r}_2 = 0\)
\[1 \times p + 0 \times 3 + 2 \times -1 = 0\]
\[p - 2 = 0\]
\[p = 2\]
They intersect \(\therefore\) components are equal
(1) \(-2 + \lambda = 4 + p\mu\)
(2) \(1 = -2 + 3\mu \implies 3 = 3\mu  so  \mu = 1\)
(3) \(3 + 2\lambda = q - \mu\)
Substitute \(\mu = 1\) and \(p = 2\) into (1)
\[-2 + \lambda = 4 + 2 \times 1\]
\[\lambda = 8\]
Substitute \(\lambda = 8\) and \(\mu = 1\) into (3)
\[3 + 2 \times 8 = q - 1\]
\[19 = q - 1\]
\[q = 20\]
\(\therefore p = 2  and  q = 20\).

(d) Prove it's true for \(n=9\):
\[\sqrt{9!} = 602.39... \quad 2^9 = 512 \implies \sqrt{9!} > 2^9\]
Assume it's true for \(n=k\): \(\sqrt{k!} > 2^k\)
Prove it's true for \(n=k+1\): Required to prove \(\sqrt{(k+1)!} > 2^{k+1}\)
\[LHS = \sqrt{(k+1)!} = \sqrt{k!} \times \sqrt{k+1} > 2^k \times \sqrt{k+1} > 2^k \times 2 = 2^{k+1} = RHS\]
\[\therefore \sqrt{(k+1)!} > 2^{k+1}  as required\]

(e(i)) Consider the square base. As \(H\) is the midpoint of \(AC\) and \(BD\), when you add them head to tail, you end up back where you started. \(\therefore \vec{HA} + \vec{HB} + \vec{HC} + \vec{HD} = \vec{0}\)
(e(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution

(e(ii)) \(\vec{GA} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HA}\), \(\vec{GB} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HB}\), \(\vec{GC} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HC}\), \(\vec{GD} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HD}\). Adding these: \(\vec{GA} + \vec{GB} + \vec{GC} + \vec{GD} = 4\vec{GH} + \vec{HA} + \vec{HB} + \vec{HC} + \vec{HD} - \vec{GS} = 4\vec{GH} + \vec{0}\). \(\therefore 4\vec{GH} + \vec{GS} = \vec{0}\)
(e(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Writes \(\vec{GA} = \vec{GH} + \vec{HA}\), or equivalent merit

(e(iii)) From part (ii)
\[4\vec{GH} + \vec{GS} = \underset{\sim}{0}\]
\[4\vec{GH} + \vec{GH} + \vec{HS} = \underset{\sim}{0}\]
\[5\vec{GH} + \vec{HS} = \underset{\sim}{0}\]
\[\vec{HS} = -5\vec{GH}\]
\[= 5\vec{HG}\]
\[\vec{HG} = \frac{1}{5}\vec{HS}\]
\[\therefore \lambda = \frac{1}{5}\]
(e(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution

questions.title: Q14 (a) Evaluate _0^2 13 + 5 x dx. (bi) An object of mass 5 kg is on a slope that is incli
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Evaluate \(\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{1}{3 + 5\cos x} dx\).

(bi) An object of mass \(5\text{ kg}\) is on a slope that is inclined at an angle of \(60^{\circ}\) to the horizontal. The acceleration due to gravity is \(g\text{ m s}^{-2}\) and the velocity of the object down the slope is \(v\text{ m s}^{-1}\). As well as the force due to gravity, the object is acted on by two forces, one of magnitude \(2v\text{ newtons}\) and one of magnitude \(2v^2\text{ newtons}\), both acting up the slope. Show that the resultant force down the slope is \(\frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}g - 2v - 2v^2\text{ newtons}\).

(bii) There is one value of \(v\) such that the object will slide down the slope at a constant speed. Find this value of \(v\text{ in m s}^{-1}\), correct to 1 decimal place, given that \(g = 10\).

(ci) Using de Moivre's theorem and the binomial expansion of \((\cos \theta + i \sin \theta)^5\), or otherwise, show that \(\cos 5\theta = 16\cos^5 \theta - 20\cos^3 \theta + 5\cos \theta\).

(cii) By using part (i), or otherwise, show that \(\text{Re}\left(e^{\frac{i\pi}{10}}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{5 + \sqrt{5}}{8}}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) \(\frac{1}{4}\ln 3\)
(b(i)) Resultant force down slope = \(5g \cos 30^{\circ} - 2v - 2v^2 = \frac{5\sqrt{3}}{2}g - 2v - 2v^2\)
(b(ii)) Constant speed \(\Rightarrow\) Resultant force = 0. Solving the quadratic equation leads to \(v \simeq 4.2  ms^{-1}\).
(c(i)) \[ \cos 5\theta = 16\cos^5 \theta - 20\cos^3 \theta + 5\cos \theta \]
(c(ii)) \[\cos\frac{\pi}{10} = \sqrt{\frac{5+\sqrt{5}}{8}}\]

questions.title: Q15 (aiii) For all non-negative real numbers x and y, xy x+y2. (Do NOT prove this.) (i) Us
questions.promptLatex:
(aiii) For all non-negative real numbers \(x\) and \(y\), \(\sqrt{xy} \le \frac{x+y}{2}\). (Do NOT prove this.)

(i) Using this fact, show that for all non-negative real numbers \(a, b\) and \(c\),
\[\sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2+b^2+2c}{4}.\]

(ii) Using part (i), or otherwise, show that for all non-negative real numbers \(a, b\) and \(c\),
\[\sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2+b^2+c^2+a+b+c}{6}.\]

(biii) For integers \(n \ge 1\), the triangular numbers \(t_n\) are defined by \(t_n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\), giving \(t_1 = 1, t_2 = 3, t_3 = 6, t_4 = 10\) and so on.

For integers \(n \ge 1\), the hexagonal numbers \(h_n\) are defined by \(h_n = 2n^2 - n\), giving \(h_1 = 1, h_2 = 6, h_3 = 15, h_4 = 28\) and so on.

(i) Show that the triangular numbers \(t_1, t_3, t_5\), and so on, are also hexagonal numbers.

(ii) Show that the triangular numbers \(t_2, t_4, t_6\), and so on, are not hexagonal numbers.

(ciii) An object of mass \(1\text{ kg}\) is projected vertically upwards with an initial velocity of \(u\text{ m/s}\). It experiences air resistance of magnitude \(kv^2\text{ newtons}\) where \(v\) is the velocity of the object, in \(\text{m/s}\), and \(k\) is a positive constant. The height of the object above its starting point is \(x\text{ metres}\). The time since projection is \(t\text{ seconds}\) and acceleration due to gravity is \(g\text{ m/s}^2\).

(i) Show that the time for the object to reach its maximum height is \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \arctan\left(u\sqrt{\frac{k}{g}}\right)\text{ seconds}\).

(ii) Find an expression for the maximum height reached by the object, in terms of \(k\), \(g\) and \(u\).

(d) Prove that \(2^n + 3^n \neq 5^n\) for all integers \(n \geq 2\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) Let \(x = \frac{a^2+b^2}{2}\) and \(y = c\). Then \[\sqrt{\frac{a^2+b^2}{2} \cdot c} \leq \frac{\frac{a^2+b^2}{2} + c}{2} = \frac{a^2+b^2+2c}{4}\]
(a(ii)) \[ \sqrt{abc} \le \frac{a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + a + b + c}{6} \]
(b(i)) The odd triangular numbers are hexagonal.
(b(ii)) The even numbers \(2, 4, 6 \dots\) can be expressed as \(2m\) where \(m\) is an integer. \[t_{2m} = \frac{2m(2m+1)}{2} = m(2m+1) = 2m^2 + m\] Use proof by contradiction to show it is not hexagonal. Assume it is hexagonal. \[2m^2 + m = 2k^2 - k \quad where  k  is an integer\] \[m + k = 2k^2 - 2m^2\] \[m + k = 2(k^2 - m^2)\] \[m + k = 2(k - m)(k + m)\] \(\therefore 1 = 2(k - m) \quad since  k + m \neq 0\) So, 1 is even, which is not true \(\therefore\) the original statement is false.
(c(i)) \[ t = \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \tan^{-1} \left( u \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}} \right) - \frac{1}{\sqrt{gk}} \tan^{-1} \left( v \sqrt{\frac{k}{g}} \right) \]
(c(ii)) \(x = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{g + ku^2}{g} \right)\)
(d) \(2^n + 3^n \neq 5^n  if  n \geq 2\)

questions.title: Q16 (a(i)) Using the position vector of P, OP = xi + yj + zk, and the triangle inequality,
questions.promptLatex:
(a(i)) Using the position vector of \(P\), \(\vec{OP} = x\underset{\sim}{i} + y\underset{\sim}{j} + z\underset{\sim}{k}\), and the triangle inequality, or otherwise, show that \(|x| + |y| + |z| \geq 1\).

(a(ii)) Given the vectors \(\underset{\sim}{a} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{b} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \\ b_3 \end{pmatrix}\), show that \(|a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3| \leq \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2}\sqrt{b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2}\).

(a(iii)) As in part (i), the point \(P(x, y, z)\) lies on the sphere of radius 1 centred at the origin \(O\). Using part (ii), or otherwise, show that \(|x| + |y| + |z| \leq \sqrt{3}\).

(b) A particle which is projected from the origin with initial speed \(u \text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle of \(\theta\) to the positive \(x\)-axis lands on the \(x\)-axis, as shown in the diagram. The particle is subject to an acceleration due to gravity of \(g \text{ m s}^{-2}\).

The position vector of the particle, \(\underset{\sim}{r}(t)\), where \(t\) is the time in seconds after the particle is projected, is given by

\[\underset{\sim}{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \ -\dfrac{gt^2}{2} + ut \sin \theta \end{pmatrix}. \quad (\text{Do NOT prove this.})\]

For some value(s) of \(\theta\) there will be two times during the time of flight when the particle's position vector is perpendicular to its velocity vector.

Find the value(s) of \(\theta\) for which this occurs, justifying that both times occur during the time of flight.

(c) Sketch the region of the complex plane defined by \(\text{Re}(z) \ge \text{Arg}(z)\) where \(\text{Arg}(z)\) is the principal argument of \(z\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) Point \(P\) is on the unit sphere, so \(\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = 1\). Hence \(|x|^2 + |y|^2 + |z|^2 = 1\), and since each of \(|x|, |y|, |z|\) is at most \(1\), \(|x|^2 \leq |x|\), \(|y|^2 \leq |y|\), and \(|z|^2 \leq |z|\). Therefore \(|x| + |y| + |z| \geq 1\).
(a(ii)) \[ |a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3| \le \sqrt{a_1^2 + a_2^2 + a_3^2} \sqrt{b_1^2 + b_2^2 + b_3^2} \]
(a(iii)) \[ |x| + |y| + |z| \le \sqrt{3} \]
(b) \[ r(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ -\frac{gt^2}{2} + ut \sin \theta \end{pmatrix}, \dot{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ -gt + u \sin \theta \end{pmatrix} \] \[ r(t) \cdot \dot{r}(t) = 0 \implies \frac{t}{2}(g^2t^2 - 3ugt \sin \theta + 2u^2) = 0 \] For \(t > 0\), the condition is \(g^2t^2 - 3ugt \sin \theta + 2u^2 = 0\).
(c) The region is determined by the inequality \(x \tan(x) < y\) (or similar based on the specific question context). The solution involves identifying the regions in the Argand plane where the condition holds, considering the quadrants and the behavior of the tangent function.

(a(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Chooses one suitable vector to use with the result from part (ii), or equivalent merit

(b) Position \( r(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \cos \theta \\ -\frac{gt^2}{2} + ut \sin \theta \end{pmatrix} \), Velocity \( \dot{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} u \cos \theta \\ -gt + u \sin \theta \end{pmatrix} \). If position vector is perpendicular to velocity vector then \( 0 = r(t) \cdot \dot{r}(t) = u^2t \cos^2 \theta + (-\frac{gt^2}{2} + ut \sin \theta)(-gt + u \sin \theta) = u^2t \cos^2 \theta + \frac{g^2t^3}{2} - \frac{gt^2u}{2} \sin \theta - gt^2u \sin \theta + u^2t \sin^2 \theta = u^2t - \frac{3ugt^2 \sin \theta}{2} + \frac{g^2t^3}{2} = \frac{t}{2}(g^2t^2 - 3ugt \sin \theta + 2u^2) \). During time of flight \( t > 0 \) so above can only be zero when \( g^2t^2 - 3ugt \sin \theta + 2u^2 = 0 \).

(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution, with justification; Shows that the relevant times are positive OR Shows that the relevant times occur before the particle lands, or equivalent merit; Obtains a quadratic that will identify all possible angles, or equivalent merit; Uses the fact that the dot product of the two vectors is 0, or equivalent merit; Obtains velocity vector, or equivalent merit

(c) If \(z = x + iy\) and \(Arg(z) = \theta\) then \(-\pi < \theta \leq \pi\).
\[ \tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}  when  x \neq 0 \]
\[ Re(z) = x \]
In 2nd quadrant \(Re(z) = x < 0 < Arg(z) < \frac{\pi}{2}\) so 2nd quadrant not in region.
In 4th quadrant \(Re(z) > 0 > Arg(z)\) so 4th quadrant included in region.
In 1st quadrant \(Arg(z) < \frac{\pi}{2}\) so if \(x \geq \frac{\pi}{2}\) then \(z\) is included in region.
In 3rd quadrant \(-\pi < Arg(z) < -\frac{\pi}{2}\) so \(x < -\pi\) excluded and \(x > -\frac{\pi}{2}\) included.

(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct sketch; Considers inequalities involving \(x \tan(x)\) AND has included or excluded at least one section of the Argand plane; Obtains the region below; States that a nominated section of the Argand plane is included in the required region OR States that a nominated section of the Argand plane is excluded from the required region

questions.title: Q41 In a particular city, the heights of adult females and the heights of adult males are
questions.promptLatex:
In a particular city, the heights of adult females and the heights of adult males are each normally distributed. Information relating to two females from that city is given in Table 1.

Table 1
| Height | Gender | Percentage of females in this city shorter than this person |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| \(175\text{ cm}\) | Female | \(97.5\%\) |
| \(160.6\text{ cm}\) | Female | \(16\%\) |

The means and standard deviations of adult females and males, in centimetres, are given in Table 2.

Table 2
| | Mean | Standard deviation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Females | \(\mu\) | \(\sigma\) |
| Males | \(1.05\mu\) | \(1.1\sigma\) |

A selected male is taller than \(84\%\) of the population of adult males in this city. By first labelling the normal distribution curve below with the heights of the two females given in Table 1, calculate the height of the selected male, in centimetres, correct to two decimal places.
workingLatex: \[\sigma = \frac{175 - 160.6}{3} = 4.8\] \[\mu = 160.6 + 4.8 = 165.4\] Selected male is taller than 84% of population \(\therefore\) Selected male is 1 standard deviation above mean. Mean height for males = \(1.05 \times 165.4 = 173.67\). Standard deviation of height for males = \(4.8 \times 1.1 = 5.28\). \(\therefore\) Height = \(1.05\mu + 1.1\sigma = 173.67 + 5.28 = 178.95  cm\)

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the mean and standard deviation for the heights of adult males, or equivalent merit; Finds \(\sigma\), or equivalent merit; Labels the diagram, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Mean and median after adding a score
questions.subtopic: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
questions.promptLatex: Consider the dataset \(13,16,17,17,21,24\). Which row shows how the median and mean are affected when a score of \(5\) is added to the dataset? Options: A. Median changes, mean changes. B. Median stays the same, mean stays the same. C. Median changes, mean stays the same. D. Median stays the same, mean changes.
questions.answerLatex: D. The median stays the same and the mean changes.
workingLatex: The original median is the average of the third and fourth scores: \(17\).
After adding \(5\), the ordered dataset is \(5,13,16,17,17,21,24\), whose median is still \(17\).
Adding a low score changes the total and the number of scores, so the mean changes.

questions.title: Tower angle expression
questions.promptLatex: A tower \(BT\) has height \(h\) metres. From point \(A\), the angle of elevation to the top of the tower is \(26^\circ\), as shown. Which expression gives the length of \(AB\)? Options: A. \(h\tan26^\circ\), B. \(h\cot26^\circ\), C. \(h\sin26^\circ\), D. \(h\cosec26^\circ\).
workingLatex: In the right triangle, \(h\) is opposite the angle \(26^\circ\), and \(AB\) is adjacent.
\(\tan26^\circ=\frac{h}{AB}\), so \(AB=\frac{h}{\tan26^\circ}=h\cot26^\circ\).

questions.title: Even function definite integral
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the even function \(y=f(x)\) is shown. The area of shaded region \(A\) is \(\frac12\), and the area of shaded region \(B\) is \(\frac32\). What is the value of \(\int_{-2}^{2}f(x)\,dx\)? Options: A. \(4\), B. \(2\), C. \(-2\), D. \(-4\).
workingLatex: Because \(f\) is even, the signed integral over \([-2,2]\) is twice the signed integral over \([0,2]\).
On \([0,2]\), the signed area is \(\frac12-\frac32=-1\).

questions.title: Two game win probability
questions.promptLatex: Liam is playing two games. He is equally likely to win each game. The probability that Liam will win at least one of the games is \(80\%\). Which of the following is closest to the probability that Liam will win both games? Options: A. \(31\%\), B. \(40\%\), C. \(55\%\), D. \(64\%\).
questions.answerLatex: A. The probability is closest to \(31\%\).
workingLatex: Let \(p\) be the probability Liam wins either game.
Assuming the games are independent, \(P(\text{at least one win})=1-(1-p)^2=0.8\).
The probability of winning both games is \(p^2=(1-\sqrt{0.2})^2\approx0.306\), closest to \(31\%\).

questions.title: Composite function graph options
questions.promptLatex: The graphs of \(y=f(x)\) and \(y=g(x)\) are shown. Which graph best represents \(y=g(f(x))\)? Options A-D are shown in the diagram.
workingLatex: The graph of \(f(x)\) is non-negative and has its minimum at \(x=0\), where \(f(0)=0\).
The input to \(g\) is therefore \(f(x)\ge0\), giving a graph symmetric about the \(y\)-axis through the even input pattern of \(f\).
Option B matches the resulting behaviour shown by \(g(f(x))\).

questions.title: Inverse variation melting time model
questions.promptLatex: A student believes that the time it takes for an ice cube to melt, \(M\) minutes, varies inversely with the room temperature, \(T^\circ\text{C}\). The student observes that at a room temperature of \(15^\circ\text{C}\), it takes \(12\) minutes for an ice cube to melt. (a) Find the equation relating \(M\) and \(T\). (b) By first completing the table of values, graph the relationship between temperature and time from \(T=5^\circ\text{C}\) to \(T=30^\circ\text{C}\).
questions.answerLatex: Inverse variation gives \(M=\frac{k}{T}\). Using \(T=15\) and \(M=12\), \(12=\frac{k}{15}\), so \(k=180\) and \(M=\frac{180}{T}\). The completed values are \(M=36\) when \(T=5\), \(M=12\) when \(T=15\), and \(M=6\) when \(T=30\).
workingLatex: \(12=\frac{k}{15}\Rightarrow k=180\).

(b) plot their points from the table of values accurately on the given number plane and draw a smooth curve rather than 2 straight lines.

One die is randomly selected and tossed. (a) What is the probability that the die shows a \(5\)? (b) Given that the die shows a \(5\), what is the probability that it is the special die?
workingLatex: \(P(5)=\frac23\cdot\frac16+\frac13\cdot\frac36\).
\(P(\text{special}\cap5)=\frac13\cdot\frac36=\frac16\).
\(P(\text{special}\mid5)=\frac{1/6}{5/18}=\frac35\).

questions.title: Area enclosed by a parabola and line
questions.promptLatex: The parabola \(y=x^2\) meets the line \(y=2x+3\) at the points \((-1,1)\) and \((3,9)\), as shown in the diagram. Find the area enclosed by the parabola and the line.
questions.answerLatex: The line is above the parabola on \([-1,3]\), so the area is \(\int_{-1}^{3}(2x+3-x^2)\,dx\). This equals \(\left[x^2+3x-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^{3}=\frac{32}{3}\), or \(10\frac23\) square units.

questions.title: House of cards arithmetic series
questions.promptLatex: Cards are stacked to build a house of cards. A house of cards requires \(3\) cards in the top row, \(6\) cards in the next row, and each successive row has \(3\) more cards than the previous row. (a) Show that a house of cards with \(12\) rows has a total of \(234\) cards. (b) Another house of cards has a total of \(828\) cards. How many rows are in this house of cards?
questions.answerLatex: The card counts form an arithmetic series with \(a=3\) and \(d=3\). For \(12\) rows, \(S_{12}=\frac{12}{2}(2\cdot3+11\cdot3)=234\). For \(828\) cards, \(\frac{n}{2}(2\cdot3+(n-1)3)=828\), so \(3n(n+1)=1656\). Hence \(n^2+n-552=0\), giving \(n=23\) rows.

betterResponses: (a) identify that the situation involved an arithmetic series to be calculated

(a) apply the summation formula correctly to show the correct sum of 234 is achieved when 𝑛𝑛= 12, or compile a list of all 12 terms that when added showed that 𝑆𝑆= 234, or solve a quadratic equation (using the quadratic formula, by factorisation or completing the square
(a) demonstrate appropriate substitutions into the formula used from the Reference Sheet after correctly identifying 𝑎𝑎 and 𝑑𝑑.
(b) select the correct formula from the Reference Sheet and obtain and solve the resulting quadratic equation, accepting the positive value and rejecting the negative value
(b) compile a list of all 23 terms that were added to show 𝑆𝑆= 828
(b) use the summation formula using 𝑆𝑆= 828, 𝑎𝑎= 3 and 𝑑𝑑= 3 to guess and check values for 𝑛𝑛, correctly stating that 𝑛𝑛= 23.
improvementAreas: (a) writing at least 3 terms to determine that an arithmetic series and not a geometric series was to be calculated

questions.title: Transformed quadratic parameters
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the function \(f(x)=x^2\) is translated \(m\) units to the right, dilated vertically by a scale factor of \(k\), and then translated \(5\) units down. The equation of the transformed function is \(g(x)=3x^2-12x+7\). Find the values of \(m\) and \(k\).
questions.answerLatex: The transformations give \(g(x)=k(x-m)^2-5\). Completing the square, \(3x^2-12x+7=3(x^2-4x)+7=3(x-2)^2-5\). Hence \(m=2\) and \(k=3\).
workingLatex: Therefore \(m=2\) and \(k=3\).

questions.title: Bacteria exponential growth model
questions.promptLatex: A scientist is studying the growth of bacteria. The scientist models the number of bacteria, \(N\), by \(N(t)=200e^{0.013t}\), where \(t\) is the number of hours after starting the experiment. (a) What is the initial number of bacteria in the experiment? (b) What is the number of bacteria \(24\) hours after starting the experiment? (c) What is the rate of increase in the number of bacteria \(24\) hours after starting the experiment?
questions.answerLatex: Initially, \(N(0)=200\). After \(24\) hours, \(N(24)=200e^{0.013\times24}\approx273\). The derivative is \(\frac{dN}{dt}=200(0.013)e^{0.013t}=0.013N(t)\), so at \(t=24\), \(\frac{dN}{dt}\approx0.013(273.23)\approx3.55\) bacteria per hour.

questions.title: Investment options future value comparison
questions.promptLatex: Eli is choosing between two investment options. Option 1 is depositing a single amount of \(\$40\,000\) today, earning interest of \(1.2\%\) per annum, compounded monthly. Option 2 is depositing \(\$1000\) at the end of each quarter, earning interest of \(2.4\%\) per annum, compounded quarterly. A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) is shown. (a) What is the value of Eli's investment after \(10\) years using Option 1? (b) What is the difference between the future values after \(10\) years using Option 1 and Option 2?
questions.answerLatex: For Option 1, the monthly rate is \(0.012/12=0.001\) and there are \(120\) months, so \(FV=40000(1.001)^{120}\approx\$45\,097.17\). For Option 2, there are \(40\) quarters and the quarterly rate is \(0.024/4=0.006\), so the table factor is \(45.05630\). Thus \(FV=1000(45.05630)=\$45\,056.30\). The difference is \(\$40.87\).

questions.title: Global extrema of a cubic on an interval
questions.promptLatex: Find the global maximum and minimum values of \(y=x^3-6x^2+8\), where \(-1\le x\le7\).
questions.answerLatex: Differentiate to get \(\frac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-12x=3x(x-4)\), so stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=4\). Check these and the endpoints: \(y(-1)=1\), \(y(0)=8\), \(y(4)=-24\), and \(y(7)=57\). The global minimum value is \(-24\), and the global maximum value is \(57\).
workingLatex: Stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=4\).
Therefore the global minimum is \(-24\), and the global maximum is \(57\).

questions.title: Tide depth trigonometric model
questions.promptLatex: The depth of water in a bay rises and falls with the tide. On a particular day the depth of the water, \(d\) metres, can be modelled by \(d=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\), where \(t\) is the time in hours since low tide. (a) Find the depth of water at low tide and at high tide. (b) What is the time interval, in hours, between two successive low tides? (c) For how long between successive low tides will the depth of water be at least \(1\) metre?
questions.answerLatex: The minimum depth is \(1.3-0.6=0.7\) m and the maximum depth is \(1.3+0.6=1.9\) m. The period is \(\frac{2\pi}{4\pi/25}=12.5\) hours. For \(d=1\), \(1=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\), so \(\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=\frac12\). Between successive low tides, this occurs at \(t=\frac{25}{12}\) and \(t=\frac{125}{12}\), so the depth is at least \(1\) metre for \(\frac{125}{12}-\frac{25}{12}=\frac{25}{3}=8\frac13\) hours.
workingLatex: \(1=1.3-0.6\cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)\Rightarrow \cos\left(\frac{4\pi}{25}t\right)=\frac12\).
\(\frac{4\pi}{25}t=\frac\pi3,\frac{5\pi}{3}\).
\(t=\frac{25}{12},\frac{125}{12}\), so the interval is \(\frac{25}{3}\) hours.

questions.answerLatex: The data show a positive approximately linear relationship, but the correlation coefficient \(0.4564\) indicates only a moderate association. The line of best fit suggests that for each additional year of character age, the actor age increases by about \(1.85\) years. Actors playing teenage characters range from about \(14\) to \(30\) years old, so actors do not need to be teenagers themselves. The widest spread of actor ages appears for characters aged \(15\), and characters older than \(15\) are generally played by older actors.
workingLatex: The association is positive and roughly linear.
\(r=0.4564\) indicates a moderate relationship rather than a strong one.
The fitted slope \(1.85\) means predicted actor age increases by about \(1.85\) years per additional year of character age.
The context shows teenage characters can be played by actors substantially older than the characters.

questions.title: Sine derivative conditions
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)=\sin(2x)\). Find the value of \(x\), for \(0<x<\pi\), for which \(f'(x)=-\sqrt3\) and \(f''(x)=2\).
questions.answerLatex: Since \(f'(x)=2\cos(2x)\), \(2\cos(2x)=-\sqrt3\), so \(2x=\frac{5\pi}{6}\) or \(\frac{7\pi}{6}\). Since \(f''(x)=-4\sin(2x)\), \(-4\sin(2x)=2\), so \(\sin(2x)=-\frac12\), giving \(2x=\frac{7\pi}{6}\) or \(\frac{11\pi}{6}\). The common solution is \(2x=\frac{7\pi}{6}\), so \(x=\frac{7\pi}{12}\).
workingLatex: \(2\cos(2x)=-\sqrt3\Rightarrow 2x=\frac{5\pi}{6},\frac{7\pi}{6}\).
\(-4\sin(2x)=2\Rightarrow \sin(2x)=-\frac12\Rightarrow 2x=\frac{7\pi}{6},\frac{11\pi}{6}\).
The common value is \(2x=\frac{7\pi}{6}\), hence \(x=\frac{7\pi}{12}\).
