questions.title: Battery lifespan normal model
questions.promptLatex: The lifespan of a brand of batteries is normally distributed with a mean of \(840\) hours and a standard deviation of \(80\) hours. It is estimated that approximately \(60\%\) of the batteries have a lifespan of less than \(860\) hours. Find the approximate percentage of batteries that have a lifespan between \(820\) hours and \(920\) hours.
questions.answerLatex: Approximately \(44\%\) of the batteries have a lifespan between \(820\) and \(920\) hours.
workingLatex: Since about \(60\%\) are below \(860\), about \(10\%\) lie between the mean \(840\) and \(860\).
By symmetry, about \(10\%\) lie between \(820\) and \(840\).
About \(68\%\) lie within one standard deviation, from \(760\) to \(920\), so about \(34\%\) lie between \(840\) and \(920\).
Therefore the required percentage is \(10\%+34\%=44\%\).

betterResponses: subtract 50% from 60% to find the percentage of batteries within the given lifespan below the mean and add this to the 34% of batteries within the given lifespan above the mean to find the approximate percentage of batteries within the given lifespan

questions.title: Exponential product curve
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)=xe^{-2x}\). It is given that \(f'(x)=e^{-2x}-2xe^{-2x}\). (a) Show that \(f''(x)=4(x-1)e^{-2x}\). (b) Find the stationary point on the graph of \(y=f(x)\) and determine its nature. (c) Sketch the curve \(y=f(x)\), showing the stationary point, the point of inflection and any intercepts.
questions.answerLatex: \(f''(x)=4(x-1)e^{-2x}\). The stationary point is \(\left(\frac12,\frac{1}{2e}\right)\), and it is a local maximum. The curve passes through \((0,0)\), has a point of inflection at \((1,e^{-2})\), and approaches the \(x\)-axis as \(x\to\infty\).
workingLatex: For a stationary point, \(f'(x)=0\Rightarrow 1-2x=0\Rightarrow x=\frac12\), giving \(y=\frac{1}{2e}\).
\(f''\left(\frac12\right)=4\left(-\frac12\right)e^{-1}<0\), so the stationary point is a local maximum.
The point of inflection occurs when \(f''(x)=0\), so \(x=1\) and \(y=e^{-2}\).

questions.title: Circle and hyperbola area
questions.promptLatex: The circle \(x^2+y^2=2\) has centre \(O\). The interval from \(O\) to the point \((1,1)\) makes an angle \(\theta\) with the positive \(x\)-axis. (a) Find the exact area of the shaded circular segment shown. A hyperbola with equation \(y=\frac{a}{b-x}-1\) passes through \((0,0)\) and \((1,1)\), with part of the region bounded by the hyperbola and circle shaded. (b) Show that \(a=b=2\). (c) Find the exact area of the shaded region bounded by the hyperbola and the circle.
questions.answerLatex: The circular segment area is \(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac12\). The hyperbola parameters are \(a=b=2\). The final shaded area is \(2\ln 2+\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac32\).
workingLatex: The sector has radius \(\sqrt2\) and angle \(\frac{\pi}{4}\), so its area is \(\frac12(\sqrt2)^2\cdot\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{4}\).
The right triangle under the radius to \((1,1)\) has area \(\frac12\), giving segment area \(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac12\).
Substituting \((0,0)\) gives \(0=\frac{a}{b}-1\), so \(a=b\). Substituting \((1,1)\) then gives \(1=\frac{b}{b-1}-1\), so \(b=2\) and \(a=2\).
The area under the hyperbola from \(0\) to \(1\) is \(\int_0^1\left(\frac{2}{2-x}-1\right)dx=2\ln2-1\).
Adding the circular segment gives \(2\ln2-1+\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac12=2\ln2+\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac32\).

questions.title: Exponential rectangle series
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=2^{-x}\) is shown with the first five rectangles of width \(1\), where the heights are the values of \(2^{-x}\) at non-negative integers. (a) Show that, in the limit, the sum of the areas of all such rectangles is \(2\). (b) Show that \(\int_0^4 2^{-x}\,dx=\frac{15}{16\ln2}\). (c) Use parts (a) and (b) to show that \(e^{15}<2^{32}\).
questions.answerLatex: The limiting rectangle sum is \(2\). Also \(\int_0^4 2^{-x}\,dx=\frac{15}{16\ln2}\), and comparing this integral with the rectangle sum gives \(e^{15}<2^{32}\).
workingLatex: The rectangle areas form the geometric series \(1+\frac12+\frac14+\cdots\).
The limiting sum is \(\frac{1}{1-\frac12}=2\).
The integral from \(0\) to \(4\) is less than the limiting rectangle sum, so \(\frac{15}{16\ln2}<2\).
Thus \(15<32\ln2\), and exponentiating gives \(e^{15}<2^{32}\).

questions.title: Minimum triangle area
questions.promptLatex: A straight line passes through the point \(P(1,2)\) and intersects the coordinate axes at \(X(x,0)\) and \(Y(0,y)\), where \(x>1\). (a) Show that \(y=\frac{2x}{x-1}\). (b) Find the minimum possible area of triangle \(XOY\).
questions.answerLatex: \(y=\frac{2x}{x-1}\), and the minimum possible area of triangle \(XOY\) is \(4\) square units.
workingLatex: Using the intercept form of the line, \(\frac{1}{x}+\frac{2}{y}=1\).
Solving gives \(y=\frac{2x}{x-1}\).
The area is \(A=\frac12xy=\frac{x^2}{x-1}\).
\(A'(x)=\frac{x(x-2)}{(x-1)^2}\), so for \(x>1\) the stationary point occurs at \(x=2\).
This gives \(y=4\) and \(A=\frac12(2)(4)=4\) square units.

questions.title: Reducing balance loan repayments
questions.promptLatex: The amount owing \(A_n\) on a reducing-balance loan after \(n\) monthly repayments of \(M\), with monthly interest rate \(0.25\%\), is modelled by \(A_n=P(1.0025)^n-M\left(1+1.0025+\cdots+(1.0025)^{n-1}\right)\). Jane borrows \(\$200\,000\) and plans to repay the loan in \(180\) equal monthly repayments. (a) Show that the monthly repayment is \(\$1381.16\), to the nearest cent. After \(100\) repayments, the interest rate changes to \(0.35\%\) per month and Jane keeps making monthly repayments of \(\$1381.16\). (b) Find how many more full monthly repayments of \(\$1381.16\) Jane will make. (c) Find Jane's final repayment, which is less than \(\$1381.16\).
questions.answerLatex: The monthly repayment is \(\$1381.16\). After the rate change, Jane makes \(83\) more full repayments of \(\$1381.16\), followed by a final repayment of approximately \(\$931.54\).
workingLatex: After \(100\) repayments, the amount owing is approximately \(\$100032\).
This gives \(n\approx83.67\), so Jane makes \(83\) more full monthly repayments.
The balance after \(83\) of these repayments is about \(\$928.29\); after one further month of interest, the final repayment is \(928.29(1.0035)\approx\$931.54\).

(a(i)) \(7\underset{\sim}{i} + 2\underset{\sim}{j}\)
(a(ii)) 1
(b) \(\sqrt{5} - 2\)
(c) Coefficient of \(x^2\) is 7, coefficient of \(x^3\) is -7
(d) a = \(-2  or  1\)
(e) \(\sqrt{3}\sin(x) - 3\cos(x) = 2\sqrt{3}\sin\left(x - \frac{\pi}{3}\right)\)

(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct answer

(b) \[ \int_{0}^{1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2+4}} dx \quad u = x^2+4, \quad du = 2x dx \] \[ = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{2x}{2\sqrt{x^2+4}} dx, \quad when  x=0, u=4; when  x=1, u=5 \] \[ = \int_{4}^{5} \frac{1}{2\sqrt{u}} du = [\sqrt{u}]_{4}^{5} = \sqrt{5}-2 \]

And coefficient of \(x^3\) is -7.
(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains a correct expression for one coefficient, or equivalent merit

(d) \[ \underset{\sim}{u} = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \underset{\sim}{v} = \begin{pmatrix} a-7 \\ 4a-1 \end{pmatrix} \] \[ \underset{\sim}{u}  and  \underset{\sim}{v}  are perpendicular  \therefore \underset{\sim}{u} \cdot \underset{\sim}{v} = 0 \] \[ a(a-7) + 2(4a-1) = 0 \] \[ a^2 - 7a + 8a - 2 = 0 \] \[ a^2 + a - 2 = 0 \] \[ (a+2)(a-1) = 0 \] \[ \therefore a = -2  or  1 \]
(d) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Observes that \(\underset{\sim}{u} \cdot \underset{\sim}{v} = 0\), or equivalent merit

(e) \[\begin{aligned} \sqrt{3}\sin(x) - 3\cos(x) &= R\sin(x + \alpha) \\ &= R\sin x \cos \alpha + R\cos x \sin \alpha \\ \therefore R\sin \alpha &= -3 \quad (1) \\ R\cos \alpha &= \sqrt{3} \quad (2) \\ (1) \div (2): \tan \alpha &= \frac{-3}{\sqrt{3}} = -\sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \alpha &= -\frac{\pi}{3}  or  \frac{2\pi}{3}  (See unit circle) \\ Since  \cos \alpha > 0  and  \sin \alpha < 0 \\ \alpha &= -\frac{\pi}{3} \\ From (1)  R^2 \sin^2 \alpha &= 9 \quad (3) \\ From (2)  R^2 \cos^2 \alpha &= 3 \quad (4) \\ (3) + (4): R^2(\sin^2 \alpha + \cos^2 \alpha) &= 12 \\ R^2 &= 12 \\ R &= 2\sqrt{3} \\ \therefore \sqrt{3}\sin(x) - 3\cos(x) &= 2\sqrt{3}\sin\left(x - \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \end{aligned}\]

(b) A sports association manages 13 junior teams. It decides to check the age of all players. Any team that has more than 3 players above the age limit will be penalised. A total of 41 players are found to be above the age limit. Will any team be penalised? Justify your answer.

(di) In a room with temperature \(12^{\circ}\text{C}\), coffee is poured into a cup. The temperature of the coffee when it is poured into the cup is \(92^{\circ}\text{C}\), and it is far too hot to drink. The temperature, \(T\), in degrees Celsius, of the coffee, \(t\) minutes after it is made, can be modelled using the differential equation \(\frac{dT}{dt} = k(T - T_1)\), where \(k\) is the constant of proportionality and \(T_1\) is a constant. It takes 5 minutes for the coffee to cool to a temperature of \(76^{\circ}\text{C}\). Using separation of variables, solve the given differential equation to show that \(T = 12 + 80e^{\frac{t}{5}\ln(\frac{4}{5})}\).

(dii) The optimal drinking temperature for a hot beverage is \(57^{\circ}\text{C}\). Find the value of \(t\) when the coffee reaches this temperature, giving your answer to the nearest minute.

(e) A game consists of randomly selecting 4 balls from a bag. After each ball is selected it is replaced in the bag. The bag contains 3 red balls and 7 green balls. For each red ball selected, 10 points are earned and for each green ball selected, 5 points are deducted. For instance, if a player picks 3 red balls and 1 green ball, the score will be \(3 \times 10 - 1 \times 5 = 25\) points. What is the expected score in the game?

(f) Use mathematical induction to prove that \(15^n + 6^{2n+1}\) is divisible by 7 for all integers \(n \ge 0\).

(a) A direction field is to be drawn for the differential equation \[ \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{x - 2y}{x^2 + y^2}. \] Clearly draw the correct slopes of the direction field at the points \(P\), \(Q\) and \(R\) shown below.
questions.answerLatex:

(a) At \(P(-1, 1)\), \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-1-2}{1+1} = -\frac{3}{2} = -0.75\). At \(Q(1, 1)\), \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{1-2}{1+1} = -\frac{1}{2} = -0.5\). At \(R(2, 1)\), \(\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{2-2}{4+1} = 0\).
(b) \(\frac{41}{13} > 3\). By the Pigeonhole principle, at least one team has more than 3 players above limit. At least one team will be penalised.
(c) \[ y = \left( \frac{\pi + 2}{4} \right) x - \frac{1}{2} \]
(d(i)) T = \(12 + 80e^{\frac{1}{5}\ln(\frac{4}{5})t}\)
(d(ii)) \(t \approx 13  minutes\)
(e) The expected score is \(-2\).
(f) Let \(a_n = 15^n + 6^{2n+1}\). Base case: \(n=0\), \(a_0 = 1 + 6 = 7\), which is divisible by 7. Assume \(a_k = 15^k + 6^{2k+1} = 7M\). Then \(a_{k+1} = 15^{k+1} + 6^{2k+3} = 15(7M - 6^{2k+1}) + 6^2 \cdot 6^{2k+1} = 105M - 15 \cdot 6^{2k+1} + 36 \cdot 6^{2k+1} = 105M + 21 \cdot 6^{2k+1} = 7(15M + 3 \cdot 6^{2k+1}) = 7Q\).

(b) Number of players above limit / Number of teams = \(\frac{41}{13} > 3\). \(\therefore\) Using the Pigeonhole principle, at least one team has more than 3 players above limit. At least one team will be penalised.
(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Attempts to use the pigeonhole principle, or equivalent merit
(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the slope of the tangent, or equivalent merit; Attempts to find the derivative of \( x \arctan x \), or equivalent merit

(d(i)) \[ \frac{dT}{dt} = k(T - T_1) \] Note that as \(t \to +\infty\), \(\frac{dT}{dt} \to 0\) and \(T\) approaches room temperature so \(T_1 = room temperature = 12\). Also \(T\) decreases with time, so \(T - T_1 > 0\). \[ \frac{dT}{T - 12} = kdt \] (separate variables) \[ \ln(T - 12) = kt + c \] where \(c\) is a constant \[ T - 12 = Ae^{kt} \] where \(A = e^c\) \[ T = 12 + Ae^{kt} \] When \(t = 0\): \(92 = 12 + A\) so \(A = 80\). When \(t = 5\): \(76 = 12 + 80e^{5k}\) \[ e^{5k} = \frac{4}{5} \] \[ 5k = \ln\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \] \[ k = \frac{1}{5}\ln\left(\frac{4}{5}\right) \] Finally \(T = 12 + 80e^{\frac{1}{5}\ln(\frac{4}{5})t}\) for \(t \ge 0\).

(d(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Integrates to obtain T as a function involving an exponential function, or equivalent merit; Separates the variables in the differential equation, or equivalent merit

(d(ii)) For \(T = 57\)
\[57 = 12 + 80e^{\frac{1}{5}\ln(\frac{4}{5})t}\]
\[e^{\frac{1}{5}\ln(\frac{4}{5})t} = \frac{57 - 12}{80} = \frac{9}{16}\]
\[\frac{1}{5}\ln(\frac{4}{5})t = \ln(\frac{9}{16})\]
\[t = \frac{5\ln(\frac{9}{16})}{\ln(\frac{4}{5})} = 12.89... \approx 13  minutes\]
The coffee will reach a temperature of \(57^\circC\) after about 13 minutes.
(d(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution

(e) Let \(p\) be the proportion of red balls and \(n\) the number of trials. Expected number of red balls \(= np = 4 \times \frac{3}{10}\). Expected number of green balls \(= n(1-p) = 4 \times \frac{7}{10}\). Expected score \(= 10 \times 4 \times \frac{3}{10} - 5 \times 4 \times \frac{7}{10} = 12 - 14 = -2\). Alternatively: The expected score if you pick one ball is \(\frac{3 \times 10 + 7 \times (-5)}{10} = -0.5\). It is the same for every one of the 4 balls since the balls are replaced so the expected score for the game is \(4 \times (-0.5) = -2\).
(e) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Attempts to use result associated with the binomial distribution, or equivalent merit

(f) METHOD 1: Let \(a_n = 15^n + 6^{2n+1}\). Base case: \(n = 0\), \(a_0 = 1 + 6 = 7\) is divisible by 7, so the property holds for \(n = 0\). Assume \(a_k\) is divisible by 7, \(a_k = 15^k + 6^{2k+1} = 7M\), for some integer \(M\). Prove true for \(a_{k+1}\). \(a_{k+1} = 15^{k+1} + 6^{2k+3} = 7Q\), for some integer \(Q\). \(LHS = 15(15^k) + 6^{2k+3} = 15(7M - 6^{2k+1}) + 6^2 \times 6^{2k+1} = 105M - 15 \times 6^{2k+1} + 36 \times 6^{2k+1} = 105M + 21 \times 6^{2k+1} = 7(15M + 3 \times 6^{2k+1}) = 7Q = RHS\). This proves, using mathematical induction, that for all integers \(n \ge 0\), \(15^n + 6^{2n+1}\) is divisible by 7.
(f) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Proves the inductive step, or equivalent merit; Establishes the base case, or equivalent merit

(c) The function \(f\) is defined by \(f(x) = \sin(x)\) for all real numbers \(x\). Let \(g\) be the function defined on \([-1, 1]\) by \(g(x) = \arcsin(x)\). Is \(g\) the inverse of \(f\)? Justify your answer.

(d) The monic polynomial, \(P\), has degree 3 and roots \(\alpha, \beta, \gamma\). It is given that 
\[\alpha^2 + \beta^2 + \gamma^2 = 85 \text{ and}\]
\[P'(\alpha) + P'(\beta) + P'(\gamma) = 87.\]
Find \(\alpha\beta + \beta\gamma + \gamma\alpha\).

(e) You may use the information on page 18 to answer this question. A chocolate factory sells 150-gram chocolate bars. There has been a complaint that the bars actually weigh less than 150 grams, so a team of inspectors was sent to the factory to check. They randomly selected 16 bars, weighed them and noted that 8 bars weighed less than 150 grams. The factory manager claims 80% of the chocolate bars produced by the factory weigh 150 grams or more.

(ei) The inspectors used the normal approximation to the binomial distribution to calculate the probability, \(P\), of having at least 8 bars weighing less than 150 grams in a random sample of 16, assuming the factory manager's claim is correct. Calculate the value of \(P\).

(eii) The factory manager disagrees with the method used by the inspectors as described in part (i). Explain why the method used by the inspectors might not be valid.
questions.answerLatex:

(a) We have \(\overline{BH} = \underline{h} - \underline{b} = \underline{a} + \underline{c}\), while \(\overline{CA} = \underline{a} - \underline{c}\). Hence \(\overline{BH} \cdot \overline{CA} = (\underline{a} + \underline{c}) \cdot (\underline{a} - \underline{c}) = \underline{a} \cdot \underline{a} - \underline{a} \cdot \underline{c} + \underline{c} \cdot \underline{a} - \underline{c} \cdot \underline{c} = |\underline{a}|^2 - |\underline{c}|^2 = 0\). Thus, the vectors are perpendicular.

(d) \(\alpha\beta + \beta\gamma + \gamma\alpha = -2\)
(e(i)) \(P \approx 0.0015\) or \(P \approx 0.0013\)

\[= \underline{a} \cdot \underline{a} - \underline{a} \cdot \underline{c} + \underline{c} \cdot \underline{a} - \underline{c} \cdot \underline{c}\]
\[= |\underline{a}|^2 - |\underline{c}|^2\]
\[= 0 \quad (as the lengths of  \underline{a}  and  \underline{c}  are equal, as they are radii of a circle)\]
Hence, the two vectors are perpendicular.

(a) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Attempts to find the dot product of \(\overline{BH}\) and \(\overline{CA}\) in terms of \(\underline{a}\), \(\underline{b}\) and \(\underline{c}\), or equivalent merit; Write \(\overline{BH}\) or \(\overline{CA}\) in terms of \(\underline{a}\), \(\underline{b}\) and \(\underline{c}\), or equivalent merit

(b) V = \(\pi \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2k}} (k+1)^2 \sin^2(kx) \, dx = \frac{\pi(k+1)^2}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2k}} (1 - \cos 2kx) \, dx = \frac{\pi(k+1)^2}{2} \left[ x - \frac{\sin 2kx}{2k} \right]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2k}} = \frac{\pi^2(k+1)^2}{4k}.  For  V = \pi^2, \frac{(k+1)^2}{4k} = 1 \implies k^2+2k+1=4k \implies (k-1)^2=0 \implies k=1.\)

(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains primitive in terms of \(\sin(2kx)\), or equivalent merit; Finds an integral expression for the volume of the solid in terms of \(\sin(kx)\), or equivalent merit

(c) The domain of \(g(f(x))\) is \(\mathbb{R}\). \(g(f(x)) = \arcsin(\sin x)\). If \(g\) is the inverse function of \(f\), then \(g(f(x))\) is equal to \(x\) for all \(x\) in the domain of \(g(f(x))\) by definition of an inverse function. Let \(x = \frac{3\pi}{4}\). \(\arcsin(\sin(\frac{3\pi}{4})) = \frac{\pi}{4}\). So for \(x = \frac{3\pi}{4}\), \(\arcsin(\sin x) \neq x\). So it is not true that \(g(f(x)) = x\) for all \(x\) in the domain \(g(f(x))\). So, \(g\) is NOT the inverse of \(f\).
(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds a value of \(x\) where \(g(f(x)) \neq x\)

(e(i)) Let \(\hat{p}\) be the proportion of chocolate bars which weigh less than 150 g. \(P = P(\hat{p} \ge 0.5)\). Approximate \(\hat{p}\) by a normal distribution with the same mean as \(\hat{p}\) and the same standard deviation as \(\hat{p}\). \(E(\hat{p}) = 0.2\), \(\sigma(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.2 \times 0.8}{16}} = 0.1\). \(P = P\left(\frac{\hat{p}-0.2}{0.1} \ge \frac{0.5-0.2}{0.1}\right) \approx P(Z \ge 3)\). So \(P = P(Z \ge 3) = 0.15\% = 0.0015\). Alternatively, using the table of values for the normal distribution, we get the more precise value of \(P = 0.13\% = 0.0013\).

questions.title: Q14 (a) Find the particular solution to the differential equation (x - 2)dydx = xy that pa
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find the particular solution to the differential equation \((x - 2)\frac{dy}{dx} = xy\) that passes through the point \((0, 1)\).

(ab) Question 14 (15 marks) Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet

(b) The vectors \(\vec{u}\) and \(\vec{v}\) are not parallel. The vector \(\vec{p}\) is the projection of \(\vec{u}\) onto the vector \(\vec{v}\). The vector \(\vec{p}\) is parallel to \(\vec{v}\) so it can be written \(\lambda_0\vec{v}\) for some real number \(\lambda_0\). (Do NOT prove this.) Prove that \(|\vec{u} - \lambda\vec{v}|\) is smallest when \(\lambda = \lambda_0\) by showing that, for all real numbers \(\lambda\), \(|\vec{u} - \lambda_0\vec{v}| \le |\vec{u} - \lambda\vec{v}|\).

(c) A video game designer wants to include an obstacle in the game they are developing. The player will reach one side of a pit and must shoot a projectile to hit a target on the other side of the pit in order to be able to cross. However, the instant the player shoots, the target begins to move away from the player at a constant speed that is half the initial speed of the projectile shot by the player, as shown in the diagram below.

The initial distance between the player and the target is \(d\), the initial speed of the projectile is \(2u\) and it is launched at an angle of \(\theta\) to the horizontal. The acceleration due to gravity is \(g\). The launch angle is the ONLY parameter that the player can change.

Taking the position of the player when the projectile is launched as the origin, the positions of the projectile and target at time \(t\) after the projectile is launched are as follows.

\[ \vec{r}_P = \begin{pmatrix} 2ut \cos \theta \\ 2ut \sin \theta - \frac{g}{2}t^2 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{Projectile} \]

\[ \vec{r}_T = \begin{pmatrix} d + ut \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \quad \text{Target} \]

(Do NOT prove these.)

Show that, for the player to have a chance of hitting the target, \(d\) must be less than 37% of the maximum possible range of the projectile (to 2 significant figures).

(d) You may use the information on page 18 to answer this question. An airline company that has empty seats on a flight is not maximising its profit. An airline company has found that there is a probability of \(5\%\) that a passenger books a flight but misses it. The management of the airline company decides to allow for overbooking, which means selling more tickets than the number of seats available on each flight. To protect their reputation, management makes the decision that no more than \(1\%\) of their flights should have more passengers showing up for the flight than available seats. Given management's decision and using a suitable approximation, find the maximum number of tickets that can be sold for a flight which has \(350\) seats.

Approximating \(X\) by the appropriate normal random variable \(Y\), without continuity correction, the condition \(P(X \le 350) \ge 0.99\) can be rewritten as \(P(Y \le 350) \ge 0.99\). This becomes \(P\left( \frac{Y - 0.95n}{\sqrt{n \times 0.95 \times 0.05}} < \frac{350 - 0.95n}{\sqrt{n \times 0.95 \times 0.05}} \right) = 0.99\). This happens if \(\frac{350 - 0.95n}{\sqrt{0.95 \times 0.05n}} \approx 2.33\). Solving \(0.95x^2 + 2.33\sqrt{0.95 \times 0.05}x - 350 = 0\) where \(x = \sqrt{n}\).

D. If there exists a real number \(\lambda\) such that \(\vec{AP} \neq \lambda \vec{AB}\), then \(P\) is not on the line \(AB\).

questions.title: Q12 (a) For real numbers a, b 0 prove that a+b2 ab. (b) A particle is moving in a straight
questions.promptLatex:
(a) For real numbers \(a, b \ge 0\) prove that \(\frac{a+b}{2} \ge \sqrt{ab}\).

The particle experiences a resistive force of magnitude \(v + 3v^2\) newtons, where \(v\text{ m s}^{-1}\) is the speed of the particle after \(t\) seconds. The particle is never at rest.

(i) Show that \(\frac{dv}{dx} = -(1 + 3v)\).

(ii) Hence, or otherwise, find \(x\) as a function of \(v\).

(d) Using partial fractions, evaluate \(\int_{2}^{n} \frac{4+x}{(1-x)(4+x^2)} dx\), giving your answer in the form \(\frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{f(n)}{8(n-1)^2}\right)\), where \(f(n)\) is a function of \(n\).

(e) Given the complex number \(z = e^{i\theta}\), show that \(w = \frac{z^2 - 1}{z^2 + 1}\) is purely imaginary.

\[w = \frac{z^2 - 1}{z^2 + 1} = \frac{z(z - \frac{1}{z})}{z(z + \frac{1}{z})} = \frac{e^{i\theta} - e^{-i\theta}}{e^{i\theta} + e^{-i\theta}} = \frac{\cos\theta + i\sin\theta - \cos\theta + i\sin\theta}{\cos\theta + i\sin\theta + \cos\theta - i\sin\theta} = \frac{2i\sin\theta}{2\cos\theta} = i\tan\theta\] which is purely imaginary.

questions.title: Q13 (a) Prove that for all integers n with n 3, if 2^n - 1 is prime, then n cannot be even
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove that for all integers \(n\) with \(n \ge 3\), if \(2^n - 1\) is prime, then \(n\) cannot be even.

(b) The numbers \(a_n\), for integers \(n \ge 1\), are defined as
\[a_1 = \sqrt{2}\]
\[a_2 = \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2}}\]
\[a_3 = \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2 + \sqrt{2}}}\, \text{and so on.}\]
These numbers satisfy the relation \(a_{n+1}^2 = 2 + a_n\), for \(n \ge 1\). (Do NOT prove this.)
Use mathematical induction to prove that \(a_n = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{n+1}}\), for all integers \(n \ge 1\).

(ci) Consider the equation \(z^5 + 1 = 0\), where \(z\) is a complex number.
(i) Solve the equation \(z^5 + 1 = 0\) by finding the 5th roots of \(-1\).

(cii) (ii) Show that if \(z\) is a solution of \(z^5 + 1 = 0\) and \(z \neq -1\), then \(u = z + \frac{1}{z}\) is a solution of \(u^2 - u - 1 = 0\).

(ciii) (iii) Hence find the exact value of \(\cos\frac{3\pi}{5}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) It is equivalent to prove the contrapositive: 'if \(n\) is even, then \(2^n - 1\) is not prime'. Assume \(n\) is even. With \(n \ge 3\), that means \(n = 2k\) where \(k\) is an integer with \(k \ge 2\). \[2^n - 1 = 2^{2k} - 1 = (2^k)^2 - 1 = (2^k + 1)(2^k - 1)\] With \(k \ge 2\), \(2^k + 1 \ge 5\) and \(2^k - 1 \ge 3\), so \(2^n - 1\) has two proper factors so it is not prime.
(b) The proof proceeds by mathematical induction. Base case: \(n=1\), \(a_1 = \sqrt{2} = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{4}\). Inductive step: Assume \(a_k = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}}\). Then \(a_{k+1} = \sqrt{2+a_k} = \sqrt{2+2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}}} = \sqrt{2(1+\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}})} = \sqrt{4\cos^2\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}}} = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}}\).
(c(i)) \[ z = -1, e^{\pm i\frac{\pi}{5}}, e^{\pm i\frac{3\pi}{5}} \]
(c(ii)) u = \(z + \frac{1}{z}  is a solution of  u^2 - u - 1 = 0\)
(c(iii)) \(\cos\frac{3\pi}{5} = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{4}\)

(b) \[a_1 = \sqrt{2}, \quad a_2 = \sqrt{2+\sqrt{2}}, \quad a_3 = \sqrt{2+\sqrt{2+\sqrt{2}}} \dots \\ a_{n+1}^2 = 2+a_n, \quad n \ge 1 \\ Consider  n=1  case \\ RHS = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{4} = 2 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} = a_1 = LHS, so the result is true for  n=1 \\ Assume the result is true for  n=k. \\ Thus,  a_k = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}}, \quad k \ge 1 \\ Prove the result is true for  n=k+1 \\ a_{k+1}^2 = 2+a_k \\ = 2+2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}} \\ = 2\left[1+\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+1}}\right] \quad 1+\cos\theta = 2\cos^2\frac{\theta}{2} \\ = 2 \times 2\cos^2\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}} \\ = 4\cos^2\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}} \\ \therefore a_{k+1} = 2\cos\frac{\pi}{2^{k+2}} \quad (a_{k+1} > 0) \\ Thus if the result is true for  k, it is also true for  k+1. \\ Hence, using the principle of mathematical induction, the result is true for all  k \ge 1.\]

(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Proves the inductive step, or equivalent merit; Establishes the base case and makes some progress with the inductive step, or equivalent merit; Establishes base case, or equivalent merit

Since \(\cos\frac{3\pi}{5} < 0\) whereas \(\frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} > 0\), so \(2\cos\frac{3\pi}{5} = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2}\), therefore \(\cos\frac{3\pi}{5} = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{4}\).

questions.title: Q14 (ai) The two non-parallel vectors u and v satisfy u + v = 0 for some real numbers and
questions.promptLatex:
(ai) The two non-parallel vectors \(\vec{u}\) and \(\vec{v}\) satisfy \(\lambda\vec{u} + \mu\vec{v} = \vec{0}\) for some real numbers \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\). Show that \(\lambda = \mu = 0\).

(aii) The two non-parallel vectors \(\vec{u}\) and \(\vec{v}\) satisfy \(\lambda_1\vec{u} + \mu_1\vec{v} = \lambda_2\vec{u} + \mu_2\vec{v}\) for some real numbers \(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \mu_1\) and \(\mu_2\). Using part (i), or otherwise, show that \(\lambda_1 = \lambda_2\) and \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\).

(aiii) The diagram below shows the tetrahedron with vertices \(A, B, C\) and \(S\). The point \(K\) is defined by \(\vec{SK} = \frac{1}{4}\vec{SB} + \frac{1}{3}\vec{SC}\), as shown in the diagram. The point \(L\) is the point of intersection of the straight lines \(SK\) and \(BC\). Using part (ii), or otherwise, determine the position of \(L\) by showing that \(\vec{BL} = \frac{4}{7}\vec{BC}\).

(aiv) The point \(P\) is defined by \(\vec{AP} = -6\vec{AB} - 8\vec{AC}\). Does \(P\) lie on the line \(AL\)? Justify your answer.

(b) Let \(J_n = \int_0^1 x^n e^{-x} dx\), where \(n\) is a non-negative integer.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) Assume \(\lambda \vec{u} + \mu \vec{v} = \vec{0}\). If \(\lambda\) and \(\mu\) are both non-zero, then \(\vec{u} = -\frac{\mu}{\lambda} \vec{v}\) and \(\vec{v}\) are parallel. But \(\vec{u}, \vec{v}\) are not parallel. Hence, one of \(\lambda\) or \(\mu\) is zero. Assume the other one is not zero. Without loss of generality, assume \(\lambda = 0\) but \(\mu \neq 0\) then \(\mu \vec{v} = \vec{0}\). But \(\vec{v} \neq \vec{0}\) so \(\mu = 0\), a contradiction. Hence \(\lambda = \mu = 0\).
(a(ii)) If \(\lambda_1 \vec{u} + \mu_1 \vec{v} = \lambda_2 \vec{u} + \mu_2 \vec{v}\). Then \((\lambda_1 - \lambda_2)\vec{u} + (\mu_1 - \mu_2)\vec{v} = \vec{0}\). So \(\lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 0\) and \(\mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0\) (From part (i)). And hence \(\lambda_1 = \lambda_2\) and \(\mu_1 = \mu_2\).
(a(iii)) \(\vec{BL} = \frac{4}{7}\vec{BC}\)
(a(iv)) Write both \(\vec{AP}\) and \(\vec{AL}\) in terms of \(\vec{AB}\) and \(\vec{AC}\). \(\vec{AL} = \vec{AB} + \vec{BL} = \vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7}\vec{BC} = \vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7}(\vec{BA} + \vec{AC}) = \frac{3}{7}\vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7}\vec{AC}\). \(\vec{AP} = -6\vec{AB} - 8\vec{AC} = -14(\frac{3}{7}\vec{AB} + \frac{4}{7}\vec{AC}) = -14\vec{AL}\). Since \(\vec{AP} = -14\vec{AL}\), \(\vec{AP}\) and \(\vec{AL}\) are parallel, and \(A\) is common to those vectors, so \(P\) lies on the line \(AL\).
(b(i)) \[ J_0 = 1 - \frac{1}{e} \]
(b(ii)) \[ J_n \leq \frac{1}{n+1} \]
(b(iii)) \[ J_n = -\frac{1}{e} + nJ_{n-1} \]
(b(iv)) The proof proceeds by induction. For \(n=0\), \(RHS = 0! - \frac{0!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^{0} \frac{1}{r!} = 1 - \frac{1}{e} = J_0 = LHS\). Assuming the property holds for \(k\), use the recurrence relation from part (iii) to show it holds for \(k+1\).
(b(v)) \[ \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( \sum_{r=0}^{n} \frac{1}{r!} \right) = e \]

(b(ii)) \[ J_n = \int_0^1 x^n e^{-x} dx \leq \int_0^1 x^n dx \quad since  x^n e^{-x} \leq x^n  for  [0, 1] \] \[ = \left[ \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1} \right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{n+1} \] \[ \therefore J_n \leq \frac{1}{n+1} \]
(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; States that \( e^{-x} \leq 1 \) for \( 0 \leq x \leq 1 \), or equivalent merit
(b(iii)) \[ J_n = \int_0^1 x^n e^{-x} dx = \left[ x^n(-e^{-x}) \right]_0^1 - \int_0^1 nx^{n-1}(-e^{-x}) dx = -\frac{1}{e} + nJ_{n-1} \]
(b(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Attempts to apply integration by parts, correctly dealing with the limits of integration, or equivalent merit

(b(iv)) \[ J_n = -\frac{1}{e} + n \times J_{n-1} \quad n \geq 0 \]
For \(n=0\):
\[ RHS = 0! - \frac{0!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^{0} \frac{1}{r!} = 1 - \frac{1}{e} = J_0 = LHS \]
Assume the property holds for \(k\), so \(J_k = k! - \frac{k!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^{k} \frac{1}{r!}\).
By part (iii):
\[ J_{k+1} = (k+1)J_k - \frac{1}{e} \]
\[ = (k+1)\left( k! - \frac{k!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^{k} \frac{1}{r!} \right) - \frac{1}{e} \]
\[ = (k+1)! - \left( \frac{(k+1)!}{e} \sum_{r=0}^{k} \frac{1}{r!} \right) - \frac{1}{e} \frac{(k+1)!}{(k+1)!} \]
\[ = (k+1)! - \frac{(k+1)!}{e} \left( \sum_{r=0}^{k} \frac{1}{r!} + \frac{1}{(k+1)!} \right) \]
\[ = (k+1)! - \frac{(k+1)!}{e} \left( \sum_{r=0}^{k+1} \frac{1}{r!} \right) \]
Thus, the result is true for \(n = k+1\), if true for \(n = k\). Thus, using the principle of mathematical induction, the result is proven.
(b(iv)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Uses part (iii) to write \(J_{k+1}\) in terms of \(J_k\), or equivalent merit

(b(v)) From part (ii) \( 0 \le J_n \le \frac{1}{n+1} \). But \( \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n+1} = 0 \), so \( \lim_{n \to \infty} J_n = 0 \). From part (iv) \( \frac{J_n}{n!} = 1 - \frac{1}{e} \sum_{r=0}^{n} \frac{1}{r!} \). So \( \sum_{r=0}^{n} \frac{1}{r!} = e - \frac{e J_n}{n!} \). \( \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( \sum_{r=0}^{n} \frac{1}{r!} \right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left( e - \frac{e J_n}{n!} \right) = e - 0 = e \)
(b(v)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution

questions.title: Q15 (a) A machine is lifted from the floor of a room using two ropes. The two ropes ensure
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A machine is lifted from the floor of a room using two ropes. The two ropes ensure that the horizontal components of the forces are balanced at all times. It is assumed that at all times the machine moves vertically upwards at a constant velocity.

The machine is located in a room with height \(h\) metres.

One of the ropes is attached to the point \(P\) on the machine and to the fixed point \(C\) on the ceiling of the room. The point \(C\) is a distance \(d\) metres to the left of \(P\). Let the vertical distance from \(P\) to the ceiling be \(\ell\) metres and let \(\theta\) be the angle this rope makes with the horizontal.

The other rope is attached to the point \(P\) and to the fixed point \(F\) on the floor of the room. The point \(F\) is a distance \(2d\) metres to the right of \(P\). Let \(\phi\) be the angle this rope makes with the horizontal.

Let the tension in the first rope be \(T_1\) newtons, the tension in the second rope be \(T_2\) newtons, the mass of the machine be \(M\) kilograms and the acceleration due to gravity be \(g \text{ m s}^{-2}\).

(a(i)) By considering horizontal and vertical components of the forces at \(P\), show that \[\tan \theta = \tan \phi + \frac{Mg}{T_2 \cos \phi}\]

(a(ii)) Hence, or otherwise, show that the point \(P\) cannot be lifted to a position \(\frac{2h}{3}\) metres above the floor.

(b) The diagrams show two positions of a single piston in the cylinder chamber of a motorcycle. The piston moves vertically, in simple harmonic motion, between a maximum height of \(0.17\text{ metres}\) and a minimum height of \(0.05\text{ metres}\). The mass of the piston is \(0.8\text{ kg}\). The piston completes \(40\text{ cycles per second}\). What is the resultant force on the piston, in newtons, that produces the maximum acceleration of the piston? Give your answer correct to the nearest newton.

(c) Using the substitution \(x = \tan^2 \theta\), evaluate \[ \int_{0}^{1} \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{x}{1+x}} \, dx. \]

(d) The complex number \(z\) satisfies \(\left| z - \frac{4}{z} \right| = 2\). Using the triangle inequality, or otherwise, show that \(|z| \le \sqrt{5} + 1\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) \(\tan \theta = \frac{Mg}{T_2 \cos \phi} + \tan \phi\)
(a(ii)) \[ h - \ell < \frac{2h}{3} \]
(b) The maximum force on the piston is approximately \(3032  newtons\).
(c) \(\frac{\pi}{2} - 1\)
(d) By triangle inequality
\[ |z| \le \left| z - \frac{4}{z} \right| + \left| \frac{4}{z} \right| \]
\[ |z| \le 2 + \frac{4}{|z|} \]
\[ |z|^2 \le 2|z| + 4 \]
\[ |z|^2 - 2|z| - 4 \le 0 \]
\[ |z| \le \frac{2 + \sqrt{4 + 4 \times 4}}{2} \]
\[ = \frac{2 + \sqrt{20}}{2} \]
\[ = 1 + \sqrt{5} \]
Hence \( |z| \le 1 + \sqrt{5} \)

(a(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains equations for the forces in both the horizontal and vertical directions; Obtains an equation for the forces in either the horizontal or vertical direction

(a(ii)) \[ \tan\theta = \frac{Mg}{T_2\cos\phi} + \tan\phi \]
From the diagram
\[ \tan\theta = \frac{\ell}{d}  and  \tan\phi = \frac{h-\ell}{2d} \]
Therefore \(\frac{\ell}{d} = \frac{Mg}{T_2\cos\phi} + \frac{h-\ell}{2d}\)
\[ \frac{Mg}{T_2\cos\phi} > 0 \]
Thus
\[ \frac{\ell}{d} > \frac{h-\ell}{2d} \]
\[ \ell > \frac{h-\ell}{2} \]
\[ \frac{3\ell}{2} > \frac{h}{2} \]
\[ \ell > \frac{h}{3} \]
So \(h - \ell < \frac{2h}{3}\)
\(	herefore P\) cannot be lifted to a position \(\frac{2h}{3}\) metres above the floor.

(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Writes the equation from part (i) in terms of \(\ell\), \(d\) and \(h\) OR shows that \(\theta > \phi\) OR considers the case where \(P\) is \(\frac{2h}{3}\) OR equivalent merit

(b) \[Amplitude a = \frac{0.17 - 0.05}{2} = 0.06  m \\
Period of motion = \frac{1}{40} = 0.025  s \\
Equation of motion simple harmonic motion (SHM) is \\
\ddot{x} = -n^2(x - b) \\
n = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \frac{2\pi}{0.025} = 80\pi and \\
Centre of motion b = 0.11 \\
herefore \ddot{x} = -(80\pi)^2(x - 0.11) \\
Acceleration is maximum at an extremity, \\
\ddot{x}_{max} = -(80\pi)^2(0.05 - 0.11) = 384\pi^2  m s^{-2} \\
The maximum force on the piston f = m\ddot{x} = 0.8 \times 384\pi^2  newtons \approx 3032  newtons\]
(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains an equation of motion of the piston, or equivalent merit; Finds the period of the motion, or equivalent merit

(c) Let \(x = \tan^2 \theta\)
At \(x = 0\), \(\theta = 0\)
At \(x = 1\), \(\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}\)
\(dx = 2 \tan \theta \sec^2 \theta \, d\theta\)
\(\sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{x}{1+x}} = \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{\tan^2 \theta}{\sec^2 \theta}} = \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\sin^2 \theta} = \theta\) for \(0 \le \theta \le \frac{\pi}{4}\), as \(\sin \theta \ge 0\)
\[ \int_0^1 \sin^{-1} \sqrt{\frac{x}{1+x}} \, dx = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} 2\theta \tan \theta \sec^2 \theta \, d\theta \]
Integration by parts:
\[ = \left[ \theta \tan^2 \theta \right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} - \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \tan^2 \theta \, d\theta \]
\[ = \frac{\pi}{4} - \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} (-1 + \sec^2 \theta) \, d\theta \]
\[ = \frac{\pi}{4} - \left[ -\theta + \tan \theta \right]_0^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \]
\[ = \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{4} - 1 \]
\[ = \frac{\pi}{2} - 1 \]

(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Attempts to use integration by parts with the correct integral, or equivalent merit; Obtains integral in terms of \(\theta\), or equivalent merit; Attempts to use the given substitution, or equivalent merit

(d) By triangle inequality
\[ |z| \le \left| z - \frac{4}{z} \right| + \left| \frac{4}{z} \right| \]
\[ |z| \le 2 + \frac{4}{|z|} \]
\[ |z|^2 \le 2|z| + 4 \]
\[ |z|^2 - 2|z| - 4 \le 0 \]
\[ |z| \le \frac{2 + \sqrt{4 + 4 \times 4}}{2} \]
\[ = \frac{2 + \sqrt{20}}{2} \]
\[ = 1 + \sqrt{5} \]
Hence \( |z| \le 1 + \sqrt{5} \)

(d) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Uses the triangle inequality to obtain a relevant inequality involving only \( |z| \), or equivalent merit; Correctly uses the triangle inequality, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q24 Peta has the choice of investing \$7000 in two different investment funds. Fund A: 5.2
questions.promptLatex:
Peta has the choice of investing \(\$7000\) in two different investment funds.

Fund A: \(5.2\%\) per annum simple interest
Fund B: \(5\%\) per annum interest, compounded annually

What is the difference between the amounts of interest earned in the two investment funds over 3 years?
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
The difference is \(\$11.38\)
workingLatex:
\[Fund A: Interest = 7000 \times 5.2\% \times 3 = 7000 \times 0.052 \times 3 = 1092 \\
Fund B: Future value = 7000(1 + 5\%)^3 = 7000(1 + 0.05)^3 = 8103.38 \\
Interest on Fund B = 8103.38 - 7000 = 1103.38 \\
\therefore difference = 1103.38 - 1092 = \$11.38\]

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Calculates simple interest and future value correctly, or equivalent merit; Calculates simple interest and attempts to apply the future value formula, or equivalent merit; Calculates simple interest, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q34 A composite solid is shown. The top section is a cylinder with a height of 3 cm and a
questions.promptLatex: A composite solid is shown. The top section is a cylinder with a height of \(3\text{ cm}\) and a diameter of \(4\text{ cm}\). The bottom section is a hemisphere with a diameter of \(6\text{ cm}\). The cylinder is centred on the flat surface of the hemisphere. Find the total surface area of the composite solid in \(\text{cm}^2\), correct to 1 decimal place.
workingLatex:
Surface area of:

Sphere = \(4\pi r^2\) (diameter of 6 cm so radius of 3 cm)

Curved bottom of hemisphere = \(\frac{1}{2} \times 4\pi \times 3^2\)

Top of hemisphere = \(\pi \times 3^2 - (bottom of cylinder)\) = \(\pi \times 3^2 - \pi \times 2^2\)

Top of cylinder = \(\pi \times 2^2\)

Curved side of cylinder = \(2\pi \times 2 \times 3\)

Total surface area = \(\frac{1}{2} \times 4\pi \times 3^2 + \pi \times 3^2 - \pi \times 2^2 + \pi \times 2^2 + 2\pi \times 6 = 122.5  cm^2\)

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Calculates the curved area of the cylinder AND of the hemisphere; Calculates the curved area of the cylinder OR of the hemisphere; Calculates any relevant area

- Characters of age 15 is the largest group and they are played by actors with the widest age range (14–27).
- Characters older than 15 are all played by older actors. The youngest actors in the dataset are playing characters close to their own age.

Marking criteria: Provides a comprehensive description and interpretation of the data and other information, in the given context; Provides a sound description and interpretation of the data and other information, in the given context; Provides some description and interpretation of the data and/or other information; Provides some relevant information

 & (at start of month) & charged & repayment & (at end of month) \\

(b) After 50 months of repaying the loan, Frankie decides to make a lump sum payment of \(\$40\,000\) and to continue making the monthly repayments of \(\$1485\). The loan will then be fully repaid after a further 146 monthly repayments. How much less will Frankie pay overall by making the lump sum payment?
questions.answerLatex:

A = 199\,715 \times 0.006 = \$1198.29 \\

questions.title: Q37 The life span of batteries from a particular factory is normally distributed with a me
questions.promptLatex:
The life span of batteries from a particular factory is normally distributed with a mean of \(840\text{ hours}\) and a standard deviation of \(80\text{ hours}\).

It is known from statistical tables that for this distribution approximately \(60\%\) of the batteries have a life span of less than \(860\text{ hours}\).

What is the approximate percentage of batteries with a life span between \(820\) and \(920\text{ hours}\)?
workingLatex: \[60\%  have a life span  < 860. \\ \therefore 10\%  have a life span between  840  and  860. \\ \therefore 10\%  have a life span between  820  and  840. \\ 68\%  have a life span between  760  and  920. \\ \therefore 34\%  have a life span between  840  and  920. \\ \therefore percentage with life span between  820  and  920  hours is  10\% + 34\% = 44\%.\]

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Recognises that 34\% have a life span between 840 and 920 AND attempts to use the information given regarding the statistical tables; Recognises that 68\% have a life span between 760 and 920, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q38 A full container has 4.8 L of a mixture of cordial and water in the ratio 1:3. After r
questions.promptLatex: A full container has \(4.8\text{ L}\) of a mixture of cordial and water in the ratio \(1:3\). After removing \(1.2\text{ L}\) of the mixture, more water is added to refill the container. What is the ratio of cordial to water in the final mixture?
workingLatex: \[3.6 L in ratio 1:3 \\ \frac{3.6}{4}  L of cordial = 0.9  L cordial \\ \frac{3.6 \times 3}{4}  L of water = 2.7  L water \\ Add 1.2 L of water \\ New ratio = 0.9  L cordial : (2.7 + 1.2)  L water \\ = 0.9 : 3.9 \\ = 3 : 13  cordial to water.\]
Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Calculates the amount of cordial and of water in the mixture of 3.6 L; Attempts to apply the ratio 1:3 to the mixture of 3.6 L

questions.answerLatex: D. The scatterplot shows a strong positive linear association, best described by \(r\approx0.8\).

questions.title: Die and spinner score probability
questions.promptLatex: A game involves throwing a die and spinning a spinner labelled \(1,2,3,4\). The sum of the two numbers obtained is the score. What is the probability of getting a score of \(7\) or more? Options: A. \(\frac16\), B. \(\frac14\), C. \(\frac{5}{18}\), D. \(\frac{5}{12}\).
questions.answerLatex: D. There are \(6\times4=24\) equally likely outcomes. Scores of at least \(7\) occur in \(10\) outcomes, so the probability is \(\frac{10}{24}=\frac{5}{12}\).
workingLatex: There are \(24\) die-spinner outcomes.
For spinner values \(1,2,3,4\), the die values giving a score of at least \(7\) are \(6\), \(5,6\), \(4,5,6\), and \(3,4,5,6\).
This gives \(1+2+3+4=10\) favourable outcomes.

questions.title: Domain of a rational radical function
questions.promptLatex: What is the domain of \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}}\)? Options: A. \(x<1\), B. \(x\le1\), C. \(x>1\), D. \(x\ge1\).
questions.answerLatex: A. The denominator \(\sqrt{1-x}\) must be real and non-zero, so \(1-x>0\), giving \(x<1\).
workingLatex: Require \(1-x>0\) because the square root is in the denominator.
\(1-x>0\Rightarrow x<1\).

questions.title: Polynomial graph recognition
questions.promptLatex: The graph of a polynomial is shown. Which row of the table is correct for this polynomial? Options: A. \(y=-(x-b)(x-c)^2\), \(b>0\), \(c<0\); B. \(y=-(x-b)(x-c)^2\), \(b<0\), \(c>0\); C. \(y=-x(x-b)(x-c)\), \(b>0\), \(c<0\); D. \(y=-x(x-b)(x-c)\), \(b<0\), \(c>0\).
questions.answerLatex: B. The graph crosses the \(x\)-axis at a negative value and touches the \(x\)-axis at a positive value, with negative leading behaviour. This matches \(y=-(x-b)(x-c)^2\), \(b<0\), \(c>0\).
workingLatex: A simple root appears to the left of the origin, so \(b<0\).
A repeated root appears to the right of the origin, so \(c>0\).
The right-hand end goes down, consistent with the negative leading coefficient.

questions.title: Odd function integral and shaded area
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph \(y=f(x)\), where \(f(x)\) is an odd function. The shaded area is \(1\) square unit. The number \(a\), where \(a>1\), is chosen so that \(\int_0^a f(x)\,dx=0\). What is the value of \(\int_{-a}^{1}f(x)\,dx\)? Options: A. \(-1\), B. \(0\), C. \(1\), D. \(3\).
questions.answerLatex: A. Since \(f\) is odd and \(\int_0^a f(x)\,dx=0\), \(\int_{-a}^{0}f(x)\,dx=0\). The shaded region from \(0\) to \(1\) lies below the \(x\)-axis and has area \(1\), so \(\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx=-1\). Therefore \(\int_{-a}^{1}f(x)\,dx=-1\).
workingLatex: \(\int_{-a}^{0}f(x)\,dx=0\) because \(f\) is odd and \(\int_0^a f(x)\,dx=0\).
\(\int_0^1f(x)\,dx=-1\) from the shaded area below the axis.

questions.title: Sketch from derivative sign table
questions.promptLatex: The table gives the signs of the first and second derivatives of a function \(y=f(x)\) for different values of \(x\): \[\begin{array}{c|ccc}x&-2&0&2\\\hline f'(x)&+&0&+\\ f''(x)&-&0&+\end{array}\] Which of the given options is a possible sketch of \(y=f(x)\)?
questions.answerLatex: C. The function is increasing on both sides of \(x=0\), has \(f'(0)=0\), and changes concavity from down to up, so the sketch should show a stationary point of inflection at \(x=0\).
workingLatex: \(f'(x)>0\) at \(x=-2\) and \(x=2\), so the function is increasing on both sides.
\(f'(0)=0\) but the derivative sign does not change, suggesting a stationary point of inflection.
\(f''(x)\) changes from negative to positive, matching concavity down then concavity up.

questions.title: Chain rule for a composite function
questions.answerLatex: A. By the chain rule, \(y'=f'(g(x))g'(x)\). At \(x=5\), this is \(f'(1)\times2=-4\times2=-8\).

questions.title: Solving a logarithmic equation
questions.promptLatex: What is the solution of the equation \(\log_a x^3=b\), where \(a\) and \(b\) are positive constants? Options: A. \(x=b^{a/3}\), B. \(x=a^{b/3}\), C. \(x=\frac{ba}{3}\), D. \(x=\frac{a^b}{3}\).
workingLatex: \(\log_a x^3=b\Rightarrow x^3=a^b\).

questions.title: Even function from an arbitrary function
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)\) be any function with domain all real numbers. Which of the following is an even function, regardless of the choice of \(f(x)\)? Options: A. \(2f(x)\), B. \(f(f(x))\), C. \((f(-x))^2\), D. \(f(x)f(-x)\).
questions.answerLatex: D. If \(h(x)=f(x)f(-x)\), then \(h(-x)=f(-x)f(x)=h(x)\), so \(h\) is even for any function \(f\).
workingLatex: Let \(h(x)=f(x)f(-x)\).
\(h(-x)=f(-x)f(x)=h(x)\), so \(h\) is even.

questions.title: Parabola intersection length under scaling
questions.promptLatex: The graph \(y=x^2\) meets the line \(y=k\), where \(k>0\), at points \(P\) and \(Q\). The length of the interval \(PQ\) is \(L\). Let \(a\) be a positive number. The graph \(y=\frac{x^2}{a^2}\) meets the line \(y=k\) at points \(S\) and \(T\). What is the length of \(ST\)? Options: A. \(\frac{L}{a}\), B. \(\frac{L}{a^2}\), C. \(aL\), D. \(a^2L\).
questions.answerLatex: C. For \(y=x^2\), the intersections with \(y=k\) are \(x=\pm\sqrt{k}\), so \(L=2\sqrt{k}\). For \(y=\frac{x^2}{a^2}\), \(x=\pm a\sqrt{k}\), so \(ST=2a\sqrt{k}=aL\).
workingLatex: \(x^2=k\Rightarrow x=\pm\sqrt{k}\), so \(L=2\sqrt{k}\).
\(\frac{x^2}{a^2}=k\Rightarrow x=\pm a\sqrt{k}\).
\(ST=2a\sqrt{k}=aL\).

questions.title: Arithmetic sequence term
questions.promptLatex: The first three terms of an arithmetic sequence are \(3\), \(7\) and \(11\). Find the \(15\)th term.
questions.answerLatex: The common difference is \(d=7-3=4\). With first term \(a=3\), the \(15\)th term is \(T_{15}=a+(15-1)d=3+14\times4=59\).

questions.title: Tangent to a cubic composite
questions.promptLatex: Find the equation of the tangent to the curve \(y=(2x+1)^3\) at the point \((0,1)\).
questions.answerLatex: The derivative is \(y'=3(2x+1)^2\cdot2=6(2x+1)^2\). At \(x=0\), the gradient is \(6\). The tangent through \((0,1)\) is \(y-1=6(x-0)\), so \(y=6x+1\).
workingLatex: \(y-1=6(x-0)\Rightarrow y=6x+1\).

questions.title: Annuity future value factors
questions.promptLatex: A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) is shown. \[\begin{array}{c|cccc}\text{Period}\backslash\text{Rate}&1.5\%&3\%&4.5\%&6\%\\\hline5&5.152&5.309&5.471&5.637\\10&10.703&11.464&12.288&13.181\\20&23.124&26.870&31.371&36.786\\40&54.268&75.401&107.030&154.762\end{array}\] (a) Micky wants to save \(\$450\,000\) over the next \(10\) years. If the interest rate is \(6\%\) per annum compounding annually, how much should Micky contribute each year? Give your answer to the nearest dollar. (b) Instead, Micky decides to contribute \(\$8535\) every three months for \(10\) years to an annuity paying \(6\%\) per annum, compounding quarterly. How much will Micky have at the end of \(10\) years?
questions.answerLatex: For part (a), use the factor \(13.181\), giving \(450000\div13.181\approx\$34\,140\). For part (b), the quarterly rate is \(1.5\%\) and there are \(40\) periods, so use the factor \(54.268\). The amount is \(8535\times54.268=\$463\,177.38\).
workingLatex: For quarterly compounding, \(r=6\%\div4=1.5\%\).
There are \(10\times4=40\) quarterly periods.

questions.title: Rectangle and arc perimeter
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows a shape \(APQBCD\). The shape consists of a rectangle \(ABCD\), with an arc \(PQ\) on side \(AB\). The side lengths are \(BC=3.6\text{ m}\) and \(CD=8.0\text{ m}\). The arc \(PQ\) is an arc of a circle with centre \(O\), radius \(2.1\text{ m}\), and \(\angle POQ=110^\circ\). What is the perimeter of the shape \(APQBCD\)? Give your answer correct to one decimal place.
questions.answerLatex: The arc length is \(\frac{110}{360}\times2\pi(2.1)\approx4.0317\). The chord \(PQ\) has length \(\sqrt{2.1^2+2.1^2-2(2.1)(2.1)\cos110^\circ}\approx3.4404\). Hence the perimeter is \(2(3.6)+8.0+(8.0-3.4404)+4.0317\approx23.8\text{ m}\).
workingLatex: \(\text{arc }PQ=\frac{110}{360}\times2\pi\times2.1\approx4.0317\).
The straight top portions total \(AB-PQ=8.0-3.4404\).

betterResponses: interpret the question correctly to identify the main parts and evaluate the length of arc PQ and the length of line segment PQ
set their working out clearly and identify the parts they were calculating, and show their rounded answers to at least three decimal places to assist the calculation for the perimeter
identify the arc length was 110/360 of a circle and not a semi-circle
correctly substitute values into the correct formulas (the sine or cosine rule for line segment PQ and arc length formula for arc PQ) and show full substitution

improvementAreas: selecting and demonstrating appropriate substitutions into the required formula from the Reference Sheet
understanding that the question requires the calculation of the perimeter of the shape, therefore no area formulas were needed
using the square root when using the cosine rule
realising the arc PQ has a radius of 2.1 m and is not a semi-circle with interval PQ as the diameter

questions.title: Gas usage least-squares regression
questions.subtopic: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
questions.promptLatex: A university uses gas to heat its buildings. Over \(10\) weekdays, the average outside temperatures were \(0^\circ,0^\circ,0^\circ,2^\circ,5^\circ,7^\circ,8^\circ,9^\circ,9^\circ,10^\circ\), and the total gas usage for the ten weekdays was \(1840\text{ MW}\). The least-squares regression line predicts that when the temperature is \(0^\circ\text{C}\), the daily gas usage is \(236\text{ MW}\). In any bivariate dataset, the least-squares regression line passes through \((\bar{x},\bar{y})\). (a) Plot \((\bar{x},\bar{y})\) and the \(y\)-intercept of the least-squares regression line on the grid. (b) What is the equation of the regression line? (c) In context, identify one problem with using the regression line to predict gas usage when the average outside temperature is \(23^\circ\text{C}\).
questions.answerLatex: The means are \(\bar{x}=5\) and \(\bar{y}=184\), so the line passes through \((5,184)\) and has \(y\)-intercept \((0,236)\). The slope is \(\frac{184-236}{5}=-10.4\), so the regression equation is \(y=236-10.4x\). At \(x=23\), the model gives a negative gas usage, which is not physically possible.

\(x=23\) is outside the observed temperature range and the equation predicts negative usage.

questions.title: Shifted sine equation
questions.promptLatex: Find all values of \(\theta\), where \(0^\circ\le\theta\le360^\circ\), such that \(\sin(\theta-60^\circ)=-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\).
questions.answerLatex: Since \(-60^\circ\le\theta-60^\circ\le300^\circ\), the relevant solutions of \(\sin u=-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\) are \(u=-60^\circ\), \(240^\circ\), and \(300^\circ\). Therefore \(\theta=0^\circ\), \(300^\circ\), and \(360^\circ\).
workingLatex: Let \(u=\theta-60^\circ\).
Then \(-60^\circ\le u\le300^\circ\).
\(\sin u=-\frac{\sqrt3}{2}\) at \(u=-60^\circ,240^\circ,300^\circ\) in this interval.
\(\theta=u+60^\circ\), so \(\theta=0^\circ,300^\circ,360^\circ\).

questions.title: Geometric sequence common ratio
questions.promptLatex: The fourth term of a geometric sequence is \(48\). The eighth term of the same sequence is \(\frac{3}{16}\). Find the possible value(s) of the common ratio and the corresponding first term(s).
questions.answerLatex: Let the first term be \(a\) and the common ratio be \(r\). Then \(ar^3=48\) and \(ar^7=\frac{3}{16}\). Dividing gives \(r^4=\frac{1}{256}\), so \(r=\pm\frac14\). If \(r=\frac14\), then \(a=3072\). If \(r=-\frac14\), then \(a=-3072\).
workingLatex: \(r=\frac14\) or \(r=-\frac14\).
Substituting into \(ar^3=48\) gives \(a=3072\) or \(a=-3072\), respectively.

questions.title: Angle in a rectangular prism
questions.promptLatex: In the rectangular prism shown, \(AD=7\) cm, \(AE=8\) cm and \(EF=6\) cm. Point \(M\) is the midpoint of \(CD\). Find \(\angle AEM\), to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex: Since \(M\) is the midpoint of \(CD\), \(DM=3\) cm. In \(\triangle ADM\), \(AM^2=7^2+3^2=58\), so \(AM=\sqrt{58}\). In \(\triangle AEM\), \(\tan \angle AEM=\frac{\sqrt{58}}{8}\). Therefore \(\angle AEM\approx43.59^\circ\), which is \(44^\circ\) to the nearest degree.
workingLatex: \(\tan \angle AEM=\frac{AM}{AE}=\frac{\sqrt{58}}{8}\).
\(\angle AEM\approx43.59^\circ\approx44^\circ\).

questions.title: Koala weights normal distribution
questions.promptLatex: A random variable is normally distributed with mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\). The table gives \(P(Z<z)\) for positive values of \(z\): \[\begin{array}{c|cccccccccc}z&1.30&1.31&1.32&1.33&1.34&1.35&1.36&1.37&1.38&1.39\\\hline P(Z<z)&0.9032&0.9049&0.9066&0.9082&0.9099&0.9115&0.9131&0.9147&0.9162&0.9177\end{array}\] The weights of adult male koalas form a normal distribution with mean \(\mu=10.40\) kg and standard deviation \(\sigma=1.15\) kg. In a group of \(400\) adult male koalas, how many would be expected to weigh more than \(11.93\) kg?
questions.answerLatex: The standardised value is \(z=\frac{11.93-10.40}{1.15}=1.33\). From the table, \(P(Z<1.33)=0.9082\), so \(P(X>11.93)=1-0.9082=0.0918\). The expected number is \(400\times0.0918=36.72\), so about \(37\) koalas would be expected.
workingLatex: \(400\times0.0918=36.72\), so the expected number is approximately \(37\).

questions.title: Minimum area of a garden path
questions.promptLatex: A gardener wants to build a rectangular garden of area \(50\text{ m}^2\) against an existing wall. A concrete path of width \(1\) metre is to be built around the other three sides of the garden. Let \(x\) and \(y\) be the dimensions, in metres, of the outer rectangle as shown. (a) Show that \(y=\frac{50}{x-2}+1\). (b) Find the value of \(x\) such that the area of the concrete path is a minimum. Show that your answer gives a minimum area.
questions.answerLatex: The garden dimensions are \((x-2)\) by \((y-1)\), so \(50=(x-2)(y-1)\) and \(y=\frac{50}{x-2}+1\). The path area is \(A=xy-50=x\left(\frac{50}{x-2}+1\right)-50=\frac{100}{x-2}+x\). Then \(A'=1-\frac{100}{(x-2)^2}\). Setting \(A'=0\) gives \((x-2)^2=100\), so \(x=12\) for \(x>2\). Also \(A''=\frac{200}{(x-2)^3}>0\) at \(x=12\), so the area is minimised when \(x=12\).
workingLatex: \(A'=0\Rightarrow (x-2)^2=100\Rightarrow x=12\), since \(x>2\).
\(A''=\frac{200}{(x-2)^3}\), so \(A''(12)>0\) and the stationary point is a minimum.

questions.title: Swing motion distance model
questions.promptLatex: A camera films the motion of a swing in a park. Let \(x(t)\) be the horizontal distance, in metres, from the camera to the seat of the swing at \(t\) seconds. The seat is released from rest at a horizontal distance of \(11.2\) m from the camera. (a) The rate of change of \(x\) can be modelled by \(\frac{dx}{dt}=-1.5\pi\sin\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\). Find an expression for \(x(t)\). (b) How many times does the swing reach the closest point to the camera during the first \(10\) seconds?
questions.answerLatex: Integrating gives \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+C\). Since \(x(0)=11.2\), \(11.2=1.2+C\), so \(C=10\) and \(x(t)=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+10\). The period is \(\frac{2\pi}{5\pi/4}=1.6\) seconds. The closest positions occur at the minima, first at \(t=0.8\) and then every \(1.6\) seconds, so this happens \(6\) times in the first \(10\) seconds.
workingLatex: \(x(t)=\int -1.5\pi\sin\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\,dt\).
\(x(t)=\frac{-1.5\pi}{5\pi/4}\left[-\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)\right]+C=1.2\cos\left(\frac{5\pi t}{4}\right)+C\).
\(x(0)=11.2\Rightarrow 11.2=1.2+C\Rightarrow C=10\).
The closest point is reached at \(t=0.8,2.4,4.0,5.6,7.2,8.8\), so \(6\) times.

questions.title: Absolute value graph parameters
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=f(x)\), where \(f(x)=a|x-b|+c\), passes through the points \((3,-5)\), \((6,7)\) and \((9,-5)\) as shown in the diagram. (a) Find the values of \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\). (b) The line \(y=mx\) cuts the graph of \(y=f(x)\) in two distinct places. Find all possible values of \(m\).
questions.answerLatex: The vertex is \((6,7)\), so \(b=6\) and \(c=7\). Using \((3,-5)\), \(-5=a|3-6|+7=3a+7\), so \(a=-4\). For \(y=mx\) to cut the graph twice, its slope must be less than the slope from the origin to the vertex, \(\frac76\), and greater than the slope of the right branch, \(-4\). Hence \(-4<m<\frac76\).
workingLatex: The graph has vertex \((6,7)\), so \(b=6\) and \(c=7\).
\(-5=a|3-6|+7\Rightarrow 3a=-12\Rightarrow a=-4\).
The boundary slope through the origin and vertex is \(\frac{7}{6}\).
The right branch has slope \(-4\).
Therefore the line cuts the graph twice for \(-4<m<\frac76\).

questions.title: Parallel tangent point on a curve
questions.promptLatex: The curve \(y=f(x)\) is shown on the diagram. The equation of the tangent to the curve at point \(T(-1,6)\) is \(y=x+7\). At a point \(R\), another tangent parallel to the tangent at \(T\) is drawn. The gradient function of the curve is \(\frac{dy}{dx}=3x^2-6x-8\). Find the coordinates of \(R\).
questions.answerLatex: Parallel tangents have the same gradient, so solve \(3x^2-6x-8=1\). This gives \(3x^2-6x-9=0\), so \(x=-1\) or \(x=3\). The point \(R\) has \(x=3\). Since \(y=x^3-3x^2-8x+C\), and \(6=(-1)^3-3(-1)^2-8(-1)+C\), \(C=2\). Thus \(y(3)=27-27-24+2=-22\), so \(R=(3,-22)\).
workingLatex: The second tangent point is at \(x=3\).
\(6=-1-3+8+C\Rightarrow C=2\).

equate 𝑑𝑑𝑑𝑑 𝑑𝑑𝑑𝑑= 1 and solve the quadratic to find the 𝑥𝑥 coordinate of 𝑅𝑅

questions.title: Continuous random variable mode and CDF
questions.promptLatex: A continuous random variable \(X\) has probability density function \(f(x)=\begin{cases}12x^2(1-x),&0\le x\le1,\\0,&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}\) (a) Find the mode of \(X\). (b) Find the cumulative distribution function for the given probability density function. (c) Without calculating the median, show that the mode is greater than the median.
questions.answerLatex: For \(0\le x\le1\), \(f(x)=12x^2-12x^3\), so \(f'(x)=24x-36x^2=12x(2-3x)\). The interior maximum occurs at \(x=\frac23\), so the mode is \(\frac23\). The CDF is \(F(x)=0\) for \(x<0\), \(F(x)=\int_0^x12t^2(1-t)\,dt=4x^3-3x^4\) for \(0\le x\le1\), and \(F(x)=1\) for \(x>1\). Since \(F\left(\frac23\right)=4\left(\frac{8}{27}\right)-3\left(\frac{16}{81}\right)=\frac{16}{27}>\frac12\), the median is less than \(\frac23\), so the mode is greater than the median.
workingLatex: \(f'(x)=0\Rightarrow x=0\) or \(x=\frac23\); the mode is \(\frac23\).
\(F\left(\frac23\right)=\frac{16}{27}\approx0.593>0.5\).
More than half the probability lies below the mode, so the median is less than the mode.

questions.title: Stationary points of a damped sine curve
questions.promptLatex: Let \(f(x)=e^{-x}\sin x\). (a) Find the coordinates of the stationary points of \(f(x)\) for \(0\le x\le2\pi\). You do not need to check the nature of the stationary points. (b) Without using any further calculus, sketch the graph of \(y=f(x)\), for \(0\le x\le2\pi\), showing stationary points and intercepts.
questions.answerLatex: Using the product rule, \(f'(x)=e^{-x}\cos x-e^{-x}\sin x=e^{-x}(\cos x-\sin x)\). Since \(e^{-x}\ne0\), stationary points occur when \(\cos x=\sin x\), so \(x=\frac\pi4\) or \(x=\frac{5\pi}{4}\). The points are \(\left(\frac\pi4,\frac{e^{-\pi/4}}{\sqrt2}\right)\) and \(\left(\frac{5\pi}{4},-\frac{e^{-5\pi/4}}{\sqrt2}\right)\). Intercepts occur at \(x=0,\pi,2\pi\).
workingLatex: \(f'(x)=e^{-x}\cos x-e^{-x}\sin x=e^{-x}(\cos x-\sin x)\).
\(f'(x)=0\Rightarrow \cos x=\sin x\).
On \(0\le x\le2\pi\), \(x=\frac\pi4,\frac{5\pi}{4}\).
\(f\left(\frac\pi4\right)=\frac{e^{-\pi/4}}{\sqrt2}\).
\(f\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right)=-\frac{e^{-5\pi/4}}{\sqrt2}\).

questions.title: Conditional probability and availability
questions.promptLatex: Four Year 12 students want to organise a graduation party. All four students have the same probability \(P(F)\) of being available next Friday and the same probability \(P(S)\) of being available next Saturday. It is given that \(P(F)=\frac{3}{10}\), \(P(S\mid F)=\frac13\), and \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\). Kim is one of the four students. (a) Is Kim's availability next Friday independent from his availability next Saturday? Justify your answer. (b) Show that the probability that Kim is available next Saturday is \(\frac45\). (c) What is the probability that at least one of the four students is not available next Saturday?
questions.answerLatex: Kim's availability is not independent because \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\ne\frac{3}{10}=P(F)\). Also \(P(S\cap F)=P(S\mid F)P(F)=\frac13\times\frac{3}{10}=\frac{1}{10}\). Since \(P(F\mid S)=\frac{P(F\cap S)}{P(S)}=\frac18\), \(P(S)=\frac{4}{5}\). Therefore the probability that at least one of the four students is not available next Saturday is \(1-\left(\frac45\right)^4=\frac{369}{625}\).
workingLatex: \(P(F\mid S)=\frac18\ne\frac{3}{10}=P(F)\), so the events are not independent.
\(P(S\cap F)=P(S\mid F)P(F)=\frac13\times\frac{3}{10}=\frac{1}{10}\).
\(\frac18=P(F\mid S)=\frac{P(F\cap S)}{P(S)}=\frac{1/10}{P(S)}\).
\(P(\text{at least one not available})=1-\left(\frac45\right)^4=\frac{369}{625}\).

(a) know how one event impacts the other
(a) use ( )() |PF P F S≠ to show that Kim’s availability next Friday changes, given Kim is available on Saturday.
(b) set out a well-structured response with the aid of conditional probability rules

questions.title: Exponential curve area and intersections
questions.promptLatex: The curves \(y=e^{-2x}\) and \(y=e^{-x}-\frac14\) intersect at exactly one point as shown in the diagram. The point of intersection has coordinates \(\left(\ln2,\frac14\right)\). Do not prove this. (a) Show that the area bounded by the two curves and the \(y\)-axis, as shaded in the diagram, is \(\frac14\ln2-\frac18\). (b) Find the values of \(k\) such that the curves \(y=e^{-2x}\) and \(y=e^{-x}+k\) intersect at two points.
questions.answerLatex: The shaded area is \(\int_0^{\ln2}\left(e^{-2x}-e^{-x}+\frac14\right)dx\). This equals \(\left[-\frac12e^{-2x}+e^{-x}+\frac{x}{4}\right]_0^{\ln2}=\frac14\ln2-\frac18\). For part (b), let \(u=e^{-x}>0\). The intersections satisfy \(u^2=u+k\), or \(u^2-u-k=0\). Two positive roots require \(1+4k>0\) and \(1-\sqrt{1+4k}>0\), so \(-\frac14<k<0\).
workingLatex: \(A=\int_0^{\ln2}\left(e^{-2x}-\left(e^{-x}-\frac14\right)\right)dx\).
\(A=\frac14\ln2-\frac18\).

(e) The region, \(R\), bounded by the hyperbola \(y = \frac{60}{x + 5}\), the line \(x = 10\) and the coordinate axes is shown. Find the volume of the solid of revolution formed when the region \(R\) is rotated about the \(y\)-axis. Leave your answer in exact form.

workingLatex: (a) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains correct anti-derivative in terms of \(u\), or equivalent merit; Obtains correct integrand in terms of \(u\), or equivalent merit

(b) When \(n=1\)
\[\text{LHS} = 1 \times 2 = 2\]
\[\text{RHS} = 2 + (1-1)2^{1+1} = 2\]
\[\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}\]
\[\therefore \text{True when } n=1\]

Assume true when \(n=k\)
ie \((1 \times 2) + (2 \times 2^2) + \dots + (k \times 2^k) = 2 + (k-1)2^{k+1}\)

Consider \(n=k+1\)
\[\text{RHS} = 2 + (k+1-1)2^{k+1+1} = 2 + k \cdot 2^{k+2}\]
\[\text{LHS} = (1 \times 2) + (2 \times 2^2) + \dots + (k \times 2^k) + ((k+1) \times 2^{k+1})\]
\[= 2 + (k-1)2^{k+1} + (k+1)2^{k+1} \quad \text{by assumption}\]
\[= 2 + 2^{k+1}(k-1+k+1)\]
\[= 2 + 2^{k+1}(2k)\]
\[= 2 + k \cdot 2^{k+2}\]
\[= \text{RHS}\]
Therefore, by mathematical induction, it is true for all integers \(n \ge 1\).
(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Establishes the inductive step, or equivalent merit; Establishes the base case, or equivalent merit
(c(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct answer; Demonstrates some understanding of binomial probability, or equivalent merit
(c(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct answer
(d) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Uses given result to combine the first two terms on the left-hand side, or equivalent merit

(e) The total volume is the sum of the volume when the given hyperbola is revolved about the y-axis, \(V_1\), and the volume of a cylinder, \(V_2\).
\[V_1 = \pi \int_{4}^{12} x^2 \, dy\]
Given \(y = \frac{60}{x+5}\), then \(x+5 = \frac{60}{y}\), so \(x = \frac{60}{y} - 5\).
\[V_1 = \pi \int_{4}^{12} \left(\frac{60}{y} - 5\right)^2 \, dy = \pi \int_{4}^{12} \left(\frac{3600}{y^2} - \frac{600}{y} + 25\right) \, dy\]
\[= \pi \left[ -\frac{3600}{y} - 600\ln y + 25y \right]_{4}^{12}\]
\[= \pi \left[ \left(-\frac{3600}{12} - 600\ln 12 + 25 \times 12\right) - \left(-\frac{3600}{4} - 600\ln 4 + 25 \times 4\right) \right]\]
\[= \pi(-600\ln 12 + 800 + 600\ln 4) = \pi(800 - 600\ln 3)\]
\[V_2 = \pi \times 10^2 \times 4 = 400\pi\]
\(\text{Total} = V_1 + V_2 = \pi(1200 - 600\ln 3) \text{ units}^3\)

questions.title: Q13 (a) A hemispherical water tank has radius R cm. The tank has a hole at the bottom whic
questions.promptLatex:
(a) A hemispherical water tank has radius \(R \text{ cm}\). The tank has a hole at the bottom which allows water to drain out. Initially the tank is empty. Water is poured into the tank at a constant rate of \(2kR \text{ cm}^3 \text{ s}^{-1}\), where \(k\) is a positive constant. After \(t\) seconds, the height of the water in the tank is \(h \text{ cm}\), as shown in the diagram, and the volume of water in the tank is \(V \text{ cm}^3\). It is known that \(V = \pi \left( Rh^2 - \frac{h^3}{3} \right)\). (Do NOT prove this.) While water flows into the tank and also drains out of the bottom, the rate of change of the volume of water in the tank is given by \(\frac{dV}{dt} = k(2R - h)\).

(a(i)) Show that \(\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{k}{\pi h}\).

(a(ii)) Show that the tank is full of water after \(T = \frac{\pi R^2}{2k}\) seconds.

(a(iii)) The instant the tank is full, water stops flowing into the tank, but it continues to drain out of the hole at the bottom as before. Show that the tank takes 3 times as long to empty as it did to fill.

(b) Particle \(A\) is projected from the origin with initial speed \(v \text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle \(\theta\) with the horizontal plane. At the same time, particle \(B\) is projected horizontally with initial speed \(u \text{ m s}^{-1}\) from a point that is \(H\) metres above the origin, as shown in the diagram.

The position vector of particle \(A\), \(t\) seconds after it is projected, is given by
\[\mathbf{r}_A(t) = \begin{pmatrix} vt \cos\theta \ vt \sin\theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix}. \quad (\text{Do NOT prove this.})\]
The position vector of particle \(B\), \(t\) seconds after it is projected, is given by
\[\mathbf{r}_B(t) = \begin{pmatrix} ut \ H - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 \end{pmatrix}. \quad (\text{Do NOT prove this.})\]
The angle \(\theta\) is chosen so that \(\tan\theta = 2\).
The two particles collide.

(bi) By first showing that \(\cos\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\), verify that \(v = \sqrt{5}u\).

(bii) Show that the particles collide at time \(T = \frac{H}{2u}\).

(biii) When the particles collide, their velocity vectors are perpendicular. Show that \(H = \frac{2u^2}{g}\).

(biv) Prior to the collision, the trajectory of particle \(A\) was a parabola. (Do NOT prove this.) Find the height of the vertex of that parabola above the horizontal plane. Give your answer in terms of \(H\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) \(\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{k}{\pi h}\)
(a(ii)) T = \(\frac{\pi R^2}{2k}\)
(a(iii)) The tank takes 3 times as long to empty.
(b(i)) v = \(\sqrt{5}u\)
(b(ii)) \[ T = \frac{H}{2u} \]
(b(iii)) \[ H = \frac{2u^2}{g} \]
(b(iv)) H = \(\frac{2u^2}{g}\)
workingLatex:
(a(i)) \[V = \pi \left( Rh^2 - \frac{h^3}{3} \right)\]
\[\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dh} \times \frac{dh}{dt}\]
So \[\frac{dV}{dt} = \pi(2Rh - h^2)\frac{dh}{dt} = \pi h(2R - h)\frac{dh}{dt}\]
But we are given \[\frac{dV}{dt} = k(2R - h)\]
So \[\pi h(2R - h)\frac{dh}{dt} = k(2R - h)\]
\[\pi h\frac{dh}{dt} = k \quad \text{as } 0 \le h \le R \text{ so } 2R - h \neq 0\]
\[\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{k}{\pi h}\]
(a(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Obtains \(\frac{dV}{dh}\), or equivalent merit

(a(ii)) \[ \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{k}{\pi h} \] When \( t=0, h=0 \) and \( t=T, h=R \). So, \[ \int_{0}^{R} \pi h \, dh = \int_{0}^{T} k \, dt \] \[ \left[ \frac{\pi h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{R} = [kt]_{0}^{T} \] \[ \frac{\pi R^2}{2} - 0 = kT - 0 \] \[ T = \frac{\pi R^2}{2k} \]
(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Separates the variables from the differential equation in part (i), or equivalent merit

(a(iii)) When the tank is full the inflow of water stops. This means that the rate of change in volume is only dependent on \(h\).
ie \(-kh = \frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{dV}{dh} \times \frac{dh}{dt}\)
So \(-kh = \pi h(2R - h) \frac{dh}{dt}\)
\(\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{-k}{\pi(2R - h)}\)
When \(t = 0, h = R\)
\(t = T_E, h = 0\)
\[ \int_{R}^{0} \pi(2R - h) dh = \int_{0}^{T_E} -k dt \]
\[ \left[ \pi \left( 2Rh - \frac{h^2}{2} \right) \right]_{R}^{0} = -kT_E \]
\[ -\pi \left( 2R^2 - \frac{R^2}{2} \right) = -kT_E \]
\[ \pi \frac{3R^2}{2} = kT_E \]
\[ T_E = 3 \frac{\pi R^2}{2k} = 3T \]
So the tank takes 3 times as long to empty.

(a(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds correct expression for \(\frac{dh}{dt}\), or equivalent merit; Models the situation to find the new differential equation \(\frac{dV}{dt} = -kh\), or equivalent merit

(b(i)) The particles collide when their x-coordinates are the same at the same time. \[vt \cos \theta = ut\] So \[u = v \cos \theta\] \(\theta\) in 1st quad. and \(\tan \theta = 2\). So \(\cos \theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\) and \(\sin \theta = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\). \[u = v \cos \theta\] \[u = \frac{v}{\sqrt{5}}\] \[v = \sqrt{5}u\]
(b(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Equates x-coordinates, or equivalent merit

(b(ii)) Particles collide at time \(T\) so \(y\)-coordinates are the same.
\[ vT \sin\theta - \frac{1}{2}gT^2 = H - \frac{1}{2}gT^2 \]
\[ \sqrt{5}uT \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} = H \]
\[ T = \frac{H}{2u} \]
(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution

(b(iii)) At \(t = T\) the vectors \(\underline{v}_A\) and \(\underline{v}_B\) are perpendicular, so \(\underline{v}_A \cdot \underline{v}_B = 0\).
\[ \begin{pmatrix} v \cos\theta \\ v \sin\theta - gT \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} u \\ -gT \end{pmatrix} = 0 \]
\[ uv \cos\theta - gT(v \sin\theta - gT) = 0 \]
\[ u\sqrt{5} \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} - \sqrt{5}ugT \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} + g^2T^2 = 0 \]
\[ u^2 - 2ugT + g^2T^2 = 0 \]
\[ (u - gT)^2 = 0 \]
\[ u = gT = \frac{gH}{2u} \]
\[ H = \frac{2u^2}{g} \]

(b(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Evaluates the dot product of the velocity vectors and equates to 0, or equivalent merit; Observes that the dot product of the velocity vectors is 0, or equivalent merit

(b(iv)) Vertex of \(y_A(t) = vt \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2\) occurs when \(\dot{y} = 0\)
\[t = \frac{-v \sin \theta}{2(-\frac{1}{2}g)} = \frac{\sqrt{5}u \cdot \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}}{g} = \frac{2u}{g}\]
Height of particle A at that time is
\[y_A = vt \sin \theta - \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = \sqrt{5}u \times \frac{2u}{g} \times \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} - \frac{g}{2} \times \frac{4u^2}{g^2} = \frac{4u^2}{g} - \frac{2u^2}{g} = \frac{2u^2}{g} = H\]
(b(iv)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Identifies that \(\dot{y} = 0\) for particle A, or equivalent merit
