questions.title: Q14 (a(i)a(ii)) Let f(x) = 2x + x, for x > 0. (i) Explain why the inverse of f(x) is a fun
questions.promptLatex:
(a(i)a(ii)) Let \(f(x) = 2x + \ln x\), for \(x > 0\).
(i) Explain why the inverse of \(f(x)\) is a function.
(ii) Let \(g(x) = f^{-1}(x)\). By considering the value of \(f(1)\), or otherwise, evaluate \(g'(2)\).

(b(i)b(ii)) Consider the hyperbola \(y = \frac{1}{x}\) and the circle \((x - c)^2 + y^2 = c^2\), where \(c\) is a constant.
(i) Show that the \(x\)-coordinates of any points of intersection of the hyperbola and circle are zeros of the polynomial \(P(x) = x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1\).
(ii) The graphs of \(y = x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1\) for \(c = 0.8\) and \(c = 1\) are shown. By considering the given graphs, or otherwise, find the exact value of \(c > 0\) such that the hyperbola \(y = \frac{1}{x}\) and the circle \((x - c)^2 + y^2 = c^2\) intersect at only one point.

(ci) Given a non-zero vector \(\begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix}\), it is known that the vector \(\begin{pmatrix} q \\ -p \end{pmatrix}\) is perpendicular to \(\begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix}\) and has the same magnitude. (Do NOT prove this.)

Points \(A\) and \(B\) have position vectors \(\vec{OA} = \begin{pmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\vec{OB} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{pmatrix}\), respectively.

Using the given information, or otherwise, show that the area of triangle \(OAB\) is \(\frac{1}{2}|a_1b_2 - a_2b_1|\).

(cii) The point \(P\) lies on the circle centred at \(I(r, 0)\) with radius \(r > 0\), such that \(\vec{IP}\) makes an angle of \(t\) to the horizontal.

The point \(Q\) lies on the circle centred at \(J(-R, 0)\) with radius \(R > 0\), such that \(\vec{JQ}\) makes an angle of \(2t\) to the horizontal.

Note that \(\vec{OP} = \vec{OI} + \vec{IP}\) and \(\vec{OQ} = \vec{OJ} + \vec{JQ}\).

Using part (i), or otherwise, find the values of \(t\), where \(-\pi \le t \le \pi\), that maximise the area of triangle \(OPQ\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) f is always increasing, therefore since f is one-to-one for x > 0, f has an inverse function.
(a(ii)) g'(2) = 1/3
(b(i)) The points of intersection of the hyperbola and the circle satisfy \[\begin{cases} y = \frac{1}{x} & (1) \\ (x-c)^2 + y^2 = c^2 & (2) \end{cases}\] Substituting (1) into (2): \[(x-c)^2 + \frac{1}{x^2} = c^2\] \[x^2(x^2 - 2cx + c^2) + 1 = c^2x^2\] \[x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1 = 0\]
(b(ii)) c = \(\frac{2}{\sqrt[4]{27}}\)
(c(i)) Area of triangle = \(\frac{1}{2} |\vec{OA}| |\vec{OB}| \sin \theta = \frac{1}{2} |a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1|\)
(c(i)) The page provides an alternative solution for Question 14 (c) (i) using geometric projection.
(c(ii)) The area is maximized when \( \cos t = \frac{1}{2} \), so \( t = \frac{\pi}{3} \).
workingLatex: (a(i)) \[ f'(x) = 2 + \frac{1}{x} > 0 \text{ for } x > 0 \] \[ f \text{ is always increasing.} \] \[ \text{Therefore since } f \text{ is one-to-one for } x > 0, f \text{ has an inverse function.} \]
(a(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct explanation
(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds g'(x) or f'(1), or equivalent merit

(b(i)) The points of intersection of the hyperbola and the circle satisfy \[\begin{cases} y = \frac{1}{x} & (1) \\ (x-c)^2 + y^2 = c^2 & (2) \end{cases}\] Substituting (1) into (2): \[(x-c)^2 + \frac{1}{x^2} = c^2\] \[x^2(x^2 - 2cx + c^2) + 1 = c^2x^2\] \[x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1 = 0\]
(b(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution

(b(ii)) Let \(f(x) = x^4 - 2cx^3 + 1\). The hyperbola and the circle have exactly one point of intersection if there exists a value of \(x\) such that \(f(x) = 0\) and \(f'(x) = 0\). \(f'(x) = 4x^3 - 6cx^2 = 2x^2(2x - 3c)\). \(f'(x) = 0 \iff x = 0\) or \(x = \frac{3c}{2}\). Note that \(f(0) \neq 0\). \(f\left(\frac{3c}{2}\right) = 0 \iff \left(\frac{3c}{2}\right)^4 - 2c\left(\frac{3c}{2}\right)^3 + 1 = 0 \iff 3^4c^4 - 3^3 \times 2^2c^4 + 2^4 = 0 \iff -27c^4 = -16 \iff c = \frac{2}{\sqrt[4]{27}}\)

(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the x-coordinate of the double root in terms of c, or equivalent merit; Recognises that the circle and hyperbola are tangent when the polynomial has a double root and so is tangent to the x-axis, or equivalent merit

(c(i)) Let \(\theta\) be the angle between \(\vec{OA}\) and \(\vec{OB}\). Area of triangle = \(\frac{1}{2} ab \sin \theta\), where \(a = |\vec{OA}|\) and \(b = |\vec{OB}|\). Let \(\vec{OC} = \begin{pmatrix} a_2 \\ -a_1 \end{pmatrix}\) be the vector perpendicular to \(\vec{OA}\). \(\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC} = \begin{pmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} a_2 \\ -a_1 \end{pmatrix} = a_2 b_1 - a_1 b_2\). \(\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC} = |\vec{OB}| |\vec{OC}| \cos(\angle BOC) = b \times a \cos(\angle BOC)\). In both cases, \(|\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC}| = |ab \sin \theta| = 2 \times (\text{Area of triangle})\). Therefore, area of triangle = \(\frac{1}{2} |\vec{OB} \cdot \vec{OC}| = \frac{1}{2} |a_2 b_1 - a_1 b_2|\).

(c(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Recognise that the dot product of \(\vec{OB}\) with the vector perpendicular to \(\vec{OA}\), using the given information, is related to the area, or vice versa, or equivalent merit; Attempts to use a vector perpendicular to \(\vec{OA}\) or \(\vec{OB}\), or equivalent merit

(c(i)) \[ \text{Area triangle} = \frac{1}{2} |\overline{OB}| \times \text{perpendicular height of triangle } OAB \] \[ = \frac{1}{2} |\overline{OB}| \times |\text{projection of } \overline{OA} \text{ onto } \overline{OC}| \] where \(\overline{OC} \perp \overline{OB}\) and \(|\overline{OC}| = |\overline{OB}|\). Given \(\overline{OC} = \begin{pmatrix} b_2 \\ -b_1 \end{pmatrix}\), then \[ |\overline{OA} \text{ projected onto } \overline{OC}| = \frac{|\overline{OA} \cdot \overline{OC}|}{|\overline{OC}|} = \frac{|a_1b_2 - a_2b_1|}{|\overline{OB}|} \] \[ \therefore \text{Area of triangle} = \frac{1}{2} |\overline{OB}| \times \frac{|a_1b_2 - a_2b_1|}{|\overline{OB}|} = \frac{1}{2} |a_1b_2 - a_2b_1| \]

(c(ii)) \[ \vec{OP} = \vec{OI} + \vec{IP} = \begin{pmatrix} r + r \cos t \\ r \sin t \end{pmatrix} \] \[ \vec{OQ} = \vec{OJ} + \vec{JQ} = \begin{pmatrix} -R + R \cos 2t \\ R \sin 2t \end{pmatrix} \] Using part (i), we get that the area of triangle \( OPQ \) is \[ \text{Area of triangle} = \frac{1}{2} |(r + r \cos t)(R \sin 2t) - (r \sin t)(-R + R \cos 2t)| \] \[ = \frac{1}{2} |Rr(1 + \cos t) \sin 2t - Rr \sin t(-1 + \cos 2t)| \] \[ = \frac{1}{2} Rr |(\sin 2t \cos t - \sin t \cos 2t) + \sin 2t + \sin t| \] \[ = \frac{1}{2} Rr |\sin t + \sin 2t + \sin t| \] \[ \text{Area of triangle} = \frac{1}{2} Rr |\sin 2t + 2 \sin t| \] Let \( f(t) = \sin 2t + 2 \sin t \), so \( \text{Area triangle} = Rr |f(t)| \) \[ f'(t) = 2 \cos 2t + 2 \cos t \] \[ = 2(2 \cos^2 t + \cos t - 1) \] \[ = 2(2 \cos t - 1)(\cos t + 1) \] For all \( t \), \( \cos t + 1 \ge 0 \) therefore \( f'(t) \) has the same sign as \( 2 \cos t - 1 \). \( f(t) \) is odd so \( |f(t)| \) is even and we only need to study \( |f(t)| \) on \( [0, \pi] \) where \( \text{area of triangle} = \frac{1}{2} Rr |f(t)| \), so \( f(t) \ge 0 \) on \( [0, \pi] \).

(c(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the values of \( t \) at the stationary points of the area, or equivalent merit; Obtains the area of triangle \( OPQ \) in terms of \( t \), or equivalent merit; Obtains the components of \( \vec{OP} \) or \( \vec{OQ} \) in terms of \( t \), or equivalent merit

questions.title: Q12 (a) Prove that 23 is irrational. (b) Prove that for all real numbers x and y, where x^
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Prove that \(\sqrt{23}\) is irrational.

(b) Prove that for all real numbers \(x\) and \(y\), where \(x^2 + y^2 \neq 0\), \[ \frac{(x + y)^2}{x^2 + y^2} \leq 2. \]

(c) An object with mass \(m\) kilograms slides down a smooth inclined plane with velocity \(\underset{\sim}{v}(t)\), where \(t\) is the time in seconds after the object started sliding down the plane. The inclined plane makes an angle \(\theta\) with the horizontal, as shown in the diagram. The normal reaction force is \(\underset{\sim}{R}\). The acceleration due to gravity is \(\underset{\sim}{g}\). No other forces act on the object.

The vectors \(\underset{\sim}{i}\) and \(\underset{\sim}{j}\) are unit vectors parallel and perpendicular, respectively, to the plane, as shown in the diagram.

(ci) Show that the resultant force on the object is \(\underset{\sim}{F} = -(mg \sin \theta) \underset{\sim}{i}\).

(cii) Given that the object is initially at rest, find its velocity \(\underset{\sim}{v}(t)\) in terms of \(g\), \(\theta\), \(t\) and \(\underset{\sim}{i}\).

(d) Find the cube roots of \(2 - 2i\). Give your answer in exponential form.

(e) The complex number \(2 + i\) is a zero of the polynomial \[P(z) = z^4 - 3z^3 + cz^2 + dz - 30\] where \(c\) and \(d\) are real numbers.

(ei) Explain why \(2 - i\) is also a zero of the polynomial \(P(z)\).

(eii) Find the remaining zeros of the polynomial \(P(z)\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) Assume the contradiction, that \(\sqrt{23}\) is rational. Let \(\sqrt{23} = \frac{p}{q}\) where \(p, q \in \mathbb{Z}\) and \(q \neq 0\). Also assume that \(p\) and \(q\) are coprime. \[(\sqrt{23})^2 = \left(\frac{p}{q}\right)^2\] \[23 = \frac{p^2}{q^2}\] \[23q^2 = p^2\] 23 divides LHS, so 23 divides \(p^2\). Since 23 is prime, 23 also divides \(p\). So \(p = 23m\), for some \(m \in \mathbb{Z}\). \(\therefore 23q^2 = (23m)^2\) \[q^2 = 23m^2\] So similarly 23 also divides \(q\). But now \(p\) and \(q\) have a common factor of 23, contradicting the assumption. Therefore, the assumption is false and \(\sqrt{23}\) is irrational.
(b) \[ \frac{(x+y)^2}{x^2+y^2} \leq 2 \]
(c(i)) Resolving parallel and perpendicular to the slope gives \(R - mg \cos \theta \underset{\sim}{j} = 0\) and \(F - (mg \sin \theta) \underset{\sim}{i} = 0\). \(\therefore\) Resultant force is \(F = -(mg \sin \theta) \underset{\sim}{i}\)
(c(ii)) \(v = -gt \sin \theta\)
(d) \(z = \sqrt{2}e^{-\frac{i\pi}{12}}, \sqrt{2}e^{\frac{7i\pi}{12}}, \sqrt{2}e^{\frac{15i\pi}{12}}\)
(e(i)) Since \(P(z)\) has real coefficients, zeros occur in complex conjugate pairs. Therefore, \(2+i\) is a zero implies \(2-i\) is also a zero.
(e(ii)) The other zeros are \(z=2\) and \(z=-3\).

questions.title: Q13 (a) Find 1-x5-4x-x^2 \, dx. (bi) Show that k^2 - 2k - 3 0 for k 3. (bii) Hence, or oth
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Find \(\int \frac{1-x}{\sqrt{5-4x-x^2}} \, dx\).

(bi) Show that \(k^2 - 2k - 3 \ge 0\) for \(k \ge 3\).

(bii) Hence, or otherwise, use mathematical induction to prove that \(2^n \ge n^2 - 2\), for all integers \(n \ge 3\).

(c) A particle of mass \(1\text{ kg}\) is projected from the origin with speed \(40\text{ m s}^{-1}\) at an angle \(30^\circ\) to the horizontal plane.

(ci) Use the information above to show that the initial velocity of the particle is \(\mathbf{v}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3} \ 20 \end{pmatrix}\).

(cii) The forces acting on the particle are gravity and air resistance. The air resistance is proportional to the velocity vector with a constant of proportionality \(4\). Let the acceleration due to gravity be \(10\text{ m s}^{-2}\). The position vector of the particle, at time \(t\) seconds after the particle is projected, is \(\mathbf{r}(t)\) and the velocity vector is \(\mathbf{v}(t)\). Show that \(\mathbf{v}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3}e^{-4t} \ \frac{45}{2}e^{-4t} - \frac{5}{2} \end{pmatrix}\).

(ciii) Show that \(\mathbf{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 5\sqrt{3}(1 - e^{-4t}) \ \frac{45}{8}(1 - e^{-4t}) - \frac{5}{2}t \end{pmatrix}\).

(civ) The graphs \(y = 1 - e^{-4x}\) and \(y = \frac{4x}{9}\) are given in the diagram below. Using the diagram, find the horizontal range of the particle, giving your answer rounded to one decimal place.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a) \[ \int \frac{1-x}{\sqrt{5-4x-x^2}} dx = \sqrt{5-4x-x^2} + 3\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{x+2}{3}\right) + C \]
(b(i)) \[ k^2 - 2k - 3 = (k-1)^2 - 4 \ge 0 \text{ for all } k \ge 3 \]
(b(ii)) Let \(P(n)\) be the statement \(2^n \ge n^2 - 2\). Consider \(n=3\) case: \(LHS = 2^3 = 8\), \(RHS = 3^2 - 2 = 7\). \(8 \ge 7\) so \(P(3)\) is true. Assume \(P(k)\) is true for some \(k \ge 3\), thus \(2^k \ge k^2 - 2\). Prove \(P(k+1)\) is true, i.e., \(2^{k+1} \ge (k+1)^2 - 2 = k^2 + 2k - 1\). \(LHS = 2^{k+1} = 2(2^k) \ge 2(k^2 - 2) = 2k^2 - 4 = k^2 + 2k - 1 + k^2 - 2k - 3\). Using part (i), \(k^2 - 2k - 3 \ge 0\) for \(k \ge 3\), so \(LHS \ge k^2 + 2k - 1 = RHS\). Therefore \(P(k+1)\) is true. Hence, by induction, \(2^n \ge n^2 - 2\) for all integers \(n \ge 3\).
(c(i)) \(\mathbf{v}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3} \\ 20 \end{pmatrix}\)
(c(ii)) \( \underset{\sim}{v}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3}e^{-4t} \\ \frac{45}{2}e^{-4t} - \frac{5}{2} \end{pmatrix} \)
(c(iii)) \[ \mathbf{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} 5\sqrt{3}(1-e^{-4t}) \\ \frac{45}{8}(1-e^{-4t}) - \frac{5}{2}t \end{pmatrix} \]
(c(iv)) The particle lands on the ground at a time \(t\) which satisfies \(y=0\), that is, \[\frac{45}{8}(1-e^{-4t})-\frac{5}{2}t=0\] \[\frac{45}{8}(1-e^{-4t})=\frac{5}{2}t\] \[(1-e^{-4t})=\frac{5}{2}\times\frac{8}{45}t\] \[1-e^{-4t}=\frac{4}{9}t\] Solution \(t \approx 2.25\) according to the diagram provided. \(\therefore \text{range} = 5\sqrt{3}(1-e^{-4\times 2.25}) = 8.65918... = 8.7 \text{ m}\) (rounded to 1 decimal place)
workingLatex: (a) \[ \int \frac{1-x}{\sqrt{5-4x-x^2}} dx = \int \frac{-2-x}{\sqrt{5-4x-x^2}} dx + \int \frac{3}{\sqrt{5-4x-x^2}} dx = \sqrt{5-4x-x^2} + \int \frac{3}{\sqrt{9-(4+4x+x^2)}} dx = \sqrt{5-4x-x^2} + 3\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{x+2}{3}\right) + C \]

(a) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Splits the integral into the sum of two integrals and correctly integrates one, or equivalent merit; Completes the square for \(5-4x-x^2\) or writes \(1-x = \frac{1}{2}(-4-2x)+3\) or equivalent merit

(b(i)) \[ k^2 - 2k - 3 = k^2 - 2k + 1 - 4 = (k-1)^2 - 4 \]

\[ \therefore k^2 - 2k - 3 \ge 0 \text{ for all } k \ge 3 \]

(b(ii)) Let \(P(n)\) be the statement \(2^n \ge n^2 - 2\). Consider \(n=3\) case: \(LHS = 2^3 = 8\), \(RHS = 3^2 - 2 = 9 - 2 = 7\). \(8 \ge 7\) so \(P(3)\) is true. Assume \(P(k)\) is true for some \(k \ge 3\). Thus, \(2^k \ge k^2 - 2, k \ge 3\). Prove that \(P(k+1)\) is true. i.e. \(2^{k+1} \ge (k+1)^2 - 2\), \(2^{k+1} \ge k^2 + 2k - 1\). \(LHS = 2^{k+1} = 2(2^k) \ge 2(k^2 - 2)\) (since we assumed \(P(k)\) is true) \(= 2k^2 - 4 = k^2 + 2k - 1 + k^2 - 2k - 3\). \(\therefore LHS \ge k^2 + 2k - 1\) (using part (i)) \(= RHS\). Therefore \(P(k+1)\) is true. Hence, using the principle of mathematical induction, \(2^n \ge n^2 - 2\) for all integers \(n \ge 3\).
(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Establishes the inductive step, or equivalent merit; Establishes the base case, or equivalent merit

(c(i)) \[t = 0 \implies \frac{\dot{x}}{40} = \cos30^\circ \implies \dot{x} = 40\cos 30^\circ = 20\sqrt{3} \\ t = 0 \implies \frac{\dot{y}}{40} = \sin30^\circ \implies \dot{y} = 40\sin 30^\circ = 20 \\ \therefore \mathbf{v}(0) = \begin{pmatrix} 20\sqrt{3} \\ 20 \end{pmatrix}\]

\[ \dot{x} = 20\sqrt{3}e^{-4t} \quad \text{since } \dot{x} = 20\sqrt{3} \text{ when } t = 0 \]
\[ \ddot{y} = -4\dot{y} - 10 \]
\[ = -4\left( \dot{y} - \frac{5}{2} \right) \]
\[ \text{So } \dot{y} = -\frac{5}{2} + Be^{-4t} \]

Substituting back in \(\mathbf{r}(t)\) yields \[ \mathbf{r}(t) = \begin{pmatrix} -5\sqrt{3}e^{-4t} + 5\sqrt{3} \\ -\frac{45}{8}e^{-4t} - \frac{5}{2}t + \frac{45}{8} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 5\sqrt{3}(1-e^{-4t}) \\ \frac{45}{8}(1-e^{-4t}) - \frac{5}{2}t \end{pmatrix} \]

questions.title: Q14 (a(i)a(ii)a(iii)) Let z be the complex number z = e^i6 and w be the complex number w =
questions.promptLatex:
(a(i)a(ii)a(iii)) Let \(z\) be the complex number \(z = e^{\frac{i\pi}{6}}\) and \(w\) be the complex number \(w = e^{\frac{3i\pi}{4}}\).

(i) By first writing \(z\) and \(w\) in Cartesian form, or otherwise, show that \(|z + w|^2 = \frac{4 - \sqrt{6} + \sqrt{2}}{2}\).

(ii) The complex numbers \(z\), \(w\) and \(z + w\) are represented in the complex plane by the vectors \(\vec{OA}\), \(\vec{OB}\) and \(\vec{OC}\) respectively, where \(O\) is the origin. Show that \(\angle AOC = \frac{7\pi}{24}\).

(iii) Deduce that \(\cos \frac{7\pi}{24} = \frac{\sqrt{8 - 2\sqrt{6} + 2\sqrt{2}}}{4}\).

(b) The point \(P\) is 4 metres to the right of the origin \(O\) on a straight line.

A particle is released from rest at \(P\) and moves along the straight line in simple harmonic motion about \(O\), with period \(8\pi\) seconds.

After \(2\pi\) seconds, another particle is released from rest at \(P\) and also moves along this straight line in simple harmonic motion about \(O\), with period \(8\pi\) seconds.

Find when and where the two particles first collide.

(c(i)c(ii)) A projectile of mass \(M\) kg is launched vertically upwards from the origin with an initial speed \(v_0 \text{ m s}^{-1}\). The acceleration due to gravity is \(g \text{ m s}^{-2}\).

The projectile experiences a resistive force of magnitude \(kMv^2\) newtons, where \(k\) is a positive constant and \(v\) is the speed of the projectile at time \(t\) seconds.

(i) The maximum height reached by the particle is \(H\) metres. Show that \(H = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{kv_0^2 + g}{g} \right)\).

(ii) When the projectile lands on the ground, its speed is \(v_1 \text{ m s}^{-1}\), where \(v_1\) is less than the magnitude of the terminal velocity. Show that \(g(v_0^2 - v_1^2) = kv_0^2v_1^2\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) \[ |z+w|^2 = \frac{1}{2}[4-\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}] \]
(a(ii)) \(\angle AOC = \frac{7\pi}{24}\)
(a(iii)) \[ \cos \frac{7\pi}{24} = \frac{\sqrt{8-2\sqrt{6}+2\sqrt{2}}}{4} \]
(b) Collide \(2\sqrt{2}\) metres left of 0 when \(t = 5\pi\).
(c(i)) H = \(\frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{kv_0^2 + g}{g} \right)\)
(c(ii)) \[ \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{g}{g - kv_1^2} \right) = H \]
(c(ii)) \[ g(v_0^2 - v_1^2) = kv_0^2v_1^2 \]
workingLatex:
(a(i)) We have \( z = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}i \) and \( w = -\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}i \)

\[ z+w = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{1}{2}i\right) + \left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}i\right) \]
\[ = \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) + \left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)i \]
\[ = \frac{\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2}}{2} + \left(\frac{1+\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)i \]

\[ |z+w|^2 = \frac{1}{4}\left[(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})^2 + (1+\sqrt{2})^2\right] \]
\[ = \frac{1}{4}[3-2\sqrt{6}+2+1+2\sqrt{2}+2] \]
\[ = \frac{1}{4}[8-2\sqrt{6}+2\sqrt{2}] \]
\[ = \frac{1}{2}[4-\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}] \]

(a(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Obtains \( |z+w|^2 = \frac{1}{4}((\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})^2+(1+\sqrt{2})^2) \), or equivalent merit; Finds \( z \) or \( w \) in Cartesian form, or equivalent merit

(a(ii)) \(\angle AOB = \pi - \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{12\pi - 3\pi - 2\pi}{12} = \frac{7\pi}{12}\) \\ \(OACB\) is a parallelogram by definition of vector addition. \\ Since it has two adjacent sides of equal length, it is a rhombus. \\ A diagonal of a rhombus bisects the angle at each vertex \\ so \(\angle AOC = \frac{1}{2}\angle AOB = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{7\pi}{12} = \frac{7\pi}{24}\) \\ \(\angle AOC = \frac{7\pi}{24}\)

(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Provides why \(\angle AOB = \frac{7\pi}{12}\) OR \(\angle AOC = \text{Arg}(w) - \text{Arg}(z + w)\) OR \(OACB\) is a rhombus OR \(OC\) bisects \(\angle AOB\) or equivalent merit

(a(iii)) \[ \cos \frac{7\pi}{24} = \frac{1^2 + |z+w|^2 - 1^2}{2 \times 1 \times |z+w|} = \frac{1}{2}|z+w| = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\sqrt{4-\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}} \times \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{8-2\sqrt{6}+2\sqrt{2}}}{2 \times 2} = \frac{\sqrt{8-2\sqrt{6}+2\sqrt{2}}}{4} \]
(a(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof

(b) Since period is \(8\pi\), first particle is at 0 when second particle leaves \(P\). First particle is at \(Q\) when second particle is at 0. 
\(	herefore\) Time of collision is \(5\pi\) after first particle is released. 
Amplitude = 4 m, starts at maximum point

\(	herefore\) Equation of motion is \(x = 4\cos\left(\frac{1}{4}t\right)\) 
When \(t = 5\pi\), \(x = 4\cos\left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right) = -\frac{4}{\sqrt{2}} = -2\sqrt{2}\) 
\(	herefore\) Collide \(2\sqrt{2}\) metres left of 0 when \(t = 5\pi\).

(b) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds when the two particles first collide, or equivalent merit; Obtains equation of motion for the first particle or sketches a relevant diagram related to the motion of the first particle or equivalent merit

(c(i)) Upward motion: \( M\ddot{x} = -kMv^2 - Mg \) at \( t=0, x=0, \dot{x}=v_0 \). \\ \( \ddot{x} = -kv^2 - g \) \\ \( v \frac{dv}{dx} = -kv^2 - g \) \\ \( \int \frac{v dv}{kv^2 + g} = \int dx \) \\ \( -\frac{1}{2k} \ln |kv^2 + g| = x + c \) \\ At \( x=0, v=v_0 \) therefore \( -\frac{1}{2k} \ln |kv_0^2 + g| = x + c \) \\ \( \therefore x = \frac{1}{2k} \ln |kv_0^2 + g| - \frac{1}{2k} \ln |kv^2 + g| = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left| \frac{kv_0^2 + g}{kv^2 + g} \right| \) \\ When \( x=H, v=0 \) \\ \( \therefore H = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left| \frac{kv_0^2 + g}{g} \right| \) \\ But \( kv_0^2 + g \) and \( g > 0 \) so \( H = \frac{1}{2k} \ln \left( \frac{kv_0^2 + g}{g} \right) \)

(c(ii)) Downward motion (downward direction positive) \(x = 0, v = 0\) 
 \(M\ddot{x} = Mg - kMv^2\) 
 \(\ddot{x} = g - kv^2\) 
 \(v  rac{dv}{dx} = g - kv^2\) 
 \(\int  rac{v dv}{g - kv^2} = \int dx\)

 \( rac{1}{2k} \ln \left|  rac{g}{g - kv^2} ight| = x\)

(c(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Equates correct expressions for \(H\) from parts (i) and (ii), with absolute values correctly dealt with, or equivalent merit; Finds the force or acceleration equation of the downwards motion

(c(ii)) \[ \frac{1}{2k}\ln\left(\frac{kv_0^2+g}{g}\right) = \frac{1}{2k}\ln\left(\frac{g}{g-kv_1^2}\right) \implies \frac{kv_0^2+g}{g} = \frac{g}{g-kv_1^2} \implies (kv_0^2+g)(g-kv_1^2) = g^2 \implies kv_0^2g - k^2v_0^2v_1^2 + g^2 - gkv_1^2 = g^2 \implies kv_0^2g - k^2v_0^2v_1^2 - gkv_1^2 = 0 \implies kg(v_0^2 - v_1^2) = k^2v_0^2v_1^2 \implies g(v_0^2 - v_1^2) = kv_0^2v_1^2 \]

questions.title: Q15 (a(i)) Let J_n = _0^2 ^n \, d where n 0 is an integer. Show that J_n = n-1n J_n-2 for
questions.promptLatex:
(a(i)) Let \(J_n = \int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin^n \theta \, d\theta\) where \(n \ge 0\) is an integer. Show that \(J_n = \frac{n-1}{n} J_{n-2}\) for all integers \(n \ge 2\).

(a(i)a(ii)a(iii)) Use the Question 15 Writing Booklet.

questions.title: Q16 (a) Let w be the complex number w = e^2i3. (ai) Show that 1 + w + w^2 = 0. (aii) The v
questions.promptLatex:
(a) Let \(w\) be the complex number \(w = e^{\frac{2i\pi}{3}}\).

(ai) Show that \(1 + w + w^2 = 0\).

(aii) The vertices of a triangle can be labelled \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) in anticlockwise or clockwise direction, as shown. Three complex numbers \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) are represented in the complex plane by points \(A\), \(B\) and \(C\) respectively. Show that if triangle \(ABC\) is anticlockwise and equilateral, then \(a + bw + cw^2 = 0\).

(aiii) It can be shown that if triangle \(ABC\) is clockwise and equilateral, then \(a + bw^2 + cw = 0\). (Do NOT prove this.) Show that if \(ABC\) is an equilateral triangle, then \[a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = ab + bc + ca.\]

(b(i)b(ii)) (b) (i) Prove that \(x > \ln x\), for \(x > 0\).
(ii) Using part (i), or otherwise, prove that for all positive integers \(n\),
\[e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2.\]

(c) (c) The complex numbers \(w\) and \(z\) both have modulus 1, and \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \text{Arg}\left(\frac{z}{w}\right) < \pi\), where Arg denotes the principal argument.

For real numbers \(x\) and \(y\), consider the complex number \(\frac{xz + yw}{z}\).

On an \(xy\)-plane, clearly sketch the region that contains all points \((x, y)\) for which
\[\frac{\pi}{2} < \text{Arg}\left(\frac{xz + yw}{z}\right) < \pi.\]
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
(a(i)) As \(w \neq 1\), \(1 + w + w^2 = \frac{1 - w^3}{1 - w} = 0\) since \(w^3 = \left(e^{\frac{2i\pi}{3}}\right)^3 = 1\)
(a(ii)) Suppose triangle \(ABC\) is equilateral and anticlockwise. Rotating \(\overline{AC}\) by \(+\frac{2\pi}{3}\) brings it onto \(\overline{BA}\) so \(w(c - a) = a - b\). Then \(a(1 + w) - b - wc = 0 \Rightarrow -aw^2 - b - wc = 0\) (by part (i)) \(\Rightarrow a + bw + w^2c = 0\) (multiplying by \(-w\))
(a(iii)) \[ a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = ab + bc + ac \]
(b(i)) Let \(f(x) = x - \ln x\) for \(x > 0\). Since \(f'(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{x}\), the minimum occurs at \(x=1\) where \(f(1) = 1 - \ln 1 = 1\). Thus \(f(x) \ge 1\) for all \(x > 0\), which implies \(x - \ln x > 0\), or \(x > \ln x\).
(b(ii)) \[ e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2 \]
(c) The region is identified as lying in the second quadrant of the complex plane.
(c) This page provides a continuation of the working for Question 16(c). The derivation shows: \[ \text{Re}\left(\frac{w}{z}\right) = \cos(-\theta) \] \[ x + y\cos(-\theta) < 0 \] \[ x < -\cos(\theta)y \] Given \(\cos\) is even and \(-1 < \cos(\theta) < 0\) for \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi\), it follows that \(0 < -\cos(\theta) < 1\), leading to \(-\sec(\theta) > 1\) and \(y > -\sec(\theta)x\).
workingLatex: (a(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Recognises that \(1 + w + w^2\) is a factor of \(1 - w^3\), or equivalent merit

(a(ii)) Suppose triangle \(ABC\) is equilateral and anticlockwise. Rotating \(\overline{AC}\) by \(+\frac{2\pi}{3}\) brings it onto \(\overline{BA}\) so \(w(c - a) = a - b\). Then \(a(1 + w) - b - wc = 0 \Rightarrow -aw^2 - b - wc = 0\) (by part (i)) \(\Rightarrow a + bw + w^2c = 0\) (multiplying by \(-w\))
(a(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Attempts to use the fact that \(\overline{AC}\) rotated by \(\frac{2\pi}{3}\) is \(\overline{BA}\), or equivalent merit

(a(iii)) Suppose \(ABC\) is equilateral. It is either anticlockwise or clockwise. Therefore \(a + bw + cw^2 = 0\) or \(a + bw^2 + cw = 0\). This is equivalent to \((a + bw + cw^2)(a + bw^2 + cw) = 0\). Since a product is zero if and only if one of its factors is zero, \((a + bw + cw^2)(a + bw^2 + cw) = 0 \iff a^2 + bw^3 + cw^3 + ab(w^2 + w) + bc(w^2 + w^4) + ac(w + w^2) = 0 \iff a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - (ab + bc + ac) = 0\) (since \(w^3 = 1\) and \(w + w^2 = -1\) by part (i)) \(\iff a^2 + b^2 + c^2 = ab + bc + ac\) as required.
(a(iii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Identifies that in either situation the product is 0, or equivalent merit

(b(i)) Let \(f(x) = x - \ln x\) for \(x > 0\). \(f'(x)\) has the same sign as \(x - 1\) when \(x > 0\). This shows that \(\forall x > 0, f(x) \ge 1\), that is, \(x - \ln x \ge 1\). This implies \(\forall x > 0, x - \ln x > 0\) so \(x > \ln x\).
(b(i)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Defines a function \(f(x) = x - \ln x\), or equivalent merit

(b(ii)) Assume \(n\) is a positive integer. 
\[ \[\begin{aligned} n &> \ln n \ n-1 &> \ln(n-1) \ &\vdots \ 2 &> \ln(2) \ 1 &> \ln(1) \end{aligned}\] \] 
Adding all those inequalities gives \(1 + 2 + \dots + (n-1) + n > \ln(n) + \ln(n-1) + \dots + \ln(2) + \ln 1\). 
\[ \frac{n(n+1)}{2} > \ln(n!) \] 
\[ n^2 + n > 2\ln(n!) = \ln((n!)^2) \] 
By taking the exponential of both sides, we get \(e^{n^2+n} > (n!)^2\) as required.

(b(ii)) Marking criteria: Provides correct proof; Applies part (i) to each term of \(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln i\) OR Establishes inductive step OR Recognises that the question is equivalent to showing \(n^2 + n > 2 \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} \ln i \right)\); Uses part (i) to establish base case of induction, or equivalent merit

(c) Let \(\theta = \text{Arg}\left(\frac{z}{w}\right)\), then \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \theta < \pi\). As \(\left|\frac{z}{w}\right| = \frac{|z|}{|w|} = 1\), \(\frac{w}{z} = \overline{\left(\frac{z}{w}\right)}\). So \(\text{Arg}\left(\frac{w}{z}\right) = -\theta\). \(\frac{xz + yw}{z} = x + y\frac{w}{z} = x + 1 + y\frac{w}{z}\). For \(\frac{\pi}{2} < \text{Arg}\left(\frac{xz + yw}{z}\right) < \pi\), the number \(x + y\frac{w}{z}\) must lie in the second quadrant of the complex plane. Clearly \(y < 0\), otherwise \(x + y\frac{w}{z}\) lies below the \(x\)-axis. The real part of \(x + y\frac{w}{z}\) must be negative.
(c) Marking criteria: Provides correct sketch; Identifies the quadrant in which the region lies, or equivalent merit; Identifies a quadrant that is excluded, or equivalent merit
(c) See derivation in answerLatex.

questions.title: Q30 A plumber leases equipment which is valued at \$60\,000. The salvage value of the equi
questions.promptLatex: A plumber leases equipment which is valued at \(\$60\,000\). The salvage value of the equipment at any time can be calculated using either of the two methods of depreciation shown in the table. Under which method of depreciation would the salvage value of the equipment be lower at the end of 3 years? Justify your answer with appropriate mathematical calculations.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
Declining-balance method would provide a lower salvage value.
workingLatex: Straight-line method: \[ S = V_0 - D_n \] \[ = \$60\,000 - \$3500 \times 3 \] \[ = \$49\,500 \] Declining-balance method: \[ S = V_0(1 - r)^n \] \[ = \$60\,000(1 - 12\%)^3 \] \[ = \$60\,000(0.88)^3 \] \[ = \$40\,888.32 \] \(\therefore\) Declining-balance method would provide a lower salvage value.

Marking criteria: Provides the correct solution; Calculates the salvage value using the declining-balance method, or equivalent merit; Attempts to calculate the salvage value using the declining-balance method, or equivalent merit

workingLatex: \[1 cm = 2 m \\ \therefore \text{Dimensions of triangle: } 4 \text{ m by } 8 \text{ m} \\ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 8 = 16 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{For L shape: Each square is } 4 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Area of all squares} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \text{ m}^2 \\ \frac{1}{2} \text{ Circle has radius } 4 \text{ m} \\ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi \times 4^2 = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Total area} = 16 + 60 + 25.13 = 101.13 \text{ m}^2 \\ \text{Volume} = 101.13 \times 0.1 = 10.113 \text{ m}^3\]

\[ BAC_{\text{male}} = \frac{10N - 7.5H}{6.8M} \]

\(N\) is the number of standard drinks consumed
\(M\) is the person's weight in kilograms
\(H\) is the number of hours of drinking

Cameron weighs \(75\text{ kg}\). His BAC was zero when he began drinking alcohol. At 9:00 pm, after consuming 3 standard drinks, his BAC was 0.02.

Using the formula, estimate at what time Cameron began drinking alcohol, to the nearest minute.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
9:00 pm - 2 h 38 min = \(6:22 pm. \therefore \text{Cameron began drinking at 6:22 pm.}\)
workingLatex: \[ BAC_{\text{male}} = \frac{10N - 7.5H}{6.8M} \] \[ 0.02 = \frac{(10 \times 3) - (7.5 \times H)}{(6.8 \times 75)} \] \[ 10.2 = 30 - (7.5 \times H) \] \[ H = \frac{30 - 10.2}{7.5} \] \[ H = 2.64 \text{ hours} \] \[ H = 2 \text{ h } 38 \text{ min} \]9:00 pm - 2 h 38 min = \(6:22 pm. \therefore \text{Cameron began drinking at 6:22 pm.}\)
Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the correct value of H; Attempts to solve for H; Substitutes values into the BAC formula, or equivalent merit

assets.alt: Formula for BAC_male = (10N - 7.5H) / 6.8M with variable definitions

questions.title: Q38 A random variable is normally distributed with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of
questions.promptLatex:
A random variable is normally distributed with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. The table gives the probability that this random variable lies below \(z\) for some positive values of \(z\).

[Table of z-scores and probabilities]

The probability values given in the table are represented by the shaded area in the following diagram.

[Diagram of normal distribution curve]

The weights of adult male koalas form a normal distribution with mean \(\mu = 10.40\text{ kg}\), and standard deviation \(\sigma = 1.15\text{ kg}\).

In a group of 400 adult male koalas, how many would be expected to weigh more than \(11.93\text{ kg}\)?
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt:
\[ z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{11.93 - 10.40}{1.15} = 1.33 \] \[ P(\text{more than } 11.93) = 1 - 0.9082 = 0.0918 \] \[ \text{Number of koalas} = 0.0918 \times 400 = 36.72 \approx 36 \text{ (or } 37) \]
workingLatex: \[ z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{11.93 - 10.40}{1.15} = 1.33 \text{ (2 decimal places)} \] \[ \therefore \text{Probability from table} = 0.9082 \] \[ P(\text{more than } 11.93) = 1 - 0.9082 = 0.0918 \] \[ \text{Number of koalas} = 0.0918 \times 400 = 36.72 = 36 \text{ (accept 37 as well)} \]

Marking criteria: Provides correct solution; Finds the correct proportion of the group of koalas, or equivalent merit; Finds the correct probability from the table, or equivalent merit; Calculates the correct z value, or equivalent merit

questions.title: Two-way counting with overlap
questions.promptLatex: In a group of \(60\) students, \(38\) play basketball, \(35\) play hockey and \(5\) do not play either basketball or hockey. How many students play both basketball and hockey? Options: A. \(55\), B. \(18\), C. \(13\), D. \(8\).
questions.answerLatex: B. Since \(5\) students play neither sport, \(55\) students play at least one sport. Therefore the overlap is \(38+35-55=18\).

questions.title: Parabola reflections
questions.promptLatex: The parabola \(y=(x-3)^2-2\) is reflected about the \(y\)-axis. This is then reflected about the \(x\)-axis. What is the equation of the resulting parabola? Options: A. \(y=(x+3)^2+2\), B. \(y=(x-3)^2+2\), C. \(y=-(x+3)^2+2\), D. \(y=-(x-3)^2+2\).
questions.answerLatex: C. Reflecting about the \(y\)-axis gives \(y=(-x-3)^2-2=(x+3)^2-2\). Reflecting this about the \(x\)-axis gives \(y=-(x+3)^2+2\).
workingLatex: A reflection in the \(y\)-axis replaces \(x\) by \(-x\).
A reflection in the \(x\)-axis multiplies the function value by \(-1\).

questions.title: Domain of a rational square-root function

questions.answerLatex: D. The expression under the square root is in the denominator, so \(x^2-1\) must be strictly positive. Hence \(x<-1\) or \(x>1\).

questions.title: Conditional probability from a tree diagram
questions.promptLatex: A bag contains \(2\) red and \(3\) white marbles. Jovan randomly selects two marbles at the same time from this bag. Given that one of the marbles selected is red, what is the probability that the other marble selected is also red? Options: A. \(\frac1{10}\), B. \(\frac17\), C. \(\frac14\), D. \(\frac7{10}\).
questions.answerLatex: B. The probability of two red marbles is \(\frac25\cdot\frac14=\frac1{10}\). The probability of at least one red marble is \(1-\frac35\cdot\frac24=\frac7{10}\). Therefore \(P(\text{two red}\mid\text{at least one red})=\frac{1/10}{7/10}=\frac17\).
workingLatex: \(P(RR)=\frac25\cdot\frac14=\frac1{10}\).
\(P(\text{at least one }R)=1-P(WW)=1-\frac35\cdot\frac24=\frac7{10}\).
\(\frac{P(RR)}{P(\text{at least one }R)}=\frac{1/10}{7/10}=\frac17\).

questions.title: Inflection points of an antiderivative
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph \(y=f(x)\). The point \(Q\) is a horizontal point of inflection. Let \(A(x)=\int_0^x f(t)\,dt\). How many points of inflection does the graph \(y=A(x)\) have? Options: A. \(2\), B. \(3\), C. \(4\), D. \(5\).
questions.answerLatex: B. Since \(A'(x)=f(x)\), \(A''(x)=f'(x)\). Points of inflection of \(A\) occur where \(f'\) changes sign, which corresponds to the turning points of \(f\). The graph shows three such turning points; the horizontal point of inflection \(Q\) does not add another because \(f'\) does not change sign there.
workingLatex: Count where the gradient of \(f\) changes sign on the graph.

questions.answerLatex: The completed entries are: for \(x=-3\), first derivative positive and second derivative negative; for \(x=1\), first derivative zero and second derivative zero; for \(x=5\), first derivative positive and second derivative positive.

questions.title: Arithmetic series sum
questions.promptLatex: Find the sum of the terms in the arithmetic series \(50+57+64+\cdots+2024\).
questions.answerLatex: The first term is \(a=50\), the common difference is \(d=7\), and the last term is \(2024\). Solving \(50+(n-1)7=2024\) gives \(n=283\). Hence \(S_n=\frac{283}{2}(50+2024)=293471\).
workingLatex: \(50+(n-1)7=2024\Rightarrow7(n-1)=1974\Rightarrow n=283\).

questions.title: Area between intersecting curves
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the curves \(y=(x-1)^2\) and \(y=5-x^2\). (a) Find the \(x\)-coordinates of the points where the curves intersect. (b) Find the area of the shaded region bounded by the two curves.
questions.answerLatex: The curves intersect when \((x-1)^2=5-x^2\), giving \(x=-1\) and \(x=2\). The area of the shaded region is \(\int_{-1}^{2}\left(5-x^2-(x-1)^2\right)\,dx=9\) square units.
workingLatex: \(2x^2-2x-4=0\Rightarrow x^2-x-2=0\Rightarrow(x-2)(x+1)=0\).
The upper curve on the interval is \(y=5-x^2\), so the area is \(\int_{-1}^{2}\left(5-x^2-(x-1)^2\right)\,dx\).

questions.title: Capacitor voltage exponential model
questions.promptLatex: In a particular electrical circuit, the voltage \(V\) volts across a capacitor is given by \(V(t)=6.5(1-e^{-kt})\), where \(k\) is a positive constant and \(t\) is the number of seconds after the circuit is switched on. (a) Draw a sketch of the graph of \(V(t)\), showing its behaviour as \(t\) increases. (b) When \(t=1\), the voltage across the capacitor is \(2.6\) volts. Find the value of \(k\), correct to three decimal places. (c) Find the rate at which the voltage is increasing when \(t=2\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: The graph starts at \((0,0)\), increases, and approaches the horizontal asymptote \(V=6.5\). From \(2.6=6.5(1-e^{-k})\), \(e^{-k}=0.6\), so \(k=-\ln(0.6)\approx0.511\). Since \(\frac{dV}{dt}=6.5ke^{-kt}\), at \(t=2\) the rate is \(6.5ke^{-2k}\approx1.195\) volts per second.

questions.title: Goal attempt probability
questions.promptLatex: In a game, the probability that a particular player scores a goal at each attempt is \(0.15\). (a) What is the probability that this player does not score a goal in the first two attempts? (b) Determine the least number of attempts that this player must make so that the probability of scoring at least one goal is greater than \(0.8\).
questions.answerLatex: The probability of missing one attempt is \(0.85\), so the probability of missing the first two attempts is \((0.85)^2=0.7225\). For \(n\) attempts, \(P(\text{at least one goal})=1-(0.85)^n\). Requiring \(1-(0.85)^n>0.8\) gives \((0.85)^n<0.2\), so \(n>\frac{\ln0.2}{\ln0.85}\approx9.90\). Therefore the least number of attempts is \(10\).

betterResponses: (a) use probability of complimentary events to answer the question
(a) demonstrate their understanding of product rule for multi-stage events.
(b) generate the correct inequality 1 − 0.85𝑛 > 0.8 from the question
(b) use algebra skills when solving inequalities
(b) use logarithms to solve the equation
(b) conclude the solution by answering the question after showing their inequality or by trial and error.
improvementAreas: (a) using the concept of complimentary events
(a) using the product rule correctly
(a) recognising the difference between 1 − 0.152 and (1 − 0.15)2.
(b) understanding probability calculations for multistage events
(b) manipulating their inequality to make 𝑛 the subject by using logarithms
(b) concluding the solution through answering the question after showing their inequality.

questions.title: Sketching a quartic from derivatives
questions.promptLatex: Sketch the curve \(y=x^4-2x^3+2\) by first finding all stationary points, checking their nature, and finding the points of inflection.
questions.answerLatex: The derivatives are \(y'=4x^3-6x^2=2x^2(2x-3)\) and \(y''=12x^2-12x=12x(x-1)\). Stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=\frac32\). At \(x=0\), \(y=2\), and the concavity changes sign, so \((0,2)\) is a horizontal point of inflection. At \(x=\frac32\), \(y=\frac{5}{16}\), and \(y''>0\), so \(\left(\frac32,\frac5{16}\right)\) is a local minimum. The other point of inflection is \((1,1)\).
workingLatex: \(y''=12x^2-12x=12x(x-1)\), so possible inflection points are at \(x=0\) and \(x=1\).
At \(x=0\), \(y=2\), and the concavity changes sign, so \((0,2)\) is a horizontal point of inflection.
At \(x=\frac32\), \(y=\frac5{16}\), and \(y''=9>0\), so this point is a local minimum.
At \(x=1\), \(y=1\), giving the other point of inflection \((1,1)\).

betterResponses: find the first and second derivatives correctly
determine the nature of the stationary points using either the first or second derivative test
identify the difference between a horizontal point of inflection and a vertical point of inflection
justify a change in concavity
clearly provide a sketch of the function.
improvementAreas: correctly solving equations using the null factor
justifying the nature of a stationary point
justifying a change in concavity
clearly sketching graphs using calculus.

questions.title: Tower bearing
questions.promptLatex: A vertical tower \(TC\) is \(40\) metres high. Point \(A\) is due east of \(C\), and the angle of elevation of \(T\) from \(A\) is \(35^\circ\). From another point \(B\), the angle of elevation of \(T\) is \(30^\circ\), and \(AB=100\) metres. (a) Show that \(AC=57.13\) metres, correct to two decimal places. (b) Find the bearing of \(B\) from \(C\), correct to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex: Since \(\tan35^\circ=\frac{40}{AC}\), \(AC=\frac{40}{\tan35^\circ}\approx57.13\) metres. Also \(BC=\frac{40}{\tan30^\circ}\approx69.28\) metres. Applying the cosine rule in \(\triangle ABC\) gives \(\angle ACB\approx104^\circ\), so the bearing of \(B\) from \(C\) is approximately \(194^\circ\).
workingLatex: \(AC=\frac{40}{\tan35^\circ}\approx57.13\text{ m}\).
\(BC=\frac{40}{\tan30^\circ}\approx69.28\text{ m}\).
Using \(AB=100\), \(\cos\angle ACB=\frac{AC^2+BC^2-AB^2}{2(AC)(BC)}\).
\(\angle ACB\approx104^\circ\). Since \(A\) is due east of \(C\), the bearing of \(B\) from \(C\) is \(090^\circ+104^\circ=194^\circ\).

betterResponses: (a) recognise that ∆𝑇𝐶𝐴 is a right-angled triangle and choose the appropriate trigonometric ratio to find the required side

(b) show all working out in answering the question
(b) identify that the length of BC needed to be calculated
(b) recognise that the cosine rule for angles needed to be used, showing all relevant substitutions
(b) add 90° to their calculated angle for ACB to find the required bearing.
improvementAreas: (a) choosing the most appropriate trigonometric rule for a problem
(a) realising that if the unknown is in the denominator, then division is needed to complete the solution

questions.title: Anaconda scatterplot observations
questions.subtopic: Descriptive statistics and bivariate data analysis
questions.promptLatex: A researcher is studying anacondas. A dataset recording the age, in years, and length, in centimetres, of female and male anacondas is displayed on the scatterplot. Anacondas reach maturity at about \(4\) years of age. Write THREE observations about anacondas that may be made from the scatterplot. No calculations are required.
questions.answerLatex: Suitable observations include: female anacondas grow at a faster rate than male anacondas; both female and male anacondas continue to grow after about \(4\) years of age; and female anacondas are generally longer than male anacondas.

questions.title: Concavity and trapezoidal rule
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the function \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)\) is shown. (a) Prove that \(f(x)\) is concave up for \(-1<x<1\). (b) A table of function values, correct to \(4\) decimal places, is provided: \[\begin{array}{c|ccccc}x&0&0.25&0.5&0.75&1\\\hline \ln(1+x^2)&0&0.0606&0.2231&0.4463&0.6931\end{array}\] Using the function values provided and the trapezoidal rule, estimate the shaded area in the diagram. (c) Is the answer to part (b) an overestimate or underestimate? Give a reason.
questions.answerLatex: For \(f(x)=\ln(1+x^2)\), \(f'(x)=\frac{2x}{1+x^2}\) and \(f''(x)=\frac{2(1-x^2)}{(1+x^2)^2}\), which is positive for \(-1<x<1\). Using symmetry and the trapezoidal rule on \([0,1]\), the shaded area is approximately \(0.538275\). This is an overestimate because the function is concave up, so the trapezoids lie above the curve.
workingLatex: For \(-1<x<1\), \(1-x^2>0\), and the denominator is always positive, so \(f''(x)>0\).
The function and shaded area are symmetric, so estimate twice the area from \(0\) to \(1\).
Because \(f\) is concave up on the interval, the straight line segments used in the trapezoidal rule lie above the curve.

questions.title: Normal table probabilities and percentiles
questions.promptLatex: A random variable is normally distributed with mean \(0\) and standard deviation \(1\). The table gives values of \(P(Z<z)\) for \(z=0.6,0.7,\ldots,1.4\), and the diagram represents the shaded area. The scores in a university examination are normally distributed with mean \(58\) and standard deviation \(15\). (a) By calculating a \(z\)-score, find the percentage of scores between \(58\) and \(70\). (b) Explain why the percentage of scores between \(46\) and \(70\) is twice your answer to part (a). (c) Using the table, find an approximate minimum score needed to be placed in the top \(10\%\) of candidates.
questions.answerLatex: For \(70\), \(z=\frac{70-58}{15}=0.8\). From the table, \(P(Z<0.8)=0.7881\), so \(P(58<X<70)=0.7881-0.5=0.2881\), or \(28.81\%\). The interval from \(46\) to \(58\) corresponds to \(-0.8<Z<0\), so by symmetry it has the same area as \(0<Z<0.8\). For the top \(10\%\), use \(P(Z<z)\approx0.9\), so \(z\approx1.3\) and \(x=58+1.3(15)=77.5\), about \(78\) as a whole-number score.
workingLatex: \(46\) has \(z=\frac{46-58}{15}=-0.8\), so the normal distribution's symmetry makes \(P(46<X<58)=P(58<X<70)\).
For the top \(10\%\), the cumulative probability is approximately \(0.9\). The table value closest to this is \(0.9032\) at \(z=1.3\).
\(x=58+1.3(15)=77.5\), so the required score is approximately \(78\) if scores are whole numbers.

questions.title: Monthly savings annuity
questions.promptLatex: Jack intends to deposit \(\$80\) into a savings account on the first day of each month for \(24\) months. The interest rate during this time is \(6\%\) per annum, compounded monthly. (a) Calculate how much money Jack will have in his account at the end of the \(24\) months. (b) A table of future value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) could have been used for this calculation. For \(24\) periods at \(0.5\%\) interest per period, find the table value \(A\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: The monthly rate is \(0.005\). Because deposits are made on the first day of each month, the future value is \(80\left((1.005)^1+(1.005)^2+\cdots+(1.005)^{24}\right)=80(1.005)\frac{(1.005)^{24}-1}{0.005}\approx2044.73\). Since \(80A=2044.73\), \(A\approx25.559\).
workingLatex: The first deposit earns interest for \(24\) months, the next for \(23\) months, and so on.

questions.title: Probability density function and median
questions.promptLatex: A function \(f(x)\) is defined by \[f(x)=\begin{cases}0,&x<0,\\1-\frac{x}{h},&0\le x\le h,\\0,&x>h,\end{cases}\] where \(h\) is a constant. (a) Find the value of \(h\) such that \(f(x)\) is a probability density function. (b) By first finding a formula for the cumulative distribution function, sketch its graph. (c) Find the value of the median of the probability density function \(f(x)\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: For \(f\) to be a probability density function, the triangular area must be \(1\), so \(\frac12(1)(h)=1\), giving \(h=2\). For \(0\le x\le2\), \(F(x)=\int_0^x\left(1-\frac{t}{2}\right)dt=x-\frac{x^2}{4}\). Thus \(F(x)=0\) for \(x<0\), \(F(x)=x-\frac{x^2}{4}\) for \(0\le x\le2\), and \(F(x)=1\) for \(x>2\). The median \(m\) satisfies \(m-\frac{m^2}{4}=\frac12\), so \(m=2-\sqrt2\approx0.586\).
workingLatex: \(\frac12\times1\times h=1\Rightarrow h=2\).
\(F(x)=\int_0^x\left(1-\frac{t}{2}\right)dt=x-\frac{x^2}{4}\) for \(0\le x\le2\).
\(F(x)=\begin{cases}0,&x<0,\\x-\frac{x^2}{4},&0\le x\le2,\\1,&x>2.\end{cases}\)
For the median, \(m-\frac{m^2}{4}=\frac12\Rightarrow m^2-4m+2=0\).
Since \(0<m<2\), \(m=2-\sqrt2\approx0.586\).

questions.title: Present value for staged withdrawals
questions.promptLatex: Twenty-five years ago, Phoenix deposited a single sum of money into a new bank account earning \(2.4\%\) interest per annum, compounded monthly. Present value interest factors for an annuity of \(\$1\) include \[\begin{array}{c|cccc}n\backslash r&0.001&0.002&0.003&0.004\\\hline60&58.207&56.487&54.835&53.249\\120&113.026&106.592&100.649&95.156\\180&164.655&151.036&138.927&128.137\\240&213.278&190.460&170.908&154.093\\300&259.071&225.430&197.627&174.521\end{array}\] Phoenix withdrew \(\$2000\) at the end of each month for the first \(15\) years, starting at the end of the first month, and \(\$1200\) at the end of each month for the next \(10\) years, starting at the end of the \(181\)st month. Calculate the minimum sum Phoenix could have deposited to make these withdrawals.
questions.answerLatex: The monthly interest rate is \(r=0.024/12=0.002\). The account must fund \(\$1200\) per month for \(300\) months, plus an extra \(\$800\) per month for the first \(180\) months. Using the table, the required present value is \(1200(225.430)+800(151.036)=270516+120828.80=\$391344.80\).

questions.title: Derivative and integral using tan x
questions.promptLatex: (a) Find the derivative of \(x^2\tan x\). (b) Hence, find \(\int (x\tan x+1)^2\,dx\).
questions.answerLatex: By the product rule, \(\frac{d}{dx}(x^2\tan x)=x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x\). Expanding the integrand gives \((x\tan x+1)^2=x^2\tan^2x+2x\tan x+1=x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x-x^2+1\). Hence \(\int (x\tan x+1)^2\,dx=x^2\tan x-\frac{x^3}{3}+x+C\).
workingLatex: \(\frac{d}{dx}(x^2\tan x)=x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x\).
Use \(\tan^2x=\sec^2x-1\) to write the integrand as \(x^2\sec^2x+2x\tan x-x^2+1\).
\(\int (x\tan x+1)^2\,dx=x^2\tan x-\frac{x^3}{3}+x+C\).

questions.title: Ferris wheel height functions
questions.promptLatex: Anna is sitting in a carriage of a Ferris wheel which is revolving. The height, \(A(t)\), in metres above the ground of the top of her carriage is given by \(A(t)=c-k\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{24}\right)\), where \(t\) is the time in seconds after Anna's carriage first reaches the bottom of its revolution and \(c\) and \(k\) are constants. The top of each carriage reaches a greatest height of \(39\) metres and a smallest height of \(3\) metres. (a) Find \(c\) and \(k\). (b) How many seconds does it take for one complete revolution of the Ferris wheel? (c) Billie is in another carriage with height \(B(t)=c-k\cos\left(\frac{\pi(t-6)}{24}\right)\). During each revolution, there are two occasions when Anna's and Billie's carriages are at the same heights. At what two heights does this occur? Give your answer correct to \(2\) decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: The maximum and minimum heights give \(c+k=39\) and \(c-k=3\), so \(c=21\) and \(k=18\). The period is \(\frac{2\pi}{\pi/24}=48\) seconds. Solving \(A(t)=B(t)\) over one revolution gives \(t=3\) and \(t=27\), so the matching heights are \(21-18\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{24}\right)\approx4.37\) metres and \(21-18\cos\left(\frac{27\pi}{24}\right)\approx37.63\) metres.
workingLatex: The midline is \(c=\frac{39+3}{2}=21\).
The amplitude is \(k=\frac{39-3}{2}=18\).
\(\frac{2\pi}{\pi/24}=48\), so one revolution takes \(48\) seconds.
Set \(21-18\cos\left(\frac{\pi t}{24}\right)=21-18\cos\left(\frac{\pi(t-6)}{24}\right)\).
Over one revolution, the relevant solutions are \(t=3\) and \(t=27\).
\(A(3)=21-18\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{24}\right)\approx4.37\) and \(A(27)=21-18\cos\left(\frac{27\pi}{24}\right)\approx37.63\).

questions.title: Quadratic tangent and normal
questions.promptLatex: Consider the curve \(y=ax^2+bx+c\), where \(a>0\). At a particular point, the tangent and normal to the curve are given by \(t(x)=2x+3\) and \(n(x)=-\frac12x-2\), respectively. The curve has a minimum turning point at \(x=-4\). Find the values of \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\).
questions.answerLatex: The tangent and normal meet where \(2x+3=-\frac12x-2\), so \(x=-2\) and \(y=-1\). For \(y=ax^2+bx+c\), \(y'=2ax+b\). The turning point at \(x=-4\) gives \(-8a+b=0\), so \(b=8a\). The tangent gradient at \(x=-2\) is \(2\), giving \(-4a+b=2\). Hence \(a=\frac12\), \(b=4\), and substituting \((-2,-1)\) gives \(c=5\).
workingLatex: \(2x+3=-\frac12x-2\Rightarrow x=-2\), and then \(y=-1\).
At the turning point \(x=-4\), \(-8a+b=0\Rightarrow b=8a\).
At \(x=-2\), the tangent gradient is \(2\), so \(-4a+b=2\).

questions.title: Range of a geometric series limiting sum
questions.promptLatex: Suppose the geometric series \(x+x^2+x^3+\cdots\) has a limiting sum \(S\). By considering the graph \(y=-1-\frac{1}{x-1}\), or otherwise, find the range of possible values of \(S\).
questions.answerLatex: For the geometric series to have a limiting sum, \(-1<x<1\). The sum is \(S=\frac{x}{1-x}\). Since \(-1<x<1\), the graph \(y=\frac{x}{1-x}=-1-\frac{1}{x-1}\) has range \(S>-\frac12\).
workingLatex: At \(x=-1\), the boundary value is \(-\frac12\), and this endpoint is excluded.
As \(x\to1^-\), \(S\to\infty\), so \(S>-\frac12\).

questions.title: Annular sector perimeter optimisation
questions.promptLatex: Two circles have the same centre \(O\). The smaller circle has radius \(1\) cm, while the larger circle has radius \(1+x\) cm. The circles enclose a shaded region \(QRST\), which is subtended by an angle \(\theta\) at \(O\). The area of \(QRST\) is \(A\text{ cm}^2\), where \(A\) is a constant and \(A>0\). Let \(P\) cm be the perimeter of \(QRST\). (a) By finding expressions for the area and perimeter of \(QRST\), show that \(P(x)=2x+\frac{2A}{x}\). (b) Show that if the perimeter \(P(x)\) is minimised, then \(\theta\) must be less than \(2\).
questions.answerLatex: The perimeter is \(P=(1+x)\theta+\theta+2x=(2+x)\theta+2x\). The area is \(A=\frac12(1+x)^2\theta-\frac12\theta=\frac12\theta x(2+x)\), so \((2+x)\theta=\frac{2A}{x}\) and \(P(x)=2x+\frac{2A}{x}\). For a minimum, \(P'(x)=2-\frac{2A}{x^2}=0\), so \(x=\sqrt A\), and \(P''(x)=\frac{4A}{x^3}>0\). Substituting \(x=\sqrt A\) into \(A=\frac12\theta x(2+x)\) gives \(\theta=\frac{2\sqrt A}{2+\sqrt A}<2\).
workingLatex: \(P=(1+x)\theta+\theta+2x=(2+x)\theta+2x\).
\(A=\frac12(1+x)^2\theta-\frac12(1)^2\theta=\frac12\theta x(2+x)\).
Thus \((2+x)\theta=\frac{2A}{x}\), so \(P(x)=2x+\frac{2A}{x}\).
\(P'(x)=2-\frac{2A}{x^2}\). Setting \(P'(x)=0\) gives \(x^2=A\), so \(x=\sqrt A\) because \(x>0\).
\(P''(x)=\frac{4A}{x^3}>0\), confirming a minimum.
\(\theta=\frac{2A}{x(2+x)}=\frac{2A}{\sqrt A(2+\sqrt A)}=\frac{2\sqrt A}{2+\sqrt A}<2\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet
(a)
Consider the vectors a = 3 i + 2 j an...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 11 (15 marks) Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet
(a) Consider the vectors \(\mathbf{a}=3\mathbf{i}+2\mathbf{j}\) and \(\mathbf{b}=-\mathbf{i}+4\mathbf{j}\).
(i) Find \(2\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}\).
(ii) Find \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}\).
(b) Solve \(x^2-8x-9\leq0\).
(c) Using the substitution \(u=x-1\), find \(\int x\sqrt{x-1}\,dx\).
(d) Solve the differential equation \(\frac{dy}{dx}=xy\), given \(y>0\). Express your answer in the form \(y=e^{f(x)}\).
(e) Differentiate the function \(f(x)=\arcsin(x^5)\).
(f) The volume of a sphere of radius \(r\) cm is given by \(V=\frac43\pi r^3\), and the volume of the sphere is increasing at a rate of \(10\ \mathrm{cm^3\ s^{-1}}\). Show that the rate of increase of the radius is given by \(\frac{dr}{dt}=\frac{5}{2\pi r^2}\ \mathrm{cm\ s^{-1}}\).
(g) The region \(R\) is bounded by the curves \(y=\sin x\), \(y=x\) and the line \(x=\frac{\pi}{2}\), as shown in the diagram. Find the area of the region \(R\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) (i) \(2\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}=7\mathbf{i}\). (ii) \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{b}=5\).
(b) \((x-9)(x+1)\leq0\), so \(-1\leq x\leq9\).
(c) With \(u=x-1\), \(\int x\sqrt{x-1}\,dx=\int(u+1)u^{1/2}\,du=\frac25(x-1)^{5/2}+\frac23(x-1)^{3/2}+C\).
(d) \(\ln y=\frac{x^2}{2}+C\), so \(y=e^{x^2/2+C}\).
(e) \(f'(x)=\frac{5x^4}{\sqrt{1-x^{10}}}\).
(f) \(\frac{dV}{dt}=4\pi r^2\frac{dr}{dt}=10\), so \(\frac{dr}{dt}=\frac{5}{2\pi r^2}\).
(g) Area \(=\int_0^{\pi/2}(x-\sin x)\,dx=\frac{\pi^2}{8}-1\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 12 Writing Booklet a 2 a + 5
(a)
The vectors f p and d n are...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 12 (15 marks) Use the Question 12 Writing Booklet
(a) The vectors \(\begin{pmatrix}a^2\\2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix}a+5\\a-4\end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular. Find the possible values of \(a\).
(b) The region \(R\) is bounded by the function \(y=x^3\), the \(x\)-axis and the lines \(x=1\) and \(x=2\). What is the volume of the solid of revolution obtained when the region \(R\) is rotated about the \(x\)-axis?
(c) A charity employs a worker to collect donations. There is a 0.31 chance that when the charity worker talks to someone a donation is made to the charity. Each day the charity worker must talk to exactly 100 people. Use the standard normal distribution and the information on page 13 to approximate the probability that, on a particular day, at least 35% of the people talked to made a donation.
(d) Use mathematical induction to prove that \(2^{3n}+13\) is divisible by 7 for all integers \(n\geq1\).
(e) The diagram shows the graph of \(y=\frac{1}{|x-5|}\). For what values of \(x\) is \(\frac{x}{6}\geq\frac{1}{|x-5|}\)?
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) Perpendicular vectors give \(a^2(a+5)+2(a-4)=0\), so \((a-1)(a+2)(a+4)=0\). Thus \(a=-4,-2,1\).
(b) \(V=\pi\int_1^2 x^6\,dx=\frac{127\pi}{7}\).
(c) If \(X\sim\operatorname{Bin}(100,0.31)\), use a normal approximation with mean 31 and standard deviation \(\sqrt{100(0.31)(0.69)}\). With continuity correction, \(P(X\geq35)\approx P\left(Z\geq\frac{34.5-31}{\sqrt{21.39}}\right)\approx0.22\).
(d) Base case: \(2^3+13=21\), divisible by 7. If \(2^{3k}+13\) is divisible by 7, then \(2^{3(k+1)}+13=8(2^{3k}+13)-91\), also divisible by 7.
(e) Since the right side is positive, \(x>0\). For \(0<x<5\), \(x(5-x)\geq6\), giving \(2\leq x\leq3\). For \(x>5\), \(x(x-5)\geq6\), giving \(x\geq6\). Hence \(x\in[2,3]\cup[6,\infty)\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 13 Writing Booklet
(a)
In an experiment, the population of i...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 13 (16 marks) Use the Question 13 Writing Booklet
(a) In an experiment, the population of insects, \(P(t)\), was modelled by the logistic differential equation \(\frac{dP}{dt}=P(2000-P)\), where \(t\) is the time in days after the beginning of the experiment. The diagram shows a direction field for this differential equation, with the point \(S\) representing the initial population.
(i) Explain why the graph of the solution that passes through the point \(S\) cannot also pass through the point \(T\).
(ii) On the diagram provided on page 1 of the Question 13 Writing Booklet, clearly sketch the graph of the solution that passes through the point \(S\).
(iii) Find the predicted value of the population, \(P(t)\), at which the rate of growth of the population is largest.
(b) (i) Show that \(\cos^4x+\sin^4x=\frac{1+\cos^2 2x}{2}\).
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, evaluate \(\int_0^{\pi/4}(\cos^4x+\sin^4x)\,dx\).
(c) The vector \(\mathbf{a}\) is \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\3\end{pmatrix}\) and the vector \(\mathbf{b}\) is \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\end{pmatrix}\). The projection of a vector \(\mathbf{x}\) onto \(\mathbf{a}\) is \(k\mathbf{a}\), where \(k\) is a real number. The projection of \(\mathbf{x}\) onto \(\mathbf{b}\) is \(p\mathbf{b}\), where \(p\) is a real number. Find the vector \(\mathbf{x}\) in terms of \(k\) and \(p\).
(d) Using the substitution \(u=e^x+2e^{-x}\), and considering \(u^2\), find \(\int\frac{e^{3x}-2e^x}{4+8e^{2x}+e^{4x}}\,dx\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) The solution through \(S\) cannot also pass through \(T\) because solutions of the differential equation do not cross and the equilibrium \(P=2000\) separates the fields. The growth rate \(P(2000-P)\) is maximised at \(P=1000\).
(b) \(\cos^4x+\sin^4x=(\cos^2x+\sin^2x)^2-2\sin^2x\cos^2x=\frac{1+\cos^2 2x}{2}\). Hence the integral is \(\int_0^{\pi/4}\left(\frac34+\frac14\cos4x\right)dx=\frac{3\pi}{16}\).
(c) Let \(\mathbf{x}=\begin{pmatrix}X\\Y\end{pmatrix}\). The projection conditions give \(X+3Y=10k\) and \(2X-Y=5p\), so \(\mathbf{x}=\begin{pmatrix}(10k+15p)/7\\(20k-5p)/7\end{pmatrix}\).
(d) Since \(u=e^x+2e^{-x}\), \(du=(e^x-2e^{-x})dx\) and the integral becomes \(\int\frac{du}{u^2+4}=\frac12\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{u}{2}\right)+C\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a)
Find the domain and range of the func...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 14 (14 marks) Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a) Find the domain and range of the function that is the solution to the differential equation \(\frac{dy}{dx}=e^{x+y}\) and whose graph passes through the origin.
(b) For what values of the constant \(k\) would the function \(f(x)=\frac{kx}{1+x^2}+\arctan x\) have an inverse?
(c) (i) Explain why the equation \(\tan^{-1}(3x)+\tan^{-1}(10x)=\theta\), where \(-\pi<\theta<\pi\), has exactly one solution.
(ii) Solve \(\tan^{-1}(3x)+\tan^{-1}(10x)=\frac{3\pi}{4}\).
(d) A particle is projected from the origin, with initial speed \(V\) at an angle of \(\theta\) to the horizontal. The position vector of the particle, \(\mathbf{r}(t)\), where \(t\) is the time after projection and \(g\) is the acceleration due to gravity, is given by \(\mathbf{r}(t)=\begin{pmatrix}Vt\cos\theta\\Vt\sin\theta-\frac{gt^2}{2}\end{pmatrix}\). Do NOT prove this. Let \(D(t)\) be the distance of the particle from the origin at time \(t\), so \(D(t)=|\mathbf{r}(t)|\). Show that for \(\theta<\sin^{-1}\left(\sqrt{\frac89}\right)\), the distance \(D(t)\) is increasing for all \(t>0\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) Solving \(\frac{dy}{dx}=e^{x+y}\) gives \(e^{-y}=2-e^x\), so \(y=-\ln(2-e^x)\). The domain is \((-\infty,\ln2)\) and the range is \((-\ln2,\infty)\).
(b) \(f'(x)=\frac{(k+1)+(1-k)x^2}{(1+x^2)^2}\). For \(f\) to be one-to-one on its domain, require \(-1\leq k\leq1\).
(c) The left side is strictly increasing, so there is exactly one solution for each \(\theta\in(-\pi,\pi)\). Solving \(\tan^{-1}(3x)+\tan^{-1}(10x)=\frac{3\pi}{4}\) gives \(x=\frac12\).
(d) \(D(t)^2=V^2t^2-Vg\sin\theta\,t^3+\frac{g^2t^4}{4}\). Its derivative is positive for \(t>0\) when \(g^2t^2-3Vg\sin\theta\,t+2V^2>0\), which follows from \(\sin^2\theta<\frac89\).

Question 11 (16 marks) Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet
(a) Find \(\int xe^x\,dx\).
(b) Let \(z=2+3i\) and \(w=1-5i\).
(i) Find \(z+\overline{w}\).
(ii) Find \(z^2\).
(c) Find the angle between the two vectors \(\mathbf{u}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\-2\end{pmatrix}\) and \(\mathbf{v}=\begin{pmatrix}4\\-4\\7\end{pmatrix}\), giving your answer in radians, correct to 1 decimal place.
(d) Evaluate \(\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{1}{\sin\theta+1}\,d\theta\).
(e) (i) Write the number \(\sqrt{3}+i\) in modulus-argument form.
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, write \((\sqrt{3}+i)^7\) in exact Cartesian form.
(f) Sketch the region defined by \(|z|<3\) and \(0\leq \arg(z-i)\leq \frac{\pi}{2}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) \(\int xe^x\,dx=xe^x-e^x+C=e^x(x-1)+C\).
(b) (i) \(z+\overline{w}=3+8i\). (ii) \(z^2=-5+12i\).
(c) \(\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}=-18\), \(|\mathbf{u}|=3\), \(|\mathbf{v}|=9\), so \(\cos\theta=-\frac{2}{3}\) and \(\theta\approx2.3\) radians.
(d) \(\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{1}{1+sin\theta}\,d\theta=1\).
(e) (i) \(\sqrt{3}+i=2\operatorname{cis}\frac{\pi}{6}\). (ii) \((\sqrt{3}+i)^7=-64\sqrt{3}-64i\).
(f) The required region is inside \(|z|<3\) and in the first-quadrant sector based at \(i\): \(\operatorname{Re}(z)\geq0\), \(\operatorname{Im}(z)\geq1\), with the boundary rays included and the circle boundary excluded.

questions.title:
Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a)
Prove that if a is any odd integer, t...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 14 (15 marks) Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a) Prove that if \(a\) is any odd integer, then \(a^2-1\) is divisible by 8.
(b) Use mathematical induction to prove that \(\binom{2n}{n}<2^{2n-2}\), for all integers \(n\geq 5\).
(c) For the complex numbers \(z\) and \(w\), it is known that \(\arg\left(\frac{z}{w}\right)=-\frac{\pi}{2}\). Find \(\left|\frac{z-w}{z+w}\right|\).
(d) The following argument attempts to prove that \(0=1\). We evaluate \(\int\frac{1}{x}\,dx\) using the method of integration by parts: \(\int\frac{1}{x}\,dx=\int\frac{1}{x}\times1\,dx=\frac{1}{x}\times x-\int -\frac{1}{x^2}x\,dx=1+\int\frac{1}{x}\,dx\). So \(\int\frac{1}{x}\,dx=1+\int\frac{1}{x}\,dx\), and subtracting \(\int\frac{1}{x}\,dx\) from both sides gives \(0=1\). Explain what is wrong with this argument.
(e) The diagram shows triangle \(OQA\). The point \(P\) lies on \(OA\) so that \(OP:OA=3:5\). The point \(B\) lies on \(OQ\) so that \(OB:OQ=1:3\). The point \(R\) is the intersection of \(AB\) and \(PQ\). The point \(T\) is chosen on \(AQ\) so that \(O,R\) and \(T\) are collinear.
Let \(\mathbf{a}=\overrightarrow{OA}\), \(\mathbf{b}=\overrightarrow{OB}\) and \(\overrightarrow{PR}=k\overrightarrow{PQ}\), where \(k\) is a real number.
(i) Show that \(\overrightarrow{OR}=\frac{3}{5}(1-k)\mathbf{a}+3k\mathbf{b}\).
Writing \(\overrightarrow{AR}=h\overrightarrow{AB}\), where \(h\) is a real number, it can be shown that \(\overrightarrow{OR}=(1-h)\mathbf{a}+h\mathbf{b}\). Do NOT prove this.
(ii) Show that \(k=\frac{1}{6}\).
(iii) Find \(\overrightarrow{OT}\) in terms of \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) Let \(a=2m+1\). Then \(a^2-1=4m(m+1)\), and one of the consecutive integers \(m,m+1\) is even, so \(a^2-1\) is divisible by 8.
(b) Check \(n=5\): \(\binom{10}{5}=252<256=2^8\). For the induction step, assume \(\binom{2k}{k}<2^{2k-2}\) and use \(\binom{2k+2}{k+1}=\binom{2k}{k}\frac{2(2k+1)}{k+1}\) to show \(\binom{2k+2}{k+1}<2^{2k}\).
(c) Since \(\arg(z/w)=-\frac{\pi}{2}\), \(z/w\) is purely imaginary. Hence \(\left|\frac{z-w}{z+w}\right|=\left|\frac{z/w-1}{z/w+1}\right|=1\).
(d) The argument treats two indefinite integrals as if they have the same constant of integration. Correctly, the constants differ, so the subtraction does not imply \(0=1\).
(e) (i) \(\overrightarrow{OR}=\overrightarrow{OP}+\overrightarrow{PR}=\frac35\mathbf{a}+k(3\mathbf{b}-\frac35\mathbf{a})=\frac35(1-k)\mathbf{a}+3k\mathbf{b}\).
(ii) Comparing coefficients with \((1-h)\mathbf{a}+h\mathbf{b}\) gives \(h=3k\) and \(1-h=\frac35(1-k)\), so \(k=\frac16\).
(iii) \(\overrightarrow{OR}=\frac12(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})\). Since \(O,R,T\) are collinear and \(T\) lies on \(AQ\), comparing coefficients gives \(\overrightarrow{OT}=\frac34\mathbf{a}+\frac34\mathbf{b}\).

betterResponses: use a direct proof by letting 𝑎 = 2𝑘 + 1 for 𝑘 ∈ ℤ to show that 𝑎 2 - 1 = 4𝑘(𝑘 + 1), then noting that 𝑘 and 𝑘 + 1 are consecutive numbers where one is even and the other odd and so their product must be even, then lastly let 𝑘(𝑘 + 1) = 2𝑝 for 𝑝 ∈ ℤ, to conclude that 𝑎 2 - 1 is divisible by 8.
use cases to prove that 𝑘(𝑘 + 1) is even
use 𝑎 = 4𝑘 + 1 and 𝑎 = 4𝑘 + 3 (or 𝑎 = 4𝑘 - 1 ) and prove that in both cases 𝑎 2 - 1 is divisible by 8
use different methods of proof, for example, mathematical induction, proof by contrapositive and proof by contradiction can all be used to prove the result.
improvementAreas: understanding that proving the result is true for only one of 𝑎 = 4𝑘 + 1 or 𝑎 = 4𝑘 + 3 or 𝑎 = 4𝑘 - 1 only proves the result is true for some odd values of 𝑎, not all odd values of 𝑎 as required
using mathematical induction appropriately. The base case must be the lowest number, in this case 𝑎 = 1, and the step value must be 2

setting up the proof properly to be able to prove the required result when using proof by contrapositive or proof by contradiction, rather than trying to prove the converse or inverse. Mathematics Extension 2 - HSC marking feedback 2024 Page 9 of 16

questions.title:
Use the Question 15 Writing Booklet
(a)
Consider the three vectors a = OA, b...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 15 (15 marks) Use the Question 15 Writing Booklet
(a) Consider the three vectors \(\mathbf{a}=\overrightarrow{OA}\), \(\mathbf{b}=\overrightarrow{OB}\) and \(\mathbf{c}=\overrightarrow{OC}\), where \(O\) is the origin and the points \(A,B,C\) are all different from each other and the origin.
The point \(M\) is the point such that \(\frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})=\overrightarrow{OM}\).
(i) Show that \(M\) lies on the line passing through \(A\) and \(B\).
(ii) The point \(G\) is the point such that \(\frac{1}{3}(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})=\overrightarrow{OG}\). Show that \(G\) lies on the line passing through \(M\) and \(C\), and lies between \(M\) and \(C\).
(iii) The complex numbers \(x,w,z\) are all different and all have modulus 1. Using part (ii), or otherwise, show that \(\frac{1}{3}(x+w+z)\) is never a cube root of \(xwz\).
(b) Let \(I_n=\int_0^a x^{n+\frac{1}{2}}(a-x)^{\frac{1}{2}}\,dx\), where \(n\geq0\). Show that \((2n+4)I_n=a(2n+1)I_{n-1}\), for \(n>0\).
(c) A bar magnet is held vertically. An object that is repelled by the magnet is to be dropped from directly above the magnet and will maintain a vertical trajectory. Let \(x\) be the distance of the object above the magnet. The object is subject to acceleration due to gravity, \(g\), and an acceleration due to the magnet \(\frac{27g}{x^3}\), so that the total acceleration of the object is given by \(a=\frac{27g}{x^3}-g\). The object is released from rest at \(x=6\).
(i) Show that \(v^2=g\left(\frac{51}{4}-2x-\frac{27}{x^2}\right)\).
(ii) Find where the object next comes to rest, giving your answer correct to 1 decimal place.
(d) Using a suitable substitution, find \(\int \frac{2x^2}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\,dx\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) (i) \(\overrightarrow{OM}=\frac12(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})=\overrightarrow{OA}+\frac12\overrightarrow{AB}\), so \(M\) lies on line \(AB\).
(ii) \(\overrightarrow{OG}=\frac13(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})=\overrightarrow{OC}+\frac23\overrightarrow{CM}\), so \(G\) lies on line \(CM\) between \(C\) and \(M\).
(iii) The point represented by \(\frac13(x+w+z)\) lies inside the unit circle, so its modulus is less than 1, while every cube root of \(xwz\) has modulus 1.
(b) Use integration by parts with \(u=x^{n+1/2}\) and \(dv=(a-x)^{1/2}dx\) to obtain \((2n+4)I_n=a(2n+1)I_{n-1}\) for \(n>0\).
(c) (i) From \(v\frac{dv}{dx}=\frac{27g}{x^3}-g\) and \(v=0\) at \(x=6\), \(v^2=g\left(\frac{51}{4}-2x-\frac{27}{x^2}\right)\).
(ii) Setting \(v=0\) gives \(8x^3-51x^2+108=0\). The next positive rest position after release is \(x=\frac{3+\sqrt{585}}{16}\approx1.7\).
(d) One valid primitive is \(-3\sin^{-1}(1-x)-(x+3)\sqrt{2x-x^2}+C\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 16 Writing Booklet
(a)
Consider the function y = cos ^ kx h,...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 16 (14 marks) Use the Question 16 Writing Booklet
(a) Consider the function \(y=\cos(kx)\), where \(k>0\). The value of \(k\) has been chosen so that a circle can be drawn, centred at the origin, which has exactly two points of intersection with the graph of the function and so that the circle is never above the graph of the function. The point \(P(a,b)\) is the point of intersection in the first quadrant, so \(a>0\) and \(b>0\), as shown in the diagram.
The vector joining the origin to the point \(P(a,b)\) is perpendicular to the tangent to the graph of the function at that point. Do NOT prove this. Show that \(k>1\).
(b) The number \(w=e^{2\pi i/3}\) is a complex cube root of unity. The number \(\gamma\) is a cube root of \(w\).
(i) Show that \(\gamma+\overline{\gamma}\) is a real root of \(z^3-3z+1=0\).
(ii) By using part (i) to find the exact value of \(\cos\frac{2\pi}{9}\cos\frac{4\pi}{9}\cos\frac{8\pi}{9}\), deduce the value(s) of \(\cos\frac{2^n\pi}{9}\cos\frac{2^{n+1}\pi}{9}\cos\frac{2^{n+2}\pi}{9}\) for all integers \(n\geq1\). Justify your answer.
(c) Two particles, \(A\) and \(B\), each have mass 1 kg and are in a medium that exerts a resistance to motion equal to \(kv\), where \(k>0\) and \(v\) is the velocity of any particle. Both particles maintain vertical trajectories. The acceleration due to gravity is \(g\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-2}}\), where \(g>0\). The two particles are simultaneously projected towards each other with the same speed, \(v_0\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\), where \(0<v_0<\frac{g}{k}\). The particle \(A\) is initially \(d\) metres directly above particle \(B\), where \(d<\frac{2v_0}{k}\). Find the time taken for the particles to meet.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) At \(P(a,b)\), \(b=\cos(ka)\) and the tangent slope is \(-k\sin(ka)\). Since \(OP\) is perpendicular to the tangent, \(a=k\sin(ka)\cos(ka)\), so \(2a=k\sin(2ka)\). If \(k\leq1\), then \(k\sin(2ka)<2a\), contradicting the equation; hence \(k>1\).
(b) (i) Since \(\gamma^3=w\) and \(\overline{\gamma}=\gamma^{-1}\), \((\gamma+\overline{\gamma})^3-3(\gamma+\overline{\gamma})+1=\gamma^3+\overline{\gamma}^3+1=w+\overline{w}+1=0\).
(ii) The three real roots are \(2\cos\frac{2\pi}{9}\), \(2\cos\frac{4\pi}{9}\), and \(2\cos\frac{8\pi}{9}\). Their product is \(-1\), so \(\cos\frac{2\pi}{9}\cos\frac{4\pi}{9}\cos\frac{8\pi}{9}=-\frac18\). The doubling sequence repeats these factors modulo \(2\pi\), so the value is \(-\frac18\) for all integers \(n\geq1\).
(c) Let \(s=x_A-x_B\). Then \(\frac{d^2s}{dt^2}=-k\frac{ds}{dt}\), with \(s(0)=d\) and \(s'(0)=-2v_0\). Hence \(s=\frac{2v_0}{k}e^{-kt}+d- rac{2v_0}{k}\). Setting \(s=0\) gives \(t=\frac1k\log\left(\frac{2v_0}{2v_0-kd}\right)\).

(b) What is the bearing of the hotel from the train station? Round your answer to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) By Pythagoras' theorem, \(x^2=186^2+50^2\), so \(x=192.603\ldots\approx193\) m.
(b) \(\tan\theta=\frac{50}{186}\), so \(\theta\approx15^\circ03'\). The bearing is \(360^\circ-15^\circ03'=344^\circ57'\approx345^\circ\).

betterResponses: link the right-angled triangle with Pythagoras’ Theorem (a)
use trigonometric ratios (tan) to calculate an angle and use this angle to calculate the hypotenuse
draw a compass at the train station and then draw in the bearing to be calculated (b)
write the bearing as a compass bearing.
improvementAreas: using right-angled triangles with Pythagoras’ Theorem, especially when two lengths are given
reading the question carefully and drawing a compass from the train station
using trigonometric ratios with calculating angles in right-angled triangles when given lengths.

questions.title: A garden is made up of a right-angled triangle and a semicircle as shown
questions.promptLatex:
Question 18
(3 marks) A garden is made up of a right-angled triangle and a semicircle as shown. What is the area of the garden, correct to the nearest square metre?
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
The triangle has base \(6\) m and height \(7\) m, so \(A_{\text{triangle}}=\frac12\times7\times6=21\).
The semicircle has radius \(3\) m, so \(A_{\text{semicircle}}=\frac12\pi(3)^2=14.137\ldots\).
The total area is \(35.137\ldots\), so the area is \(35\text{ m}^2\), correct to the nearest square metre.

betterResponses: correctly apply the formula for the area of a semicircle
correctly apply the formula for the area of a triangle
correctly adds areas for composite shapes.
improvementAreas: understanding which sides of a triangle are important when finding area
calculating the area of the composite shapes separately
understanding the difference between perimeter, circumference and area.

questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
\(A_{\text{circle}}=\pi(30)^2=2827.433\ldots\).
The area of one central triangle is \(\frac12\times30\times30\sin72^\circ=427.975\ldots\).
The pentagon area is \(5\times427.975\ldots=2139.877\ldots\).

questions.title: Discrete probability table
questions.promptLatex: The probability distribution table for a discrete random variable \(X\) is shown: \[\begin{array}{c|c}x&P(X=x)\\\hline 1&0.4\\2&0.2\\3&\ ?\end{array}\] What is the value of \(P(X=3)\)? Options: A. \(0.2\), B. \(0.4\), C. \(1.2\), D. \(2.0\).
questions.answerLatex: B. The probabilities must add to \(1\), so \(P(X=3)=1-0.4-0.2=0.4\).

questions.answerLatex: A. The function \(y=4^x\) is positive, increasing, concave up, and has horizontal asymptote \(y=0\).
workingLatex: Since the base \(4>1\), \(4^x\) is increasing.
The graph is always positive and approaches \(0\) as \(x\to-\infty\).

questions.title: Domain of a radical function
questions.promptLatex: What is the domain of the function \(y=\sqrt{6-x^2}\)? Options: A. \((0,6)\), B. \([0,6]\), C. \((-\sqrt6,\sqrt6)\), D. \([-\sqrt6,\sqrt6]\).
questions.answerLatex: D. The expression under the square root must be non-negative: \(6-x^2\ge0\), so \(-\sqrt6\le x\le\sqrt6\).
workingLatex: \(6-x^2\ge0\Rightarrow x^2\le6\).
Therefore the domain is \([-\sqrt6,\sqrt6]\).

workingLatex: The graph has x-intercepts at \(0\), \(2\), and \(3\), with negative left-end and positive right-end behaviour.

questions.answerLatex: C. The transformation \(f(-x)\) reflects the graph in the \(y\)-axis, and the negative sign reflects it in the \(x\)-axis. The transformed graph has vertical asymptote \(x=2\), horizontal asymptote \(y=-1\), and y-intercept \(-3\).
workingLatex: Reflect \(y=f(x)\) in the \(y\)-axis to form \(y=f(-x)\).
Then reflect in the \(x\)-axis to form \(y=-f(-x)\).
The resulting asymptotes and intercept match option C.
