questions.title: Relative frequency for a weighted die
questions.promptLatex: A ten-sided die has faces numbered 1 to 10. The probability of obtaining 1 is greater than the probability of obtaining any other number, and the numbers 2 to 10 are equally likely. When the die is rolled 153 times, a 1 is obtained 72 times. Using the relative frequency of rolling a 1, which is the best estimate for the probability of rolling a 10? Options: A. \(\frac1{17}\), B. \(\frac1{11}\), C. \(\frac1{10}\), D. \(\frac19\).
questions.answerLatex: A. The estimated probability of rolling 1 is \(\frac{72}{153}=\frac8{17}\), so the remaining probability is \(\frac9{17}\). This is shared equally by the nine other faces, giving \(\frac1{17}\).
workingLatex: Each of the nine remaining faces has probability \(\frac{9/17}{9}=\frac1{17}\).

questions.title: Normal distribution above zero
questions.promptLatex: The minimum daily temperature of a town each year follows a normal distribution with its mean equal to its standard deviation. The minimum daily temperature was recorded over one year. What percentage of the recorded minimum daily temperatures was above zero degrees? Options: A. \(16\%\), B. \(50\%\), C. \(68\%\), D. \(84\%\).
workingLatex: Since \(\mu=\sigma\), zero is one standard deviation below the mean.
For a normal distribution, about \(84\%\) of values lie above \(z=-1\).

questions.title: Estimate from a derivative graph
questions.promptLatex: The diagram shows the graph of \(y=f'(x)\). Given \(f(1)=6\), which interval includes the best estimate for \(f(1.1)\)? Options: A. \([6.2,6.4)\), B. \([6.0,6.2)\), C. \([5.8,6.0)\), D. \([5.6,5.8)\).
questions.answerLatex: B. From the graph, \(f'(1)\) is about \(2\), so \(f(1.1)\approx f(1)+0.1f'(1)\approx6+0.2=6.2\). The graph decreases over this small interval, so the estimate lies just below \(6.2\), in \([6.0,6.2)\).
workingLatex: Use the local linear approximation \(f(1.1)\approx f(1)+(0.1)f'(1)\).
The graph gives \(f'(1)\approx2\), so \(f(1.1)\approx6.2\).
Since \(f'\) is decreasing near \(x=1\), the accumulated change is slightly less than \(0.2\).

questions.title: Stationary points of a composite function
questions.promptLatex: The graph of \(y=f(x)\), with all its stationary points, is shown. How many stationary points does the graph of \(y=f(e^x)\) have? Options: A. \(0\), B. \(1\), C. \(2\), D. \(3\).
questions.answerLatex: C. For \(g(x)=f(e^x)\), \(g'(x)=e^x f'(e^x)\). Since \(e^x>0\), stationary points occur when \(f'(e^x)=0\). The input \(e^x\) is always positive, so only the stationary points of \(f\) with positive input are counted. The graph shows two such stationary points.
workingLatex: Because \(e^x>0\), \(g'(x)=0\) exactly when \(f'(e^x)=0\).
The graph of \(f\) has two stationary points for positive input values.

questions.title: Quadratic turning point from a graph
questions.promptLatex: The graph of the quadratic function \(h=t^2-8t+12\) is shown. (a) Find the values of \(t\) and \(h\) at the turning point of the graph. (b) The graph shows \(h=12\) when \(t=0\). What is the other value of \(t\) for which \(h=12\)?
questions.answerLatex: Completing the square gives \(h=(t-4)^2-4\), so the turning point is \((4,-4)\). By symmetry, the other value of \(t\) where \(h=12\) is \(8\).
workingLatex: \(h=t^2-8t+12=(t-4)^2-4\), so at the turning point \(t=4\) and \(h=-4\).
The axis of symmetry is \(t=4\). Since \(t=0\) gives \(h=12\), the matching point on the other side is \(t=8\).

questions.title: Equation of a tangent
questions.promptLatex: Find the equation of the tangent to \(y=5x^3-\frac{2}{x^2}-9\) at the point \((1,-6)\).
questions.answerLatex: The derivative is \(y'=15x^2+4x^{-3}\), so the gradient at \(x=1\) is \(19\). The tangent through \((1,-6)\) is \(y+6=19(x-1)\), hence \(y=19x-25\).
workingLatex: \(y-y_1=m(x-x_1)\Rightarrow y+6=19(x-1)\Rightarrow y=19x-25\).

questions.title: Bivariate data and least-squares regression
questions.promptLatex: A research study compared each participant's average daily television watching time, \(x\) minutes, with their average daily exercise time, \(y\) minutes, over a three-month period. The data are shown in the scatterplot. (a) State the form and direction of the bivariate dataset. (b) The least-squares regression line is \(y=64.3-0.7x\). Interpret the slope and the \(y\)-intercept in context. (c) Use the line to estimate the average daily exercise time for \(x=42\). (d) Explain why the line should not be used to estimate the exercise time for a person who watches television for two hours per day.
questions.answerLatex: The association is linear and negative. The slope means that, for each additional minute of average daily television watching, the predicted average daily exercise time decreases by \(0.7\) minutes. The \(y\)-intercept means that a participant who watches no television is predicted to exercise for \(64.3\) minutes per day. For \(x=42\), \(y=64.3-0.7(42)=34.9\), so the estimate is \(34.9\) minutes. Two hours is outside the range of the observed data, so this would be unreliable extrapolation.
workingLatex: Form: linear. Direction: negative.
Slope: \(-0.7\), so predicted exercise time falls by \(0.7\) minutes per extra minute of television watching.
The value \(x=120\) is outside the plotted data range, so the prediction would be an extrapolation.

(c) correctly substitute 𝑥 = 42 into the equation to calculate the answer.
(d) convert 2 hours to minutes and substitute 𝑥 = 120 minutes into the formula
(d) identify that the graph goes below zero and cannot have a negative number of minutes of exercise
(d) recognise 2 hours is outside the dataset
(d) understand the meaning of the key word ‘extrapolate’.

(a) refining their knowledge of statistical terms and key words used in the syllabus for bivariate data analysis.
(b) interpreting the slope and 𝑦-intercept in context of the question with values shown
(b) interpreting the slope and 𝑦-intercept correctly, not just identifying the values
(b) understanding that the slope is not the same as the correlation coefficient.
(c) showing correct substitution into the formula

questions.promptLatex: A sound wave can be modelled using \(P(t)=k\sin(at)\), where \(P\) is air pressure in Pascals, \(t\) is time in milliseconds, and \(k\) and \(a\) are constants. (a) Write the equation for \(P_1(t)\), a sound wave with amplitude \(2\) Pascals and period \(5\) ms. (b) The graph of \(P_1(t)\) is shown. On the diagram, sketch \(P_2(t)=4\sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{10}\right)\) for \(0\le t\le10\). (c) Hence, find the values of \(t\), where \(0<t<10\), for which \(P_1(t)\) and \(P_2(t)\) are both decreasing.
questions.answerLatex: For \(P_1\), \(k=2\) and \(\frac{2\pi}{a}=5\), so \(a=\frac{2\pi}{5}\) and \(P_1(t)=2\sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{5}\right)\). The graph of \(P_2\) has amplitude \(4\) and period \(20\). From the graph, both functions are decreasing for \(6.25<t<8.75\).
workingLatex: Amplitude \(2\) gives \(k=2\).

questions.title: Stationary points of an exponential quotient
questions.promptLatex: Consider the function \(f(x)=\frac{x^2}{e^x}\). (a) Find the stationary points of the function and determine their nature. (b) A partially completed graph of \(f(x)=\frac{x^2}{e^x}\) is shown. Use your answer from part (a) to complete the graph.
questions.answerLatex: Writing \(f(x)=x^2e^{-x}\), \(f'(x)=2xe^{-x}-x^2e^{-x}=\frac{x(2-x)}{e^x}\). Hence stationary points occur at \(x=0\) and \(x=2\). Since \(f'(x)\) changes from negative to positive at \(0\), \((0,0)\) is a minimum. Since \(f'(x)\) changes from positive to negative at \(2\), \(\left(2,\frac4{e^2}\right)\) is a maximum.
workingLatex: \(f'(x)=0\Rightarrow x=0\) or \(x=2\).
The sign of \(f'(x)\) is negative, then positive, then negative across \(x=0\) and \(x=2\), so there is a minimum at \((0,0)\) and a maximum at \(\left(2,\frac4{e^2}\right)\).

questions.title: Reducing-balance loan model
questions.promptLatex: A borrower obtains a reducing-balance loan of \($800\,000\). Interest is charged at \(0.5\%\) monthly, compounded monthly. On the last day of each month, interest is added and then a repayment of \($5740\) is made. Let \(A_n\) be the balance owing at the end of \(n\) months. (a) Show that \(A_2=800000(1.005)^2-5740(1.005)-5740\). (b) Show that \(A_n=1148000-348000(1.005)^n\). (c) Find when the balance owing is first less than \($400\,000\).
questions.answerLatex: The recurrence is \(A_n=1.005A_{n-1}-5740\). This gives \(A_2=800000(1.005)^2-5740(1.005)-5740\). Summing the geometric repayment terms gives \(A_n=800000(1.005)^n-5740\frac{(1.005)^n-1}{0.005}=1148000-348000(1.005)^n\). Solving \(1148000-348000(1.005)^n<400000\) gives \(n>153.4\ldots\), so the balance is first less than \($400\,000\) after \(154\) months.

questions.title: Range of a composite function
questions.promptLatex: Find the range of \(g(f(x))\), given \(f(x)=\frac{3}{x-1}\) and \(g(x)=x+5\).
questions.answerLatex: \(g(f(x))=\frac{3}{x-1}+5\). The term \(\frac{3}{x-1}\) cannot equal \(0\), so the range is all real \(y\) with \(y\ne5\).

Because \(\frac{3}{x-1}\ne0\), the output cannot be \(5\).

questions.title: Conditional probability from selection odds
questions.promptLatex: Amara, Bala and Cassie have nominated for a soccer team, and exactly one will be selected. Their chances of selection are in the ratio \(1:2:3\), respectively. The probability that the team wins is \(0.5\) if Amara is selected, \(0.4\) if Bala is selected, and \(0.2\) if Cassie is selected. Given that the team wins, find the probability that Amara was selected.
questions.answerLatex: The selection probabilities are \(\frac16\), \(\frac13\), and \(\frac12\). Therefore \[P(\text{Amara}\mid W)=\frac{\frac16\times0.5}{\frac16\times0.5+\frac13\times0.4+\frac12\times0.2}=\frac{5}{19}.\]

Yemi wants the same future value by depositing a fixed amount at the end of each month for 7 years in an account with the same interest rate. Determine Yemi's monthly deposit.
questions.answerLatex: There are \(84\) monthly periods and the monthly rate is \(0.005\). Lin's future value is \(21000(1.005)^{84}\approx31927.76\). Using the annuity factor \(104.07393\), Yemi's monthly deposit is \(31927.76\div104.07393\approx\$306.78\).

questions.title: Continuous random variable percentile
questions.promptLatex: A continuous random variable \(X\) has probability density function \[f(x)=\begin{cases}0,&x<1,\\ \frac1x,&1\le x\le e,\\ 0,&x>e.\end{cases}\] (a) Find the mode and justify your answer. (b) Calculate the value of the 25th percentile, \(Q_1\), correct to three decimal places.
questions.answerLatex: On \([1,e]\), \(f(x)=\frac1x\) is decreasing, so the mode is \(1\). For the 25th percentile, \(\int_1^{Q_1}\frac1x\,dx=0.25\), so \(\ln Q_1=0.25\) and \(Q_1=e^{0.25}\approx1.284\).
workingLatex: \(f(x)=\frac1x\) is largest at the left endpoint of \([1,e]\), so the mode is \(1\).
\(P(X\le Q_1)=0.25\Rightarrow\int_1^{Q_1}\frac1x\,dx=0.25\).
\(\ln Q_1-\ln1=0.25\Rightarrow Q_1=e^{0.25}\approx1.284\).

questions.title: Trigonometric identity proof
questions.promptLatex: Prove that \(\frac{\sin^4\theta+\cos^4\theta+2\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}=\sec^2\theta\,\cosec^2\theta\).
questions.answerLatex: The numerator is \((\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta)^2=1\). Hence the left side is \(\frac{1}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}=\sec^2\theta\,\cosec^2\theta\), as required.
workingLatex: \(\sin^4\theta+\cos^4\theta+2\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta=(\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta)^2\).
Since \(\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\), the fraction becomes \(\frac{1}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}\).
\(\frac{1}{\sin^2\theta\cos^2\theta}=\frac{1}{\sin^2\theta}\frac{1}{\cos^2\theta}=\cosec^2\theta\sec^2\theta\).

questions.title: Normal model for sheep weights
questions.promptLatex: (a) In a flock of \(12\,600\) sheep, the ratio of males to females is \(1:20\). The weights of the male sheep are normally distributed with mean \(76.2\) kg and standard deviation \(6.8\) kg. In the flock, \(15\) male sheep each weigh more than \(x\) kg. Find \(x\). (b) The female sheep weights are also normally distributed but have a smaller mean and smaller standard deviation than the male sheep weights. Explain whether it could be expected that \(300\) females each weigh more than \(x\) kg.
questions.answerLatex: There are \(12600\div21=600\) male sheep, and \(15\) out of \(600\) is \(2.5\%\). This corresponds to approximately two standard deviations above the mean, so \(x=76.2+2(6.8)=89.8\) kg. For females, the smaller mean and smaller standard deviation make the corresponding \(z\)-score larger than \(2\), so fewer than \(2.5\%\) should exceed \(x\); it would not be expected that \(300\) females exceed \(x\).
workingLatex: Number of male sheep \(=12600\times\frac1{21}=600\).
\(15\div600=0.025=2.5\%\), so \(x\) is about \(2\) standard deviations above the mean.
For females, \(z=\frac{x-\mu}{\sigma}\) is larger because both \(\mu\) and \(\sigma\) are smaller, so the upper-tail percentage is less than \(2.5\%\).

questions.title: Common tangent to a logarithmic graph and parabola
questions.promptLatex: The graphs of \(y=e\ln x\) and \(y=ax^2+c\) are shown. The line \(y=x\) is a tangent to both graphs at their point of intersection. Find the values of \(a\) and \(c\).
questions.answerLatex: The common tangent has gradient \(1\). Since \(\frac{d}{dx}(e\ln x)=\frac{e}{x}\), setting \(\frac{e}{x}=1\) gives \(x=e\), and then \(y=e\). For \(y=ax^2+c\), the derivative is \(2ax\), so \(2ae=1\) and \(a=\frac{1}{2e}\). Substituting \((e,e)\) gives \(e=ae^2+c=\frac{e}{2}+c\), hence \(c=\frac{e}{2}\).
workingLatex: At the point of tangency, both graphs have gradient \(1\).
\(\frac{d}{dx}(e\ln x)=\frac{e}{x}\), so \(\frac{e}{x}=1\Rightarrow x=e\).
The point of intersection is therefore \((e,e)\).
For \(y=ax^2+c\), \(\frac{dy}{dx}=2ax\), so \(2ae=1\Rightarrow a=\frac{1}{2e}\).
\(e=a e^2+c=\frac{1}{2e}e^2+c=\frac{e}{2}+c\Rightarrow c=\frac{e}{2}\).

betterResponses: recognise that the tangent will have the same gradient as each function at the point of intersection
differentiate both functions and equate them to 1

use the point of intersection, 𝑎 and the quadratic to solve for 𝑐.
improvementAreas: differentiating logarithmic functions

questions.title: Areas under x sin x using a series
questions.promptLatex: (a) Show that \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x-x\cos x)=x\sin x\). (b) Hence, find \(\int_0^{2025\pi}x\sin x\,dx\). (c) The regions bounded by the \(x\)-axis and the graph of \(y=x\sin x\), for \(x\ge0\), are shown. Let \(A_n=\int_{(n-1)\pi}^{n\pi}x\sin x\,dx\), where \(n\) is a positive integer. It can be shown that \(|A_n|=(2n-1)\pi\). Find the exact total area of the regions bounded by \(y=x\sin x\) and the \(x\)-axis between \(x=0\) and \(x=2025\pi\).
questions.answerLatex: Using the product rule, \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x-x\cos x)=\cos x-(\cos x-x\sin x)=x\sin x\). Hence \(\int_0^{2025\pi}x\sin x\,dx=[\sin x-x\cos x]_0^{2025\pi}=2025\pi\). For total area, sum \(\pi+3\pi+5\pi+\cdots+4049\pi\), an arithmetic series with \(2025\) terms. Therefore the exact total area is \(2025^2\pi\) square units.
workingLatex: \(\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x-x\cos x)=\cos x-(\cos x-x\sin x)=x\sin x\).
\(\sin(2025\pi)=0\) and \(\cos(2025\pi)=-1\), so the integral is \(2025\pi\).
The total area is \(\sum_{n=1}^{2025}|A_n|=\pi(1+3+5+\cdots+4049)\).
The sum of the first \(2025\) odd numbers is \(2025^2\), so the total area is \(2025^2\pi\).

questions.title: Wire area optimisation
questions.promptLatex: A piece of wire is \(100\) cm long. Some is used to make a circle of radius \(r\) cm, and the remainder is used to make an equilateral triangle of side length \(x\) cm. (a) Show that the combined area is \[A(x)=\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2+\frac{(100-3x)^2}{4\pi}.\] (b) By considering this quadratic function, show that the maximum value of \(A(x)\) occurs when all the wire is used for the circle.
questions.answerLatex: Since \(2\pi r+3x=100\), \(r=\frac{100-3x}{2\pi}\). Hence \(A(x)=\pi r^2+\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2=\frac{(100-3x)^2}{4\pi}+\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2\). This quadratic has positive leading coefficient, so its maximum on the physical domain occurs at an endpoint. At \(x=0\), the area is \(\frac{2500}{\pi}\approx796\). At \(x=\frac{100}{3}\), the triangle-only area is about \(481\). Therefore the maximum occurs when all wire is used for the circle.
workingLatex: \(2\pi r+3x=100\Rightarrow r=\frac{100-3x}{2\pi}\).
\(A(x)=\pi\left(\frac{100-3x}{2\pi}\right)^2+\frac{\sqrt3}{4}x^2\).
The coefficient of \(x^2\) is positive, so the quadratic has a minimum turning point and the maximum on the domain is at an endpoint.
\(A(0)=\frac{2500}{\pi}\approx796\), while \(A(100/3)=\frac{\sqrt3}{4}\left(\frac{100}{3}\right)^2\approx481\).

questions.title: Exponential area and an inequality for e
questions.promptLatex: The shaded region is bounded by the graph \(y=\left(\frac12\right)^x\), the coordinate axes, and \(x=2\). (a) Use two applications of the trapezoidal rule to estimate the area of the shaded region. (b) Show that the exact area of the shaded region is \(\frac{3}{4\ln2}\). (c) Using your answers from parts (a) and (b), deduce \(e<2\sqrt2\).
questions.answerLatex: Using two trapezia with width \(1\), the estimate is \(\frac12\left(1+2\cdot\frac12+\frac14\right)=\frac98\). The exact area is \(\int_0^2 2^{-x}\,dx=\left[-\frac{2^{-x}}{\ln2}\right]_0^2=\frac{3}{4\ln2}\). Since the graph is concave up, the exact area is less than the trapezoidal estimate, so \(\frac{3}{4\ln2}<\frac98\). Hence \(2<3\ln2=\ln8\), so \(e^2<8\) and \(e<2\sqrt2\).
workingLatex: With two trapezia, \(h=1\), \(f(0)=1\), \(f(1)=\frac12\), and \(f(2)=\frac14\).
\(A\approx\frac{1}{2}\left(1+2\cdot\frac12+\frac14\right)=\frac98\).
\(\int_0^2\left(\frac12\right)^x dx=\int_0^2 2^{-x}\,dx=\left[-\frac{2^{-x}}{\ln2}\right]_0^2=\frac{3}{4\ln2}\).
The curve is concave up, so the trapezoidal estimate is greater than the exact area.
\(\frac{3}{4\ln2}<\frac98\Rightarrow24<36\ln2\Rightarrow2<3\ln2=\ln8\).
\(e^2<8\Rightarrow e<\sqrt8=2\sqrt2\).

questions.title: Circular segment area and arc length
questions.promptLatex: A farmer wants to use a straight fence to divide a circular paddock of radius \(10\) metres into two segments. The smaller segment is \(\frac14\) of the paddock and is shaded in the diagram. The fence subtends an angle of \(\theta\) radians at the centre of the circle. (a) Show that \(\theta=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\). (b) The graph of \(y=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\) is shown. Use the graph and the result in part (a) to estimate the arc length of the smaller segment to the nearest metre.
questions.answerLatex: The shaded segment is the sector area minus the triangle area. Since it is one quarter of the circular paddock, \(\frac12(10)^2\theta-\frac12(10)^2\sin\theta=\frac14\pi(10)^2\). Hence \(50(\theta-\sin\theta)=25\pi\), so \(\theta=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\). From the graph, the intersection of \(y=\theta\) and \(y=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\) is about \(\theta=2.3\) radians. The arc length is \(r\theta\approx10\times2.3=23\) metres.
workingLatex: Area of shaded segment \(=\) area of sector \(-\) area of triangle.
\(\frac12(10)^2\theta-\frac12(10)^2\sin\theta=\frac14\pi(10)^2\).
\(50\theta-50\sin\theta=25\pi\Rightarrow\theta-\sin\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}\Rightarrow\theta=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\).
Use the graph to estimate where \(y=	heta\) intersects \(y=\sin\theta+\frac{\pi}{2}\), giving \(\theta\approx2.3\).
Arc length \(=r\theta\approx10(2.3)=23\) metres.

questions.title: Mountain height and bearing
questions.promptLatex: The point \(T\) is the peak of a mountain and the point \(O\) is directly below the mountain's peak. The point \(Y\) is due east of \(O\) and the angle of elevation of \(T\) from \(Y\) is \(60^\circ\). The point \(F\) is \(4\) km south-west of \(Y\). The points \(O\), \(Y\) and \(F\) are on level ground. The angle of elevation of \(T\) from \(F\) is \(45^\circ\). (a) Let the height of the mountain be \(h\). Show that \(OY=\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\). (b) Hence, or otherwise, find the value of \(h\), correct to 2 decimal places. (c) Find the bearing of point \(O\) from point \(F\), correct to the nearest degree.
questions.answerLatex: From \(Y\), \(\tan60^\circ=\frac{h}{OY}\), so \(OY=\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\). From \(F\), the angle of elevation is \(45^\circ\), so \(OF=h\). In \(\triangle FOY\), \(FY=4\) and \(\angle OYF=45^\circ\). By the cosine rule, \(h^2=\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\right)^2+4^2-2\cdot\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\cdot4\cos45^\circ\). Solving gives \(h\approx3.03\) km. If \(\theta=\angle OFY\), then \(\frac{\sin\theta}{h/\sqrt3}=\frac{\sin45^\circ}{h}\), so \(\theta\approx24^\circ\). The bearing of \(O\) from \(F\) is \(45^\circ-24^\circ\approx021^\circ\).
workingLatex: \(\tan60^\circ=\frac{h}{OY}\Rightarrow OY=\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\).
Since \(\angle TFO=45^\circ\) and \(\angle TOF=90^\circ\), \(\triangle TFO\) is isosceles and \(OF=h\).
In \(\triangle FOY\), \(h^2=\left(\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\right)^2+4^2-2\cdot4\cdot\frac{h}{\sqrt3}\cos45^\circ\).
\(\frac{2}{3}h^2+\frac{8}{\sqrt6}h-16=0\), giving \(h\approx3.03\) km.
For \(\theta=\angle OFY\), \(\frac{\sin\theta}{h/\sqrt3}=\frac{\sin45^\circ}{h}\), so \(\theta\approx24^\circ\).
Bearing of \(O\) from \(F\) \(=45^\circ-24^\circ\approx021^\circ\).

questions.title: Translated parabola parameter
questions.promptLatex: The parabola with equation \(y=(x-1)(x-5)\) is translated both horizontally to the right and vertically up by \(k\) units, where \(k\) is positive. The translated parabola passes through \((6,11)\). Find \(k\).
questions.answerLatex: After translating right and up by \(k\), substitute \(x-k\) for \(x\) and \(y-k\) for \(y\): \(11-k=(6-k-1)(6-k-5)\). This gives \(k^2-5k-6=0\), so \(k=6\) because \(k\) is positive.

questions.title: Cosine equation parameter range
questions.promptLatex: The equation \(\cos(px)=\frac12\) has exactly two solutions for \(0\le x\le2\pi\), where \(p>0\). Find all possible values of \(p\).
questions.answerLatex: Let \(u=px\). Then \(0\le u\le2\pi p\), and \(\cos u=\frac12\) at \(u=\frac{\pi}{3},\frac{5\pi}{3},\frac{7\pi}{3},\ldots\). Exactly two solutions occur when \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\le2\pi p<\frac{7\pi}{3}\). Hence \(\frac56\le p<\frac76\).
workingLatex: \(u=px\) changes the interval to \(0\le u\le2\pi p\).
The first positive solutions of \(\cos u=\frac12\) are \(\frac{\pi}{3}\), \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\), and \(\frac{7\pi}{3}\).
For exactly two solutions, include \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\) but exclude \(\frac{7\pi}{3}\): \(\frac{5\pi}{3}\le2\pi p<\frac{7\pi}{3}\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet 1

Find the inverse function, ƒ - 1 (...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 11 (15 marks) Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet
(a) Find the inverse function, \(f^{-1}(x)\), of the function \(f(x)=1-\frac{1}{x-2}\).
(b) Solve \(\sin2\theta-\sin\theta=0\) for \(0\leq\theta\leq\pi\).
(c) Find \(\int\sin3x\cos x\,dx\).
(d) Sketch the graph of \(y=\frac13\cos^{-1}(2x)\).
(e) For what value of \(m\) is the vector \(\begin{pmatrix}1\\m\end{pmatrix}\) parallel to the vector \(\begin{pmatrix}2\\6\end{pmatrix}\)?
(f) The roots of \(2x^3+6x^2+x-1=0\) are \(\alpha\), \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\). What is the value of \(\frac{1}{\alpha\beta}+\frac{1}{\alpha\gamma}+\frac{1}{\beta\gamma}\)?
(g) Evaluate \(\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3}\cos^2(3x)\,dx\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) \(f^{-1}(x)=2-\frac{1}{x-1}\).
(b) \(\sin\theta(2\cos\theta-1)=0\), so \(\theta=0,\frac{\pi}{3},\pi\).
(c) \(\int\sin3x\cos x\,dx=-\frac18\cos4x-\frac14\cos2x+C\).
(d) Correct graph with domain \([-\frac12,\frac12]\) and range \([0,\frac{\pi}{3}]\).
(e) \(m=3\).
(f) \(\frac{1}{\alpha\beta}+\frac{1}{\alpha\gamma}+\frac{1}{\beta\gamma}=\frac{\alpha+\beta+\gamma}{\alpha\beta\gamma}=-6\).
(g) \(\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/3}\cos^2(3x)\,dx=\frac{\pi}{12}\).

The radius, r cm, and angle, q radian...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 12 (14 marks) Use the Question 12 Writing Booklet
(a) The radius, \(r\) cm, and angle, \(\theta\) radians, of a sector vary in such a way that its area remains a constant \(10\ \mathrm{cm^2}\). The angle \(\theta\) is increasing at a constant rate of 2 radians per second. Find the rate at which the radius is changing when the radius is 4 cm.
(b) Consider the region bounded by the hyperbola \(y=\frac1x\), the \(y\)-axis and the lines \(y=1\) and \(y=a\) for \(a>1\). Find the volume of the solid of revolution formed when the region is rotated about the \(y\)-axis.
(c) Prove by mathematical induction that \(1\times(1!)+2\times(2!)+\cdots+n\times(n!)=(n+1)!-1\) for integers \(n\geq1\).
(d) Find the solution of \(\frac{dy}{dx}=\sqrt{(2-y)(2+y)}\), given that \(y=1\) when \(x=0\).
(e) (i) Express \(\sqrt3\sin x-\cos x\) in the form \(2\sin(x-\alpha)\), where \(0<\alpha<\frac{\pi}{2}\).
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, solve \(\sqrt3\sin x=\cos x+1\), where \(0\leq x\leq2\pi\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) Since \(\frac12r^2\theta=10\), \(\frac{dr}{dt}=-\frac{r^3}{20}\). When \(r=4\), \(\frac{dr}{dt}=-\frac{16}{5}\ \mathrm{cm\ s^{-1}}\).
(b) \(V=\pi\int_1^a\frac{1}{y^2}\,dy=\pi\left(1-\frac1a\right)\).
(c) The base case \(n=1\) is true. Assuming the result for \(n=k\), adding \((k+1)(k+1)!\) gives \((k+2)!-1\), so the result holds by induction.
(d) \(x=\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{2}\right)-\frac{\pi}{6}\), equivalently \(y=2\sin\left(x+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\).
(e) (i) \(\sqrt3\sin x-\cos x=2\sin\left(x-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\). (ii) \(x=\frac{\pi}{3}\) or \(x=\pi\).

Use the Question 13 Writing Booklet dy 5

It is given that = and y = - 4 w...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 13 (16 marks) Use the Question 13 Writing Booklet
(a) It is given that \(\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac5y\) and \(y=-4\) when \(x=0\). Find \(y\) as a function of \(x\).
(b) Eight guests are to be seated at a round table. If two of these guests refuse to sit next to each other, how many seating arrangements are possible?
(c) At time \(t\), a particle has position vector \(\mathbf{r}(t)=t\mathbf{i}+\frac{t^2}{9}\mathbf{j}\), velocity vector \(\mathbf{v}(t)\) and acceleration vector \(\mathbf{a}(t)\). Find the time when the angle between \(\mathbf{v}(t)\) and \(\mathbf{a}(t)\) is \(\frac{\pi}{4}\).
(d) A bag contains counters, some of which are green. One hundred trials of an experiment are run. In each trial, one counter is selected from the bag at random and its colour noted. The counter is returned to the bag after each trial. Let \(X\) be the random variable representing the number of times that a green counter is selected. Given that \(E(X)=20\) and \(P(X\geq k)=0.0668\), find the value of \(k\). You may use the standard normal approximation and the information on page 13.
(e) (i) The Pascal's triangle relation can be expressed as \(\binom{n}{r}=\binom{n-1}{r-1}+\binom{n-1}{r}\). Do NOT prove this. Show that \(\binom{m}{R}=\binom{m+1}{R+1}-\binom{m}{R+1}\).
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, prove that \(\binom{2000}{2000}+\binom{2001}{2000}+\binom{2002}{2000}+\cdots+\binom{2050}{2000}=\binom{2051}{2001}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) \(y=-\sqrt{10x+16}\).
(b) Unrestricted arrangements: \(7!\). Arrangements with the two guests together: \(6!\times2\). Hence the answer is \(3600\).
(c) \(\mathbf{v}(t)=\mathbf{i}+\frac{2t}{9}\mathbf{j}\) and \(\mathbf{a}(t)=\frac29\mathbf{j}\). Equating scalar-product expressions gives \(t=\frac92\).
(d) With \(X\sim\operatorname{Bin}(100,0.2)\), use \(Z=\frac{X-20}{4}\). Since \(P(X\geq k)=0.0668\), \(k=26\).
(e) (i) Rearranging Pascal's relation with \(r=R+1\) and \(n=m+1\) gives the result. (ii) Applying the identity telescopes the sum to \(\binom{2051}{2001}\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a)
Prove that the product of any seven d...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 14 (15 marks) Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a) Prove that the product of any seven distinct factors of 60 must be a multiple of 60.
(b) Points \(A\) and \(B\) lie vertically above the origin. Point \(A\) is higher than point \(B\) such that \(\frac{OA}{OB}=k\), where \(k>1\). A particle is projected horizontally from point \(A\) with velocity \(U\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\). After \(T\) seconds, another particle is projected horizontally from point \(B\) with velocity \(V\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\). The two particles land on the ground in the same place. Show that the ratio \(\frac{V}{U}\) depends only on \(k\).
(c) The hands of an analogue clock are \(OA\) and \(OB\), where \(A\) is \(\left(\sin\frac{\pi t}{360},\cos\frac{\pi t}{360}\right)\), \(B\) is \(\left(2\sin\frac{\pi t}{30},2\cos\frac{\pi t}{30}\right)\), \(O\) is the origin, and \(t\geq0\) is the number of minutes past midnight. Find the values of \(t\) when the hands are perpendicular for the first and second time after midnight. Give your answers to 3 decimal places.
(d) The function \(f(x)\) is defined by \(f(x)=\cos^{-1}(\sin x)\) in the domain \((0,\pi)\). Find \(f'(x)\) for those values of \(x\) where it is defined.
(e) It is given that \(\tan\alpha\), \(\tan\beta\) and \(\tan\gamma\) are the three real roots of the polynomial equation \(x^3+bx^2+cx-1+b+c=0\), where \(b\) and \(c\) are real numbers and \(c\neq1\). Find the smallest positive value of \(\alpha+\beta+\gamma\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) Pair the factors into six buckets whose products are 60; choosing seven distinct factors forces two from one bucket, so the product is a multiple of 60.
(b) Let \(OB=h\) and \(OA=kh\). If the first particle has flight time \(t\), then \(t^2=k(t-T)^2\), and \(Ut=V(t-T)\). Hence \(\frac{V}{U}=\sqrt{k}\).
(c) The perpendicular condition reduces to \(\cos\left(\frac{11\pi t}{360}\right)=0\). The first two times are \(t\approx16.364\) and \(t\approx49.091\) minutes.
(d) \(f'(x)=-1\) for \(0<x<\frac{\pi}{2}\), \(f'(x)=1\) for \(\frac{\pi}{2}<x<\pi\), and \(f'(x)\) is undefined at \(x=\frac{\pi}{2}\).
(e) Using the tangent addition formula and the root relations gives \(\tan(\alpha+\beta+\gamma)=-1\). The smallest positive value is \(\frac{3\pi}{4}\).

betterResponses: recognise that there were 6 factor pairs, each with a product of 60, and treat them as pigeonholes
explain that in choosing 7 factors, there would be at least one pair chosen
identify the prime factors of 60 as 2,2,3,5 and then use the pigeonhole principle to explain clearly why the prime factors of at least 7 factors of 60 were needed in this case.
improvementAreas: understanding that the choosing of some factors does not constitute a proof for the general case
succinctly presenting a mathematical argument. Mathematics Extension 1 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 9 of 10

questions.title: 1 Points A and B are ( - 3, 1) and (1, 4) respectively
questions.promptLatex:
1 Points \(A\) and \(B\) are \((-3,1)\) and \((1,4)\) respectively.
Which of the following is a vector equation of the line \(AB\) with parameter \(\lambda\)?
A. \(\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\4\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}3\\4\end{pmatrix}\)
B. \(\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}3\\4\end{pmatrix}+\lambda\begin{pmatrix}1\\4\end{pmatrix}\)

questions.title:
Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet
(a)
The location of the complex number z...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 11 (14 marks) Use the Question 11 Writing Booklet
(a) The location of the complex number \(z\) is shown on the diagram on page 1 of the Question 11 Writing Booklet. On the diagram provided in the writing booklet, indicate the locations of \(\overline{z}\) and \(i\overline{z}\).
(b) The complex numbers \(w\) and \(z\) are given by \(w=2e^{i\pi/6}\) and \(z=3e^{i\pi/6}\). Find the modulus and argument of \(wz\).
(c) The complex number \(z\) is given by \(x+iy\). Find, in Cartesian form: (i) \(z^2\); (ii) \(\frac1z\).
(d) (i) Force \(\mathbf{F}_1\) has magnitude 12 newtons in the direction of vector \(2\mathbf{i}-2\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}\). Show that \(\mathbf{F}_1=8\mathbf{i}-8\mathbf{j}+4\mathbf{k}\).
(ii) Force \(\mathbf{F}_1\) from part (i) and a second force, \(\mathbf{F}_2=-6\mathbf{i}+12\mathbf{j}+4\mathbf{k}\), both act upon a particle. Show that the resultant force acting on the particle is given by \(\mathbf{F}_3=2\mathbf{i}+4\mathbf{j}+8\mathbf{k}\).
(iii) Calculate \(\mathbf{F}_3\cdot\mathbf{d}\), where \(\mathbf{F}_3\) is the resultant force from part (ii) and \(\mathbf{d}=\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+2\mathbf{k}\).
(e) Prove by contradiction that \(\sqrt3+\sqrt5>\sqrt{11}\).
(f) Find \(\int\frac{5}{7-x^2-6x}\,dx\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) \(\overline{z}\) is reflected in the real axis, and \(i\overline{z}\) is obtained by rotating \(\overline{z}\) through \(\frac{\pi}{2}\).
(b) \(wz=6e^{i\pi/3}\), so the modulus is 6 and the argument is \(\frac{\pi}{3}\).
(c) (i) \(z^2=(x^2-y^2)+2xyi\). (ii) \(\frac1z=\frac{x}{x^2+y^2}-\frac{y}{x^2+y^2}i\).
(d) (i) \(|2\mathbf{i}-2\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}|=3\), so \(\mathbf{F}_1=4(2\mathbf{i}-2\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k})\). (ii) \(\mathbf{F}_3=\mathbf{F}_1+\mathbf{F}_2=2\mathbf{i}+4\mathbf{j}+8\mathbf{k}\). (iii) \(\mathbf{F}_3\cdot\mathbf{d}=22\).
(e) Assuming \(\sqrt3+\sqrt5\leq\sqrt{11}\) leads after squaring to \(60\leq9\), a contradiction.
(f) \(\int\frac{5}{7-x^2-6x}\,dx=5\sin^{-1}\left(\frac{x+3}{4}\right)+C\).

questions.title:
Use the Question 12 Writing Booklet p 2
(a)
Using integration by parts, evalu...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 12 (16 marks) Use the Question 12 Writing Booklet
(a) Using integration by parts, evaluate \(\int_0^{\pi/2}x\sin x\,dx\).
(b) Given the function \(y=xe^{2x}\), use mathematical induction to prove that \(\frac{d^ny}{dx^n}=\left(2^nx+n2^{n-1}\right)e^{2x}\) for all positive integers \(n\), where \(\frac{d^ny}{dx^n}\) is the \(n\)th derivative of \(y\) and \(\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{d^ny}{dx^n}\right)=\frac{d^{n+1}y}{dx^{n+1}}\).
(c) Sketch the region of the complex plane defined by \(|z+5-i|>|z-3+3i|\).
(d) Find \(\int\frac{x^2-2x+9}{(4-x)(x^2+1)}\,dx\).
(e) A particle of mass \(m\) kg moves along a horizontal line with an initial velocity of \(V_0\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\). The motion of the particle is resisted by a constant force of \(mk\) newtons and a variable force of \(mv^2\) newtons, where \(k\) is a positive constant and \(v\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\) is the velocity of the particle at \(t\) seconds. Show that the distance travelled when the particle is brought to rest is \(\frac12\ln\left(\frac{k+V_0^2}{k}\right)\) metres.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) \(\int_0^{\pi/2}x\sin x\,dx=1\).
(b) The base case \(n=1\) is true. Differentiating the assumed \(n=k\) form gives \(\frac{d^{k+1}y}{dx^{k+1}}=(2^{k+1}x+(k+1)2^k)e^{2x}\), completing induction.
(c) The locus is the half-plane closer to \(3-3i\) than to \(-5+i\).
(d) \(-\ln|4-x|+2\tan^{-1}x+C\).
(e) From \(v\frac{dv}{dx}=-(k+v^2)\), integrate from \(v=V_0\) to \(v=0\) to obtain \(x=\frac12\ln\left(\frac{k+V_0^2}{k}\right)\).

betterResponses: demonstrate their knowledge of integration by parts
determine which terms of the integrand to differentiate and integrate
evaluate the relevant trigonometric ratios.
improvementAreas: choosing the correct expressions of 𝑢 = 𝑥 and 𝑑𝑣 = sin 𝑥
substituting the limits of the integral carefully
evaluating the resulting trigonometric ratios.

questions.title:
Use the Question 13 Writing Booklet 4 e x
(a)
It is given that A = dx x - 1 2...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 13 (15 marks) Use the Question 13 Writing Booklet
(a) It is given that \(A=\int_2^4\frac{e^x}{x-1}\,dx\). Show that \(\int_{m-4}^{m-2}\frac{e^{-x}}{x-m+1}\,dx=kA\), where \(k\) and \(m\) are constants.
(b) Let \(\mathbf{c}=x\mathbf{i}+y\mathbf{j}+z\mathbf{k}\) be a unit vector that is perpendicular to both \(\mathbf{a}=2\mathbf{i}+4\mathbf{j}-3\mathbf{k}\) and \(\mathbf{b}=-4\mathbf{i}-5\mathbf{j}+3\mathbf{k}\). Find all possible vectors \(\mathbf{c}\).
(c) (i) For positive real numbers \(a\) and \(b\), prove that \(\frac{a+b}{2}\geq\sqrt{ab}\).
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, show that \(\frac{2n+1}{2n+2}<\sqrt{\frac{2n+1}{2n+3}}\) for any integer \(n\geq0\).
(d) Evaluate \(\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{u}{1+\sin u+\cos u}\,du\), by first using the substitution \(u=\frac{\pi}{2}-x\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) With \(u=m-x\), the second integral becomes \(-e^{-m}\int_2^4\frac{e^u}{u-1}\,du\), so \(k=-e^{-m}\).
(b) Solving \(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{c}=0\), \(\mathbf{b}\cdot\mathbf{c}=0\) and \(|\mathbf{c}|=1\) gives \(\mathbf{c}=\pm\left(\frac13\mathbf{i}-\frac23\mathbf{j}-\frac23\mathbf{k}\right)\).
(c) (i) From \((\sqrt{a}-\sqrt{b})^2\geq0\), \(a+b\geq2\sqrt{ab}\). (ii) Apply the result with \(a=2n+1\) and \(b=2n+3\).
(d) Using the given substitution and then \(t=\tan\frac{x}{2}\) gives \(\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{u}{1+\sin u+\cos u}\,du=\frac{\pi}{4}\ln2\).

betterResponses: work fluently with an integral 𝑑𝑥 that included a constant 𝑚
choose a suitable substitution 𝑢 = 𝑚 - 𝑥 to make the boundaries simpler in the second integral and put it in the form 𝑘𝐴
justify the existence of a constant 𝑘 by evaluation
keep track of which integral they were substituting into, depending upon the substitution method chosen, as this helped with recognising that they needed to obtain the 𝑘𝐴 as opposed to 1 𝑘.
improvementAreas: focusing on the ‘ show that’ element of the statement and using the laws of indices appropriately in this calculus context
clarifying which substitution was being used, especially when the response trialled several options
taking note of both the desired limits and the form of the new integral when making a decision about which substitution to use.

questions.title:
Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet p 2
(a)
Let I = cot 2 n q d q for integer...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 14 (15 marks) Use the Question 14 Writing Booklet
(a) Let \(I_n=\int_{\pi/4}^{\pi/2}\cot^{2n}\theta\,d\theta\) for integers \(n\geq0\).
(i) Show that \(I_n=\frac{1}{2n-1}-I_{n-1}\) for \(n>0\), given that \(\frac{d}{d\theta}\cot\theta=-\cosec^2\theta\).
(ii) Hence, or otherwise, calculate \(I_2\).
(b) The acceleration of a particle is given by \(\ddot{x}=32x(x^2+3)\), where \(x\) is the displacement of the particle from a fixed point \(O\) after \(t\) seconds, in metres. Initially the particle is at \(O\) and has a velocity of \(12\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\) in the negative direction. (i) Show that the velocity of the particle is given by \(v=-4(x^2+3)\). (ii) Find the time taken for the particle to travel 3 metres from the origin.
(c) Let \(w\) be a complex number such that \(1+w+w^2+\cdots+w^6=0\). (i) Show that \(w\) is a 7th root of unity. The complex number \(\alpha=w+w^2+w^4\) is a root of the equation \(x^2+bx+c=0\), where \(b\) and \(c\) are real and \(\alpha\) is not real. (ii) Find the other root of \(x^2+bx+c=0\) in terms of positive powers of \(w\). (iii) Find the numerical value of \(c\).
(d) Positive real numbers \(a\), \(b\), \(c\) and \(d\) are chosen such that \(\frac1a\), \(\frac1b\), \(\frac1c\) and \(\frac1d\) are consecutive terms in an arithmetic sequence with common difference \(k\), where \(k\in\mathbb{R}\) and \(k>0\). Show that \(b+c<a+d\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) (i) Writing \(\cot^{2n}\theta=\cot^{2n-2}\theta(\cosec^2\theta-1)\) gives \(I_n=\frac{1}{2n-1}-I_{n-1}\). (ii) \(I_2=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac23\).
(b) (i) Integrating \(v\frac{dv}{dx}=32x(x^2+3)\) and using \(v(0)=-12\) gives \(v=-4(x^2+3)\). (ii) The time is \(\frac{\pi}{12\sqrt3}\) seconds.
(c) (i) Multiplying the finite series by \(1-w\) gives \(w^7=1\). (ii) The other root is \(w^3+w^5+w^6\). (iii) \(c=2\).
(d) Using \(b=\frac{a}{1+ak}\), \(c=\frac{a}{1+2ak}\), and \(d=\frac{a}{1+3ak}\), compare \(a+d\) with \(b+c\) to obtain \(b+c<a+d\).

betterResponses: use 𝑐𝑜𝑡 2 𝜃 = 𝑐𝑜𝑠𝑒𝑐 2 𝜃 - 1
replace integral with 𝐼 𝑛-1.
improvementAreas: recognising that this question did not involve integration by parts.

questions.title:
Use the Question 15 Writing Booklet
(a)
The adjacent sides of a parallelogram...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 15 (15 marks) Use the Question 15 Writing Booklet
(a) The adjacent sides of a parallelogram are represented by the vectors \(\mathbf{a}=4\mathbf{i}+3\mathbf{j}-\mathbf{k}\) and \(\mathbf{b}=2\mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}+3\mathbf{k}\). Show that the area of the parallelogram is \(6\sqrt{10}\) square units.
(b) A particle moves in simple harmonic motion about the origin with amplitude \(A\), and it completes two cycles per second. When it is \(\frac14\) metres from the origin, its speed is half its maximum speed. Find the maximum positive acceleration of the particle during its motion.
(c) (i) Show that \(\frac{1+\cos\theta+i\sin\theta}{1-\cos\theta-i\sin\theta}=i\cot\frac{\theta}{2}\).
(ii) Use De Moivre's theorem to show that the sixth roots of \(-1\) are given by \(\cos\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{6}+i\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{6}\) for \(k=0,1,2,3,4,5\).
(iii) Hence, or otherwise, show the solutions to \(\left(\frac{z-6}{z+1}\right)^6=-1\) are \(z=i\cot\frac{\pi}{12}, i\cot\frac{3\pi}{12}, i\cot\frac{5\pi}{12}, i\cot\frac{7\pi}{12}, i\cot\frac{9\pi}{12}\), and \(i\cot\frac{11\pi}{12}\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) Using base \(|\mathbf{b}|=\sqrt{14}\) and perpendicular height \(\sqrt{\frac{180}{7}}\), the area is \(6\sqrt{10}\).
(b) The period is \(\frac12\), so angular speed is \(4\pi\). The amplitude is \(\frac{1}{2\sqrt3}\), giving maximum positive acceleration \(\frac{8\pi^2}{\sqrt3}\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-2}}\).
(c) (i) Rationalising the denominator and using double-angle identities gives \(i\cot\frac{\theta}{2}\). (ii) The sixth roots follow from \(6\theta=(2k+1)\pi\). (iii) Put \(\frac{z-6}{z+1}\) equal to each sixth root and use part (i).

betterResponses: recognise that the area of a parallelogram is given by 𝑏𝑎𝑠𝑒 × ℎ𝑒𝑖𝑔ℎ𝑡, then calculate the height using vector methods.
improvementAreas: applying understanding of properties of a parallelogram, for example, diagonals are not perpendicular
showing appropriate working for a 4-mark question
checking simple arithmetic carefully when working across scalar (dot) products and vectors.

questions.title:
Use the Question 16 Writing Booklet
(a)
Consider the equation z n cos n q + z...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 16 (15 marks) Use the Question 16 Writing Booklet
(a) Consider the equation \(z^n\cos(n\theta)+z^{n-1}\cos((n-1)\theta)+z^{n-2}\cos((n-2)\theta)+\cdots+z\cos\theta=1\), where \(z\in\mathbb{C}\), \(\theta\in\mathbb{R}\), and \(n\) is a positive integer. Using a proof by contradiction and the triangle inequality, or otherwise, prove that all the solutions to the equation lie outside the circle \(|z|=\frac12\) on the complex plane.
(b) A particle of mass 1 kg is projected from the origin with a speed of \(50\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-1}}\), at an angle of \(\theta\) below the horizontal into a resistive medium. The position of the particle \(t\) seconds after projection is \((x,y)\), and the velocity of the particle at that time is \(\mathbf{v}=\begin{pmatrix}\dot{x}\\\dot{y}\end{pmatrix}\). The resistive force, \(\mathbf{R}\), is proportional to the velocity of the particle, so that \(\mathbf{R}=-k\mathbf{v}\), where \(k\) is a positive constant. Taking the acceleration due to gravity to be \(10\ \mathrm{m\ s^{-2}}\), and the upwards vertical direction to be positive, the acceleration of the particle at time \(t\) is given by \(\mathbf{a}=\begin{pmatrix}-k\dot{x}\\-k\dot{y}-10\end{pmatrix}\). Do NOT prove this. Derive the Cartesian equation of the motion of the particle, given \(\sin\theta=\frac35\).
(c) Consider the point \(B\) with three-dimensional position vector \(\mathbf{b}\) and the line \(C:\mathbf{a}+\lambda\mathbf{d}\), where \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{d}\) are three-dimensional vectors, \(|\mathbf{d}|=1\) and \(\lambda\) is a parameter. Let \(f(\lambda)\) be the distance between a point on the line \(C\) and the point \(B\). (i) Find \(\lambda_0\), the value of \(\lambda\) that minimises \(f\), in terms of \(\mathbf{a}\), \(\mathbf{b}\) and \(\mathbf{d}\). (ii) Let \(P\) be the point with position vector \(\mathbf{a}+\lambda_0\mathbf{d}\). Show that \(PB\) is perpendicular to the direction of the line \(C\). (iii) Hence, or otherwise, find the shortest distance between the line \(C\) and the sphere of radius 1 unit, centred at the origin \(O\), in terms of \(\mathbf{d}\) and \(\mathbf{a}\). You may assume that if \(B\) is the point on the sphere closest to \(C\), then \(O\), \(B\) and \(P\) are a straight line.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) If \(|w|\leq\frac12\), then the triangle inequality gives \(1\leq |w|^n+|w|^{n-1}+\cdots+|w|<1\), a contradiction. Hence all solutions lie outside \(|z|=\frac12\).
(b) Horizontal motion gives \(x=\frac{40}{k}(1-e^{-kt})\). Vertical motion and \(\sin\theta=\frac35\) give \(y=\frac{10(3k-1)}{k^2}(e^{-kt}-1)-\frac{10t}{k}\). Eliminating \(t\) gives \(y=\frac{10(1-3k)x}{40k}+\frac{10}{k^2}\ln\left(\frac{40}{40-kx}\right)\).
(c) (i) \(\lambda_0=(\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{a})\cdot\mathbf{d}\). (ii) \(\overrightarrow{PB}\cdot\mathbf{d}=0\). (iii) The shortest distance is \(\sqrt{|\mathbf{a}|^2-(\mathbf{a}\cdot\mathbf{d})^2}-1\), or 0 if the line intersects the sphere.

betterResponses: recognise that the negation of all solutions lying outside the circle | 𝑧| = 1 2 is at least one solution on or inside the circle
take the modulus of the sum of the 𝑛 terms on the left-hand side of the equation and show that this is equal to 1
show that the modulus of the sum of the 𝑛 terms is less than or equal to the sum of the 𝑛 moduli
use modulus properties to rewrite the moduli in the form | 𝑧| 𝑘 |cos[𝑘θ]|
show that since cos 𝛼 <= 1 for all 𝛼, the sum of the 𝑛 moduli is less than or equal to a geometric series with 𝑛 terms, with the first term and common ratio both equal to | 𝑧|
suppose by contradiction that | 𝑧| <= 1 2
show that the sum of the geometric series is less than 1
show that there is a contradiction as the modulus of the left-hand side cannot be equal to 1 and less than 1
interpret the contradiction to mean that the assumption is incorrect and so all solutions must lie outside the circle | 𝑧| = 1 2. Mathematics Extension 2 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 13 of 15
improvementAreas: finding the correct negation of regions on the complex plane
recognising that the triangle inequality only applies to the sum of non-negative real numbers, so moduli and absolute values are required for any expression which is not a non-negative real number
recognising that the left-hand and right-hand sides of the triangle inequality can be equal, so use either <= or >= as appropriate, not < or >
recognising the need to use cos 𝛼 <= 1 rather than cos 𝛼 < 1
recognising the need to use | 𝑧| <= 1 2 rather than | 𝑧| = 1 2 or | 𝑧| < 1 2
understanding that cos[𝑛θ] = 1 2 Re(𝑧 𝑛 ) only when 𝑧 is defined as | 𝑧| = 1 with Arg(𝑧) = 𝜃.

(b) From the graph, identify the dependent variable.

(b) Average minutes per day exercising.
(c) Form: linear. Direction: negative.

questions.title: A scale of 1: 50 is used to draw a rectangular area on a 2 mm grid as shown
questions.promptLatex:
Question 26
(4 marks) A scale of 1:50 is used to draw a rectangular area on a 2 mm grid as shown. The actual rectangular area is to be tiled.
The tiles cost \(\$150\) per square metre and the tiler orders 15% extra tiles to allow for cutting and breakage. The tiler charges \(\$90\) per hour and will take 20 hours to complete the tiling.

questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
The drawing measures 10.4 cm by 9.6 cm. Using the scale, the actual dimensions are \(10.4\times50=520\text{ cm}=5.2\text{ m}\) and \(9.6\times50=480\text{ cm}=4.8\text{ m}\).
Area \(=5.2\times4.8=24.96\text{ m}^2\). Total tile area required \(=24.96+15\%\times24.96=28.704\text{ m}^2\).
Tile cost \(=150\times28.704=\$4305.60\). Tiler cost \(=90\times20=\$1800\). Total cost \(=\$6105.60\), so \(\$6106\) to the nearest dollar.

betterResponses: use the given scale/ratio to convert diagram lengths to real lengths
calculate the total cost components, that is, tile cost and labour
apply the 15% extra correctly by multiplying by 1.15 or adding 15% to the original area, to allow for cutting and breakage

increasing amounts by 15% correctly. Mathematics Standard 1 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 8 of 8

Question 27

The salvage values are based on the declining-balance method.
By what amount will the car's value depreciate during the 10th year?
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
\(\$44\ 000=\$55\ 000(1-r)\), so \(1-r=\frac{44\ 000}{55\ 000}=0.8\) and \(r=0.2=20\%\).
Value after 9 years \(=55\ 000(1-0.2)^9=\$7381.98\). Value of depreciation during the 10th year \(=7381.98\times0.2=\$1476.40\).

betterResponses: use the formula to find the salvage values at the end of the 9th and 10th years

improvementAreas: demonstrating understanding that depreciating means the value is decreasing as time progresses
demonstrating understanding that with declining balance, the depreciating amount is not a constant value but rather a rate/percentage

(a) Calculate the mass of a \(\$2\) coin in grams, correct to 1 decimal place.

questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) 500 \(\$2\) coins have a mass of 3.3 kg, so the mass of one \(\$2\) coin is \(\frac{3.3}{500}=0.0066\text{ kg}=6.6\text{ g}\).
(b) 200 notes have a mass of 157 g, so one note has mass \(\frac{157}{200}=0.785\text{ g}\). The mass of 500 notes is \(0.785\times500=392.5\text{ g}\), which is 393 g to the nearest gram.

betterResponses: convert units correctly between kilograms and grams

questions.title: A toy has a curved surface on the top which has been shaded as shown
questions.promptLatex:
Question 26
(6 marks) A toy has a curved surface on the top which has been shaded as shown. The toy has a uniform cross-section and a rectangular base.
(a) Use two applications of the trapezoidal rule to find an approximate area of the cross-section of the toy.
(b) The total surface area of the plastic toy is \(1300\text{ cm}^2\). What is the approximate area of the curved surface?
(c) The measurements shown on the diagram are given to the nearest millimetre. What is the percentage error of the measurement of 10.2 cm? Give your answer correct to 3 significant figures.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
(a) \(A\approx\frac{5.1}{2}(6+3.8)+\frac{5.1}{2}(3.8+0)\approx34.68\text{ cm}^2\).
(b) \(1300=(2\times34.68)+(10.2\times40)+(6\times40)+\text{curved surface}\). Therefore the curved surface area is \(1300-717.36=582.64\text{ cm}^2\).
(c) Absolute error \(=\frac12\times0.1\text{ cm}=0.05\text{ cm}\). Percentage error \(=\frac{0.05}{10.2}\times100\%=0.4901\ldots\%=0.490\%\), to 3 significant figures.

betterResponses: apply the trapezoidal rule correctly, identifying the height, that is, ℎ = 5.1, and appropriate parallel sides
use two applications of the trapezoidal rule where required
apply absolute and percentage error formulas accurately
convert between units, that is, mm ↔ cm, and round to the correct number of significant figures.
improvementAreas: applying and rounding to a specific number of significant figures consistently
demonstrating understanding of absolute and percentage error and calculating these correctly
converting units correctly and ensuring consistent use. Mathematics Standard 2 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 7 of 12

questions.title: Solid spheres are placed inside a square-based pyramid as shown
questions.promptLatex:
Question 32
(3 marks) Solid spheres are placed inside a square-based pyramid as shown.
The base of the pyramid has side lengths of 14 cm. The height of the pyramid is \(h\) cm. The radius of each sphere is 1.5 cm.
The amount of empty space remaining inside the pyramid after 30 spheres have been placed inside the pyramid is \(634\text{ cm}^3\).
What is the height, \(h\), of the pyramid? Give your answer correct to the nearest centimetre.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
Volume of 30 spheres \(=\frac43\pi(1.5)^3\times30=424.115\ldots\).
Volume of pyramid \(=424.115\ldots+634=1058.115\ldots\).
\(1058.115\ldots=\frac13(14\times14)h\), so \(h=\frac{1058.115\ldots\times3}{14\times14}\approx16.2\text{ cm}\).
Therefore \(h=16\text{ cm}\), to the nearest centimetre.

betterResponses: find the volume of a sphere and a pyramid
interpret that the remaining space needs to be found
multiply the volume of the sphere by 30 and then add the given volume to go on to find ℎ.
improvementAreas: knowing the formulas to find the volumes of a pyramid and a sphere

using given information, that is, the number of spheres placed inside the pyramid, to support calculations, rather than relying on the diagram to estimate. Mathematics Standard 2 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 9 of 12

questions.title: The table shows future value interest factors for an annuity of $1
questions.promptLatex:
Question 34
(3 marks) The table shows future value interest factors for an annuity of \($1\).
Lin invests a lump sum of \(\$21\ 000\) for 7 years at an interest rate of 6% per annum, compounding monthly.
Yemi wants to achieve the same future value as Lin by using an annuity. Yemi plans to deposit a fixed amount into an investment account at the end of each month for 7 years. The investment account pays 6% per annum, compounding monthly.
Using the table provided, determine how much Yemi needs to deposit each month.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
\(n=7\times12=84\) periods and \(r=\frac{6\%}{12}\).
Lin's future value is \(21\ 000\left(1+\frac{6\%}{12}\right)^{84}=\$31\ 927.76\).
Monthly deposit \(=\$31\ 927.76\div104.07393=\$306.78\).

betterResponses: calculate 𝑛 = 84, 𝑟 = 6 12 % = 0.005 and identify from the future value table, the interest factor of 104.07393
use the future value formula correctly: 𝐴 = 21000 (1 + 6 12 %) 84
calculate the monthly deposit correctly: 𝐴 ÷ 104.07393 = $306.78.
improvementAreas: converting the interest rate (𝑟) and number of periods (𝑛) to the compounding period
recognising when to use the future value formula and when to use the provided future value interest factors tables
understanding when to divide by the interest factor and when to multiply.

questions.title: The triangle PTA is shown
questions.promptLatex:
Question 35
(3 marks) The triangle \(PTA\) is shown. The length of \(PA\) is 75 m and the length of \(PT\) is 51 m.
The angle of depression from \(T\) to \(A\) is \(36^\circ\), and the angle \(PTA\) is obtuse.
Find the length of \(TA\). Give your answer correct to 2 decimal places.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
\(\angle PTA=\theta\). Using the sine rule, \(\frac{\sin\theta}{75}=\frac{\sin36^\circ}{51}\), so \(\theta=59.81\ldots^\circ\).
Since \(\angle PTA\) is obtuse, \(\angle PTA=180^\circ-60^\circ=120^\circ\).
\(\angle TPA=180^\circ-120^\circ-36^\circ=24^\circ\).
\(\frac{TA}{\sin24^\circ}=\frac{51}{\sin36^\circ}\), so \(TA=\frac{51\sin24^\circ}{\sin36^\circ}\approx35.29\text{ m}\).

betterResponses: identify the angle of depression on diagrams correctly and understand its relationship to the angle of elevation
recognise when non-right-angled trigonometry is required
apply sine and cosine rules accurately, showing clear substitution and working
demonstrate understanding of alternate and corresponding angles in parallel lines
recognise and handle the ambiguous case in the sine rule.
improvementAreas: placing and interpreting angles of depression on diagrams correctly
demonstrating understanding of when to use sine or cosine rules, and the information required for each
recognising ambiguous sine rule cases. Mathematics Standard 2 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 10 of 12

assets.label: Triangle PTA
assets.alt: Triangle PTA with PA equal to 75 metres, PT equal to 51 metres, a vertical height from T, and not-to-scale label.

questions.title: The graph shows the salvage value of a car over 5 years
questions.promptLatex:
Question 36
(4 marks) The graph shows the salvage value of a car over 5 years.
The salvage values are based on the declining-balance method.
By what amount will the car's value depreciate during the 10th year?
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
\(\$44\ 000=\$55\ 000(1-r)\), so \(1-r=\frac{44\ 000}{55\ 000}=0.8\) and \(r=0.2=20\%\).
Value after 9 years \(=55\ 000(1-0.2)^9=\$7381.98\).
Value of depreciation during the 10th year \(=7381.98\times0.2=\$1476.40\).

betterResponses: use the formula to find the salvage values at the end of the 9th and 10th years
calculate the amount the car depreciates in the 10th year
demonstrate understanding that the 10th year is not at the end of year number 10, but how much the car has dropped in value during that 10th year.
improvementAreas: demonstrating understanding that depreciating means the value is decreasing as time progresses
demonstrating understanding that with a declining balance, the depreciating amount is not a constant value but rather a rate/percentage
working out a rate using a graph.

assets.label: Car salvage value graph
assets.alt: Graph showing a car value declining from 55 thousand dollars at year 0 to about 18 thousand dollars at year 5.

questions.title: The diagram shows a park consisting of two equilateral triangles
questions.promptLatex:
Question 37
(4 marks) The diagram shows a park consisting of two equilateral triangles. The shaded triangle is a grassed section. All measurements on the diagram are in metres.
How long will it take to mow the grassed section if it takes 5 minutes to mow \(20\text{ m}^2\)? Give your answer to the nearest minute.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
Using the cosine rule with included angle \(60^\circ\), \(c^2=9^2+3^2-2\times9\times3\cos60^\circ\), so \(c\approx7.937\).
Area of the grassed section \(=\frac12\times7.937\times7.937\times\sin60^\circ=27.2798\ldots\text{ m}^2\).
Time \(=\frac{5}{20}\times27.2798\ldots=6.81\ldots\) minutes, which is approximately 7 minutes.

betterResponses: use the area formula for non-right-angled triangles
label the new information, then calculate on the diagram
recognise that the shaded grassed area = the area of the bigger equilateral triangle - 3 times the area of one of the small triangles
use ratios correctly to find the time taken to mow the area.
improvementAreas: knowing when to use the relevant trigonometric formulas to calculate area and sides of both right-angled and non-right-angled triangles
demonstrating understanding of geometric properties to find unknown angles within the triangles
using ratios as applied to the problem presented.

assets.label: Park with equilateral triangles
assets.alt: Diagram of two equilateral triangles with a shaded inner grassed triangle and side lengths 3 and 9 metres.

questions.title: A car’s fuel efficiency is 30 miles per US gallon
questions.promptLatex:
Question 38
(3 marks) A car's fuel efficiency is 30 miles per US gallon.
Use the conversion information shown.
Calculate the car's fuel efficiency in litres per 100 km, correct to 1 decimal place.
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
1 US gallon \(=3.8\text{ L}\) and 30 miles \(=30\times1.6=48\text{ km}\).
Fuel efficiency \(=\frac{3.8\text{ L}}{48\text{ km}}\times100=7.916\ldots\text{ L}/100\text{ km}\), so \(7.9\text{ L}/100\text{ km}\), correct to 1 decimal place.

betterResponses: convert between different units, including unfamiliar units
convert miles per gallon to 3.8 L/48 km
complete distance and volume conversion calculations without mixing them up.
improvementAreas: completing one conversion at a time, rather than attempting multiple at once
using words as a support strategy to connect to their calculations to establish next steps on further calculations and conversions. Mathematics Standard 2 - HSC marking feedback 2025 Page 11 of 12

assets.label: Fuel efficiency conversion information
assets.alt: Boxed conversion information stating one US gallon equals 3.8 litres and one mile equals 1.6 kilometres, both correct to 2 significant figures.

questions.title: After a dose of a medication, the amount of the medication remaining in a per...
questions.promptLatex:
Question 39
(3 marks) After a dose of a medication, the amount of the medication remaining in a person can be modelled by the equation \(y=ka^x\), where \(x\) is the number of hours after taking the dose, and \(y\) is the amount remaining in milligrams (mg).
The graph shows the amount of the medication remaining in a person after \(x\) hours. Two points are also shown on the graph.
Using the information provided, find the amount of medication that remains in a person when \(x=4\).
questions.answerLatex:
Official marking guide excerpt (source-reviewed):
At \(x=0\), \(y=15\), so \(k=15\).
When \(x=2\), \(y=9\). Hence \(9=15a^2\), so \(a^2=\frac{9}{15}=0.6\) and \(a=0.7746\ldots\).
When \(x=4\), \(y=15(0.7746\ldots)^4=5.4\text{ mg}\).

betterResponses: recognise an exponential equation and its key features on a graph
substitute values for (𝑥, 𝑦) and/or 𝑘 correctly to attempt to find the value of 𝑦 when 𝑥 = 4
apply key features of the exponential graph, for example, 𝑦-intercept, to determine the equation.
improvementAreas: distinguishing between different types of graphs and functions
demonstrating understanding of the link between the 𝑦-intercept and 𝑘.

assets.alt: Exponential decay graph of medication remaining in a person, with points at 15 mg when x equals 0 and 9 mg when x equals 2.
